topics.title: Process Flow Sheeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Flow Sheeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **Process Flow Sheeting (PFS)** is the systematic graphical and computational representation of a complete bioprocess plant, depicting the sequence of **unit operations** and **unit processes** interconnected by process streams, along with their associated **material balances**, **energy balances**, and **operating conditions**. It is a standardized engineering document used for the design, simulation, analysis, and communication of industrial bioprocesses (e.g., fermentation, downstream processing, and formulation).

In a bioprocess context, the **flow sheet** acts as the central engineering "blueprint" that translates a microbial or enzymatic transformation into an industrial-scale reality. It is governed by the principles of **mass conservation (Law of Conservation of Mass)** and **energy conservation (First Law of Thermodynamics)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] Real-World Analogy
> Think of a **Process Flow Sheet as a "Recipe Card on Steroids"** for a chef running an industrial bakery. A home recipe might just say "mix flour, water, yeast, then bake." A flow sheet is like an engineer's recipe card that also specifies:
> - **How much** flour enters per hour (mass flow rate, e.g., **kg/h**)
> - **At what temperature** the dough is fermented (e.g., **30 °C**)
> - **What pressure** the oven operates at (e.g., **1.2 bar**)
> - **How much CO₂** leaves the fermentor as exhaust (e.g., **464.4 kg/h**)
> - **Where** the heat exchangers, pumps, valves, and sensors are placed
>
> In short, it converts a *biological idea* into a *factory design* that any engineer, contractor, or regulator can read, build, and audit.

### 1.3 Why Process Flow Sheeting is Indispensable in Bioprocess Engineering

- **Plant Design & Sizing**: Determines the dimensions of fermentors, centrifuges, and columns.
- **Capital Cost Estimation**: Equipment sizing feeds directly into CAPEX calculations.
- **Operating Cost Estimation**: Utility consumption (steam, cooling water, electricity) is derived from stream enthalpies.
- **Process Simulation & Optimization**: Software like **ASPEN Plus**, **SuperPro Designer**, and **BioPro Designer** require a flow sheet as input.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Essential for **cGMP** documentation, **FDA**, and **Environmental Impact Assessments**.
- **Safety Analysis**: HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) studies are based on P&IDs derived from flow sheets.

> [!TIP] KTU High-Yield Point
> Every KTU question on this topic will test three things: (1) **what information a flow sheet carries**, (2) **how to construct one**, and (3) **how to perform a material balance on a flow sheet**. Memorize the difference between BFD, PFD, and P&ID.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Process Flow Diagrams

Process flow sheeting is not a single document — it is a **hierarchy** of three increasingly detailed diagrams. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

#### A. Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
- **Level**: Most simplified.
- **Contents**: Major process sections shown as labeled rectangles; only primary material flows are shown.
- **Use Case**: Feasibility studies, preliminary presentations, teaching.

#### B. Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
- **Level**: Intermediate detail.
- **Contents**: All major equipment, all process lines, stream numbers, mass/energy flow rates, temperatures, pressures, compositions.
- **Use Case**: Process design, material balance closure, simulation input.

#### C. Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)
- **Level**: Maximum engineering detail.
- **Contents**: Every pipe, every valve, every instrument, control loops, line numbers, pipe specifications, material of construction.
- **Use Case**: Construction, commissioning, maintenance, regulatory audit.

### 2.2 Standardized Symbols Used in Bioprocess Flow Sheets

| Equipment / Function | Symbolic Representation | Purpose in Bioprocess |
|---|---|---|
| **Fermentor / Bioreactor** | Cylindrical vessel with agitator shaft | Carries out the biochemical conversion |
| **Heat Exchanger** | Two concentric circles or shell-and-coil icon | Sterilization of media, temperature control |
| **Centrifuge** | Bowl-shaped icon with rotating arrow | Cell separation from broth |
| **Filter Press / Microfilter** | Perforated drum or membrane symbol | Solid-liquid separation, sterile filtration |
| **Distillation / Extraction Column** | Vertical column with trays or packing | Primary product recovery |
| **Pump** | Circle with directional arrow | Fluid transfer |
| **Storage Tank** | Horizontal or vertical cylinder | Raw material / product hold-up |
| **Compressor / Blower** | Triangle or impeller symbol | Aeration, CO₂ venting |
| **Control Valve** | Bow-tie symbol on line | Flow regulation |
| **Sensor / Transmitter** | Circle with letter code (T, P, F, L) | Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level |

> [!IMPORTANT] Convention
> - **Instrument circles** carry a letter inside: **T** = Temperature, **P** = Pressure, **F** = Flow, **L** = Level, **A** = Analyzer, **C** = Conductivity.
> - The first letter indicates the **measured variable**, succeeding letters indicate **function** (e.g., **TIC** = Temperature Indicating Controller).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] The following table consolidates **all** governing equations you must memorize for a KTU Module 1 question on flow sheeting. All variables are dimensionally consistent in **SI units** unless stated otherwise.

| # | Equation | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum F_{\text{in}} - \sum F_{\text{out}} = \frac{dM_{\text{system}}}{dt}$ | **General Mass Balance** (unsteady state) | kg/h or kg |
| 2 | $\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$ | **Steady-State Mass Balance** (most flowsheet calcs) | kg/h |
| 3 | $F_{\text{in}} \cdot x_{A,\text{in}} = F_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{A,\text{out}} + r_A \cdot V$ | **Component Balance** with reaction (extent $r_A V$ in kg/h of A consumed) | kg/h |
| 4 | $Y_{P/S} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | **Product Yield Coefficient** (mass product per mass substrate) | dimensionless (kg/kg) |
| 5 | $\eta = \frac{Y_{P/S,\text{actual}}}{Y_{P/S,\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | **Process Efficiency / Conversion** | % |
| 6 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | **Heat Duty** on a stream (sensible heat) | kW or kJ/h |
| 7 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | **Latent Heat Duty** (e.g., steam for sterilization) | kW |
| 8 | $R_{\text{overall}} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J}$ | **Membrane Flux** (Darcy's law) | L/m²·h |
| 9 | $D = \frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}$ | **Doubling / Dilution Rate** (in chemostat: $D = F/V$) | h⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\dot{n}_{CO_2} = Y_{CO_2/S} \cdot \dot{m}_{S,\text{consumed}}$ | **CO₂ Vent Rate** for aerated fermentation | kmol/h |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Bioprocess Industries

- In a **recombinant insulin plant**, the PFD pinpoints the location of every chromatography column, every UF/DF skid, and every sterile filter.
- In a **bioethanol distillery**, the flow sheet shows the beer column, rectification column, and molecular sieve dehydration train.
- In **penicillin manufacturing**, the flow sheet reveals that most of the operating cost is in the **downstream processing** (extraction, crystallization), not the fermentor — guiding engineers to optimize solvent recovery loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Algorithmic Procedure to Construct a Process Flow Sheet

> [!TIP] This is the **canonical 7-step methodology** KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

1. **Define the process objective** — e.g., "Produce 10,000 kg/day of bioethanol at 99.5 % purity."
2. **Establish the basis of design** — feed composition, throughput, conversion, yield, purity.
3. **Identify all input and output streams** — raw materials, products, by-products, waste.
4. **Select the sequence of unit operations** — e.g., milling → saccharification → fermentation → centrifugation → distillation → dehydration.
5. **Perform component and overall mass balances** on each unit.
6. **Perform energy balances** to size heat exchangers, reboilers, condensers.
7. **Annotate the diagram** with stream numbers, T, P, compositions, and utility duties.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Example: Bioethanol Fermentation Flow Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Worked Example — KTU Board Exam Standard
> **Problem Statement:**
> A continuous stirred-tank fermentor (CSTF) processes a glucose feed solution at a rate of **10,000 kg/h** with **5 wt % glucose**. The fermentor operates at a glucose conversion of **95 %**. The reaction stoichiometry is:
>
> $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2$$
>
> The effluent broth is sent to a **centrifuge** that recovers **98 %** of the ethanol in the liquid (cell-free) stream. The cell paste leaves as a separate stream (ethanol-free for simplicity).
>
> **Required:** Draw the flow sheet and perform a complete material balance. Compute the **ethanol production rate (kg/h)** and the **CO₂ vent rate (kg/h)**.

#### Step 1 — Molar Mass Audit

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{glucose}}\,(C_6H_{12}O_6) &= 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180\ \text{g/mol} \\
M_{\text{ethanol}}\,(C_2H_5OH) &= 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 46\ \text{g/mol} \\
M_{CO_2} &= 12 + 2(16) = 44\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* All three molecular weights are required because the stoichiometry is per mole, but the flow rates will be in mass units (kg/h). Always normalize to a common basis (molar or mass) **before** the next step.

#### Step 2 — Glucose Mass Flow In

Since the feed is **5 wt % glucose** at 10,000 kg/h total:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{glucose, in}} = 0.05 \times 10{,}000 = 500\ \text{kg/h}
$$

> *Logic:* Mass fraction × total mass = mass of the solute.

#### Step 3 — Glucose Consumed and Glucose Leaving

With a conversion of **95 %**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{glucose, consumed}} &= 0.95 \times 500 = 475\ \text{kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{glucose, out}} &= 500 - 475 = 25\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Conversion = (mass reacted / mass fed). The unconverted fraction leaves the fermentor dissolved in the broth.

#### Step 4 — Ethanol Produced (Stoichiometric Mass Yield)

For every **180 g** of glucose consumed, **92 g** of ethanol is formed (since 2 mol × 46 = 92 g).

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{ethanol, produced}} = 475 \times \frac{92}{180} = 475 \times 0.5111 = 242.78\ \text{kg/h}
$$

> *Logic:* Multiply by the stoichiometric mass ratio. This is the **theoretical yield** assuming the reaction goes to completion as written.

#### Step 5 — CO₂ Produced (Vent Stream)

For every **180 g** of glucose consumed, **88 g** of CO₂ is formed (2 mol × 44 g).

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,\text{vent}} = 475 \times \frac{88}{180} = 475 \times 0.4889 = 232.22\ \text{kg/h}
$$

> *Logic:* The CO₂ exits the fermentor as a **gas** and must be vented through a condenser (to recover entrained ethanol) and a CO₂ scrubber.

#### Step 6 — Ethanol Recovered After Centrifuge

The centrifuge captures **98 %** of the broth's ethanol in the clarified liquid stream:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{ethanol, recovered}} = 0.98 \times 242.78 = 237.93\ \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{ethanol, lost with cells}} = 0.02 \times 242.78 = 4.85\ \text{kg/h}
$$

> *Logic:* The 2 % loss is associated with the cell paste — this is a typical engineering assumption to simplify the mass balance; in reality, a small amount of interstitial liquid carries dissolved ethanol with the cells.

#### Step 7 — Overall Mass Balance Closure Check

**Inputs to the system:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in,total}} = 10{,}000\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Outputs from the system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{out,total}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{clarified liquid}} + \dot{m}_{\text{cell paste}} + \dot{m}_{CO_2} \\
\text{Clarified liquid} &\approx 10{,}000 - 500(\text{glucose fed}) + 242.78(\text{ethanol formed}) \approx 9{,}767.78\ \text{kg/h} \\
\text{Cell paste (approx)} &\approx 25(\text{glucose}) + 4.85(\text{ethanol}) + \text{cells} \approx \text{negligible for material balance closure} \\
\text{CO}_2 &= 232.22\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* In a properly drawn flow sheet, the input arrow mass = sum of all output arrow masses, within the resolution of the cell mass term (which is **bio-generated**, not from the feed glucose mass, since it is synthesized by the microbes from the substrate carbon). For exam purposes, we close the balance by acknowledging the cells as an additional bio-product stream.

### 3.3 Final Consolidated Result Table

| Stream | Mass Flow (kg/h) | Glucose (kg/h) | Ethanol (kg/h) | CO₂ (kg/h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed (S1) | 10,000 | 500 | 0 | 0 |
| Fermentor Outlet Broth (S2) | 9,975 | 25 | 242.78 | 0 |
| Clarified Liquid (S3) | 9,742.78 | 25 | 237.93 | 0 |
| Cell Paste (S4) | ~232.22 | 0 | 4.85 | 0 |
| Vent Gas (S5) | 232.22 | 0 | 0 | 232.22 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Ethanol-from-Glucose Bioprocess

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete PFD-level flow sheet** for the worked example, including the **recycle / vent / utility streams** that students frequently forget.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RAW["RAW MATERIAL SECTION"]
        A1[Glucose Syrup Storage Tank]
        A2[Water + Nutrient Mix Tank]
    end

    subgraph PREP["PREPARATION SECTION"]
        B1[Mixing Vessel]
        B2[Plate Heat Exchanger - Sterilization at 121 C]
    end

    subgraph REACT["REACTION SECTION"]
        C1[CSTF Fermentor - 30 C, pH 5.0]
        C2[CO2 Vent Scrubber]
        C3[Cooling Water Jacket]
    end

    subgraph DSP["DOWNSTREAM PROCESSING SECTION"]
        D1[Disc Stack Centrifuge]
        D2[Beer Column Distillation]
        D3[Rectification Column]
        D4[Molecular Sieve Dehydration]
    end

    subgraph PROD["PRODUCT SECTION"]
        E1[Ethanol 99.5 percent Storage]
        E2[Denaturant Addition]
    end

    subgraph UTIL["UTILITIES AND WASTE"]
        F1[Cell Paste to Biogas Reactor]
        F2[Spent Wash to Effluent Treatment]
        F3[Steam Supply 4 bar]
        F4[Chilled Water Return]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    F3 --> B2
    C3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> F1
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    D2 --> F2
    D3 --> F2
    C1 --> F4
```

> [!NOTE] Reading the Diagram
> - Each **rectangle** = a unit operation / equipment item.
> - Each **arrow** = a process stream (solid, liquid, or gas).
> - The **subgraphs** group equipment into process sections — this is exactly the convention used on a real PFD title block.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Generic Bioprocess Master Template

The following is a **generalized bioprocess flow sheet template** applicable to antibiotics, enzymes, vaccines, and recombinant proteins.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Inoculum Development - Shake Flask] --> S2[Seed Fermentor - 50 L]
    S2 --> S3[Production Fermentor - 50,000 L]
    S3 --> S4[Harvest Tank - Broth Hold]
    S4 --> S5[Primary Recovery - Centrifugation or Filtration]
    S5 --> S6[Cell Debris Disposal]
    S5 --> S7[Purification - Chromatography Train]
    S7 --> S8[Polishing - UF DF and Sterile Filter]
    S8 --> S9[Formulation and Fill Finish]
    S9 --> S10[Lyophilizer or Vial Storage]
    S3 --> S11[Off Gas Treatment - CO2 and VOC Scrubber]
    S7 --> S12[Buffer Preparation Skid]
    S8 --> S13[Water for Injection System]
```

> [!TIP] KTU Exam Strategy
> When asked to "draw a flow sheet for the production of X", always include: **(i)** raw material section, **(ii)** fermentation section, **(iii)** downstream processing section, and **(iv)** utilities/waste section. Marks are awarded for *every* section — partial flow sheets lose up to **4 of 7 marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Block Flow Diagram (BFD) and a Process Flow Diagram (PFD).**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Block Flow Diagram (BFD) | Process Flow Diagram (PFD) |
|---|---|---|
| Level of detail | Lowest — only major process blocks | Intermediate — every major equipment shown |
| Stream information | Only major material flows, no T, P, or composition | All streams numbered with T, P, flow rate, composition |
| Purpose | Feasibility, preliminary costing, teaching | Detailed design, material balance closure, simulation |
| Utility lines | Not shown | Steam, cooling water, refrigerants included |
| Instruments | Not shown | Major instruments and control loops shown |

> Marks allocation: 1 mark for stating the difference in **detail level**, 1 mark for **stream information**, 1 mark for **purpose/application**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six pieces of information that must be annotated on a process flow sheet for a fermentation plant.**

**Model Answer (any six):**
1. Stream identification numbers (e.g., S-101, S-202).
2. Mass (or molar) flow rate of each stream in **kg/h** (or **kmol/h**).
3. Temperature of each stream in **°C**.
4. Pressure of each stream in **bar (g)**.
5. Composition of each stream (mass fractions of key components).
6. Heat duties of heat exchangers in **kW**.
7. Equipment tag numbers and sizes (e.g., V-101, 50 m³).
8. Utility specifications (steam pressure, cooling water inlet T).

> Marks allocation: 0.5 marks per correct point, 6 × 0.5 = 3 marks.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow sheet, describe the industrial production of **bioethanol** from sugarcane molasses. List the major unit operations and the conditions maintained in the fermentor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fermentor receives **20,000 kg/h** of a **10 wt % glucose** feed. The conversion in the fermentor is **90 %** and the theoretical yield of ethanol is **0.511 kg/kg glucose consumed**. The downstream distillation column recovers **97 %** of the ethanol entering it. Calculate the **ethanol production rate (kg/h)** leaving the distillation column. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

The industrial production of bioethanol from sugarcane molasses involves the following **six major unit operations**:

1. **Dilution and Nutrient Addition** — Molasses is diluted with water to a sugar concentration of **15–20 %** (w/v), and nutrients (NH₄)₂SO₄, KH₂PO₄, and MgSO₄ are added in a mixing tank.
2. **pH Adjustment and Sterilization** — pH is adjusted to **4.5–5.0** using H₂SO₄, and the medium is sterilized at **121 °C for 15 minutes** in a continuous plate heat exchanger.
3. **Fermentation** — Conducted in a CSTF at **30 °C, pH 5.0**, with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* as the inoculum. Residence time ≈ **6–8 hours**. Anaerobic conditions maintained. Conversion ≈ **90–95 %**.
4. **Cell Separation** — The fermented "beer" is sent to a **centrifuge** to remove yeast cells.
5. **Distillation** — Two-column system: **beer column** removes dissolved solids and most water; **rectification column** concentrates ethanol to **95.6 % (azeotrope)**.
6. **Dehydration** — **Molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite)** break the azeotrope to give anhydrous ethanol at **99.5–99.9 %**.

> Mark split: 1 mark for each of the 6 unit operations named + 1 mark for fermentor conditions (30 °C, pH 5.0, anaerobic, 90 % conversion).

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given Data:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = 20{,}000\ \text{kg/h},\quad x_{\text{glucose}} = 0.10,\quad X = 0.90,\quad Y_{P/S} = 0.511\ \text{kg/kg},\quad \eta_{\text{dist}} = 0.97
$$

**Step 1 — Glucose Fed:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{glucose, fed}} = 0.10 \times 20{,}000 = 2{,}000\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Glucose Consumed:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{glucose, consumed}} = 0.90 \times 2{,}000 = 1{,}800\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Ethanol Produced in Fermentor:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{ethanol, fermentor}} = 1{,}800 \times 0.511 = 919.8\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Ethanol Leaving Distillation Column:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{ethanol, product}} = 919.8 \times 0.97 = 892.21\ \text{kg/h}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{ethanol, product}} \approx 892.2\ \text{kg/h}}$

> Mark split: 1 mark for glucose fed, 1 mark for glucose consumed, 2 marks for ethanol produced, 2 marks for ethanol leaving column, 1 mark for the boxed final answer with correct units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **standardized symbols** used in a process flow sheet to represent the following: **(i)** Fermentor, **(ii)** Heat exchanger, **(iii)** Centrifuge, **(iv)** Distillation column, **(v)** Control valve, **(vi)** Temperature indicator-controller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of a Process Flow Sheet** in **(i)** plant design, **(ii)** cost estimation, **(iii)** process simulation, and **(iv)** safety analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

| # | Equipment | Symbol Description |
|---|---|---|
| (i) | Fermentor | Vertical cylindrical vessel with an agitator shaft and impeller inside; may show a sparger at the bottom for aeration. |
| (ii) | Heat Exchanger | Two concentric circles with a dividing line (shell-and-tube) OR a rectangle with internal zigzag (plate type). |
| (iii) | Centrifuge | Bowl-shaped icon with a curved arrow indicating rotation, often with inlet and outlet nozzles on opposite sides. |
| (iv) | Distillation Column | Tall vertical rectangle with horizontal lines inside representing trays; reboiler at the base, condenser at the top. |
| (v) | Control Valve | Bow-tie symbol (two triangles meeting at a point) drawn on the process line, with an actuator box on top. |
| (vi) | Temperature Indicator-Controller (TIC) | A circle with the letters "TIC" inside, connected to the process line and the control valve. |

> Mark split: 1 mark for each correct symbol description × 6 = 6 marks, + 1 mark for overall legibility / neatness.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**(i) Role in Plant Design:**
A flow sheet is the **starting point** for all equipment sizing calculations. The mass and energy balances derived from the flow sheet fix the **duties** of pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, and the **volumes** of vessels and columns. Without a flow sheet, no equipment datasheet can be issued.

**(ii) Role in Cost Estimation:**
Equipment costs (using correlations like the **Six-Tenths Rule** or the **Lang Factor method**) are functions of equipment size, which in turn comes from the flow sheet. Total Capital Investment (TCI) and Total Operating Cost (TOC) cannot be estimated without a flow sheet.

**(iii) Role in Process Simulation:**
Software tools (**ASPEN Plus, SuperPro Designer, ChemCAD**) require a topological flow sheet — equipment + stream connections + thermodynamic models — as the *primary input*. Simulation outputs (utility consumption, recycle loop convergence) guide process optimization.

**(iv) Role in Safety Analysis:**
**HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) studies**, **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)**, and **what-if analyses** all require a P&ID derived from the process flow sheet. The P&ID reveals deviations (e.g., loss of cooling water, valve failure) and their consequences, forming the basis for **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** classification.

> Mark split: 1.75 marks per role × 4 = 7 marks (0.5 marks for stating the role, 1.25 marks for explaining with bioprocess-specific examples).

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers
> 1. **Forgetting utility streams** (steam, cooling water, chilled water) on the flow sheet → loses up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Not numbering the streams** (S-101, S-102, …) → loses **1 mark** for lack of professionalism.
> 3. **Forgetting the off-gas / vent line** in fermentations → loses **1–2 marks**, especially when the question specifies "complete flow sheet."
> 4. **Using the wrong stoichiometric ratio** in mass balance problems (using moles instead of grams, or vice versa) → loses **2–3 marks** for an incorrect intermediate step.
> 5. **Not writing units** (kg/h, °C, bar) on the flow sheet annotations → loses **1 mark** for each missing unit cluster.
> 6. **Confusing PFD with P&ID** in definition-type questions → loses **2 marks** outright.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process Flow Sheeting** is the **engineering blueprint** of a bioprocess plant — it is a hierarchy of three diagrams: **BFD → PFD → P&ID**.
- Every flow sheet must show: **equipment**, **streams (with T, P, flow, composition)**, **utility lines**, and **major instruments**.
- Standard symbols are mandatory: **fermentor (cylindrical + agitator)**, **heat exchanger (concentric circles)**, **centrifuge (rotating bowl)**, **control valve (bow-tie)**, **instrument (circle with letter code)**.
- The **steady-state mass balance** $\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$ is the workhorse equation for 90 % of KTU numerical problems.
- The **stoichiometric conversion** of glucose to ethanol: 1 mole glucose → 2 moles ethanol + 2 moles CO₂; the mass ratio is **92/180 = 0.5111 kg ethanol/kg glucose**.
- Always include **vent/off-gas** streams for CO₂ in aerobic/anaerobic fermentations.
- A "**complete flow sheet**" in KTU parlance means: **raw material + fermentation + downstream + product + utilities/waste** — all five sections.
- The **product yield** $Y_{P/S}$ and **conversion** $\eta$ are the two key parameters that link the flow sheet to the process economics.
- In mass balance problems, **always state units**, **always close the balance** (inputs = outputs), and **always show intermediate steps** — KTU examiners award method marks generously but award final-answer marks strictly.
- **P&ID ≠ PFD**: the P&ID is the *construction* document; the PFD is the *design* document. Mixing them up in definition questions is the single most common mark-killer in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outline of the Various Unit Operations Used in Upstream and Downstream Operations in a Bioprocess Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Outline of the Various Unit Operations Used in Upstream and Downstream Operations in a Bioprocess Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bioprocess Technology – The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioprocess Technology** is the integrated application of **biochemistry, microbiology, and engineering sciences** to develop, optimize, and scale-up processes that use living cells (microorganisms, plant cells, animal cells) or their components (enzymes) to manufacture commercially valuable products such as antibiotics, vaccines, enzymes, organic acids, amino acids, biofuels, and therapeutic proteins.

A **bioprocess industry** is any industrial facility (e.g., a fermentation plant or a recombinant protein manufacturing unit) where biological transformations are deliberately harnessed to convert raw renewable substrates into high-value products. The complete industrial workflow is conventionally partitioned into two functional halves — **Upstream Processing (USP)** and **Downstream Processing (DSP)** — bracketing a central **Bioreaction (Fermentation) Stage**.

### 1.1.1 Upstream Processing (USP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Upstream processing refers to all preparatory unit operations performed before the actual biological conversion step, including the preparation, sterilization, and supply of the medium and the air required for the fermentation process, and the development of a suitable inoculum.*

In essence, USP deals with the **"preparation phase"** — everything that is done *to get the biological factory ready* before production begins.

### 1.1.2 Downstream Processing (DSP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Downstream processing refers to the recovery, separation, purification, and formulation of the desired product from the fermentation broth or from the cell mass, generating a saleable end-product of the required specification.*

DSP deals with the **"harvesting and polishing phase"** — everything that is done *to isolate, clean, and package* the product into a marketable form.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Reader

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The "Tea Factory" Model**
> 
> Imagine you own a factory that produces **specialty tea** from fresh tea leaves using **enzymes (biological agents)** for oxidation and flavor development.
> 
> - **Upstream Processing (USP)** is the *kitchen preparation* — buying fresh leaves (raw materials), washing them, sorting, weighing (media preparation), boiling/sterilizing the water (sterilization), and culturing a small batch of good enzyme-producing microbes (inoculum preparation).
> - **Bioreaction (Fermentation)** is the *actual brewing* — putting the prepared leaves and enzymes in a controlled tank where the magic happens.
> - **Downstream Processing (DSP)** is the *packaging line* — filtering out the leaves (separation), concentrating the tea (evaporation), removing bitter compounds (purification), adding preservatives, and bottling the final product (formulation).
> 
> If the kitchen is dirty → the brew is contaminated (USP failure).
> If the bottling line is faulty → the product is impure or unstable (DSP failure).

A bioprocess is therefore a **three-act play**: Act 1 (USP – set the stage), Act 2 (Fermentation – the drama), and Act 3 (DSP – take the bow with a clean, packaged product).

---

## 1.3 Why Is USP and DSP Engineering Important in the Bioprocess Industry?

| S.No. | Industrial Reason | Direct Consequence |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Biological systems are **sterile-sensitive** | USP must guarantee aseptic conditions to prevent contamination |
| 2 | Product concentrations in broth are **typically very low (g/L to mg/L range)** | DSP must perform high-recovery purification economically |
| 3 | Products are often **heat-labile proteins** | DSP must use **mild, non-denaturing** techniques |
| 4 | Regulatory bodies (US-FDA, WHO-GMP) require **validated, reproducible processes** | Every unit operation must be **documented and qualified** |
| 5 | Cost of DSP can be **up to 60-80%** of the total manufacturing cost | Choice of unit operations directly impacts **process economics** |

---

## 1.4 Schematic Bird's-Eye View of a Bioprocess Industry

```
RAW MATERIALS  ──►  UPSTREAM  ──►  BIOREACTOR  ──►  DOWNSTREAM  ──►  PACKAGED PRODUCT
  (Substrates,    (Media prep,    (Fermentation,     (Recovery,         (Bulk API or
   Water, Air)     Sterilization,  Bioconversion)      Purification,      Finished
                   Inoculum)                          Formulation)        Dosage Form)
```

The **bioprocess industry** is therefore not just one reactor — it is an **integrated chain of unit operations**, where every operation contributes to **yield, purity, cost, and quality** of the final product.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Unit Operations Sheet

## 2.1 UPSTREAM PROCESSING (USP) — Unit Operations in Detail

Upstream processing consists of the following sequential unit operations. Each is described below with its engineering rationale.

### 2.1.1 Raw Material Selection and Procurement
- Substrates (carbon, nitrogen sources — e.g., molasses, corn steep liquor, glucose, soybean meal).
- Process water of **purified grade (PW)** / **water for injection (WFI)**.
- Minerals, vitamins, antifoam agents, precursors.
- **Engineering rationale:** Substrate cost dominates raw-material economics; consistent quality ensures reproducible fermentation.

### 2.1.2 Media Preparation
- Weighing, dissolving, and mixing of nutrients in a **formulation/preparation tank (mixer)**.
- pH adjustment using acids (HCl, H₂SO₄) or alkalis (NaOH, NH₄OH).
- **Key equipment:** Mixing tanks, agitators, in-line pH meters.
- **Why it matters:** Incorrect C:N:P ratio → poor biomass yield and by-product formation.

### 2.1.3 Media Sterilization
Sterilization eliminates microbial contaminants from the medium so that the production strain grows **axenically** (alone).

Two industrial methods are dominant:

| Method | Condition | Mechanism | Industrial Use |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **Batch Sterilization** | **121 °C, 15 psi, 15–30 min** | Moist-heat protein coagulation | Small-scale, batch plants |
| **Continuous Sterilization (HTST)** | **~140 °C, 30–120 s** (High-Temp Short-Time) | Rapid exposure, plate/coil heat exchangers | Large-scale, continuous plants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sterilization Kinetics:** Microbial death follows first-order kinetics:
> $$N = N_0 \, e^{-k_d t}$$
> where $N$ = viable spores at time $t$, $N_0$ = initial spore count, $k_d$ = specific death rate (min⁻¹). The **Del factor** $\nabla = k_d \cdot t$ quantifies the log reduction ($\log(N_0/N)$).

### 2.1.4 Air Sterilization (for Aerobic Fermentations)
- Air supplied to a bioreactor (1–2 vvm) must be **sterile** to avoid contaminating the broth.
- **Method:** Depth filtration through **fibrous / membrane filters** (e.g., 0.2 µm PTFE) or **cyclone separators** to remove aerosols, followed by cartridge filters.
- Compressed air is pre-heated and passed through a **sterile filter train**.

### 2.1.5 Inoculum (Seed) Preparation
- Gradual scale-up of the production strain from **lyophilized ampoule → agar slant → shake flask → seed fermenter → production fermenter**.
- **Volume ratio rule of thumb:** each stage is typically **5–10 %** of the next-stage volume.
- Ensures the production fermenter is inoculated with a **healthy, exponentially growing, genetically stable** population.

### 2.1.6 Bioreactor Preparation and Sterilization
- Clean-in-place (CIP) and steam-in-place (SIP) of the vessel.
- Validation of probes (pH, DO, temperature) before inoculation.

### 2.1.7 Process Control Hardware
- Sensors (pH electrode, dissolved oxygen probe, temperature RTD, foam probe).
- Controllers (PID loops) and actuators (pump for base/acid, motor for agitator, valve for air).
- **SCADA / DCS** for data logging and recipe management.

---

## 2.2 BIOREACTION (FERMENTATION) STAGE — The Central Operation

The bioreactor is the **heart of a bioprocess plant**. Industrial types include:
- **Stirred Tank Reactor (STR / CSTR)** — most common; *Cylindrical, with 2–4 Rushton impellers, baffles, sparger.*
- **Air-Lift / Loop Reactor** — used for shear-sensitive cells (e.g., *animal cell culture*).
- **Bubble Column Reactor** — simple, no mechanical agitator.
- **Packed-Bed / Fluidized-Bed Reactor** — for immobilized enzymes or cells.
- **Photobioreactor** — for algae (light-driven).

**Critical operating parameters** to be controlled:
- Temperature (typical 30–37 °C for mesophiles).
- pH (often 6.8–7.2).
- Dissolved Oxygen (DO ≥ 30 % air saturation for aerobic cultures).
- Agitator speed (rpm) and aeration rate (vvm).
- Foam control (mechanical + chemical antifoam).
- Substrate feed (for fed-batch).

---

## 2.3 DOWNSTREAM PROCESSING (DSP) — Unit Operations in Detail

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical DSP train follows the **"4S Rule"** — **Separation → Separation → Separation → Purification**, often summarized as: *Removal of insolubles → Isolation → Purification → Polishing/Formulation.*

### 2.3.1 Step 1: Cell (Biomass) Separation
Removes the **insoluble** fraction (cells, cell debris, unconverted solids) from the liquid broth.

| Technique | Principle | Best For |
|:---:|---|---|
| **Centrifugation** (disc-stack, tubular bowl) | Sedimentation under centrifugal force (g-forces 5000–50000 × g) | Robust cells, large volumes |
| **Microfiltration** (0.1–10 µm) | Size-based sieving on a membrane | Shear-sensitive cells, clarification |
| **Flocculation + Sedimentation** | Aggregation of cells via polyelectrolytes (e.g., chitosan, alum) | Pre-treatment before centrifugation |
| **Precipitation** (isoelectric, salting-out) | Reduces product solubility; typically for **proteins** | Initial concentration step |

### 2.3.2 Step 2: Cell Disruption (only if product is **intracellular**)
- **Mechanical:** High-pressure homogenizer, bead mill, French press, sonication.
- **Non-mechanical:** Enzymatic lysis (lysozyme), chemical permeabilization, osmotic shock.
- **Why it matters:** Release of intracellular products (e.g., *E. coli*-derived recombinant proteins, intracellular enzymes) requires energy-intensive disruption; balance yield vs. cost.

### 2.3.3 Step 3: Concentration
Reduces the working volume before purification (typically 10–50 fold).
- **Evaporation** (rotary vacuum, falling film, mechanical vapor recompression).
- **Membrane ultrafiltration (UF) / nanofiltration (NF)** — molecular weight cut-off based.
- **Reverse osmosis (RO)** for water removal (e.g., *spent broth water recovery*).
- **Precipitation** followed by re-dissolution.

### 2.3.4 Step 4: Purification (the most cost-intensive step)
Selective removal of contaminants while retaining product **activity and structure**.

| Technique | Selectivity Basis | Typical Application |
|:---:|---|---|
| **Ion-Exchange Chromatography (IEC)** | Net surface charge | Proteins, antibiotics, amino acids |
| **Hydrophobic Interaction Chromatography (HIC)** | Surface hydrophobicity | Antibody purification |
| **Affinity Chromatography (AC)** | Biospecific ligand binding | Tag-purified recombinant proteins (e.g., **Ni-NTA for 6×His tag**) |
| **Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC)** | Molecular size | Polishing, buffer exchange |
| **Reverse-Phase Chromatography (RPC)** | Hydrophobicity at high organic content | Peptides, small molecules |
| **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** | Partition coefficient (K_D) | Antibiotics (e.g., penicillin into butyl acetate) |
| **Crystallization** | Solubility vs. supersaturation | Penicillin G, citric acid, lysine |
| **Membrane-based Electro-dialysis** | Ionic mobility | Desalting, organic acid recovery |

### 2.3.5 Step 5: Formulation and Finishing
Converts the purified bulk into a **stable, market-ready form**.
- **Sterile filtration** (0.22 µm) of liquid injectables.
- **Lyophilization (freeze-drying)** for thermolabile biopharmaceuticals.
- **Spray drying** for enzymes and probiotics.
- **Encapsulation / micro-encapsulation** for controlled release.
- **Excipient addition** (stabilizers: trehalose, mannitol, HSA, polysorbate 80).

### 2.3.6 Step 6: Quality Control and Packaging
- QC tests: identity, potency, purity (HPLC, SDS-PAGE, ELISA), sterility, endotoxin (LAL test), moisture (Karl Fischer), particulate matter.
- Aseptic filling into vials, syringes, or bulk containers.
- Labelling, secondary packaging, cold-chain storage (2–8 °C for many biologics).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Quick Reference — Unit Operation Summary

| Stage | Unit Operation | Type | Key Engineering Parameter |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| USP | Media preparation | Physical | Mixing time, homogeneity |
| USP | Batch sterilization | Thermal | **121 °C, 15 min** |
| USP | Continuous sterilization | Thermal | **140 °C, 30 s** (HTST) |
| USP | Air sterilization | Filtration | **0.2 µm** filter |
| USP | Inoculum build-up | Biological | 5–10 % volume transfer ratio |
| Reaction | Stirred-tank bioreactor | Bio-reaction | pH, T, DO, agitation, aeration |
| DSP | Centrifugation | Mechanical | g-force, residence time |
| DSP | Microfiltration | Membrane | Transmembrane pressure (TMP) |
| DSP | Cell disruption | Mechanical / chemical | Pressure (homogenizer) |
| DSP | Chromatography (IEC, HIC, AC, SEC) | Adsorptive | Linear velocity, column HETP |
| DSP | Extraction | Two-phase | Partition coefficient K_D |
| DSP | Crystallization | Phase change | Supersaturation ratio |
| DSP | Lyophilization | Phase change (sublimation) | Shelf T, chamber P (mTorr) |
| Formulation | Sterile filtration | Membrane | 0.22 µm filter, bubble point test |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Linkage:**
> - **Pharmaceutical industry (e.g., insulin, vaccines):** Upstream uses *E. coli / Pichia pastoris* in stainless-steel STR; DSP uses multi-step chromatography culminating in lyophilized vials.
> - **Industrial enzymes (e.g., amylase, protease):** Mostly extracellular → DSP skips cell disruption, dominated by UF + spray drying.
> - **Antibiotics (e.g., penicillin, streptomycin):** Classical fermentation → solvent extraction → crystallization → formulation.
> - **Biofuels (e.g., ethanol):** Continuous fermentation → distillation → dehydration → denaturation.
> - **Citric acid / lactic acid:** Fungal / bacterial fermentation → Ca(OH)₂ precipitation → H₂SO₄ re-acidification → crystallization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Quantitative Matrix

Although this topic is largely descriptive, three quantitative relationships are **frequently asked in KTU examinations**. They are derived step-by-step below.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Sterilization Kinetics (USP)

### Objective
Derive the relationship between the number of viable spores remaining after a sterilization time and the initial spore load, to compute the **Del factor (sterilization criterion)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Statement of the rate of microbial death.**

The death of microbial spores in a constant-temperature, well-mixed batch sterilizer is described empirically as a **first-order process**:

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = k_d \cdot N$$

**Step 2 — Rearrangement of variables.**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \cdot dt$$

**Step 3 — Integration between initial condition $N_0$ at $t = 0$ and final $N$ at $t$:**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\left[ \ln N \right]_{N_0}^{N} = -k_d \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_d \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k_d \, t}$$

$$\boxed{\,N = N_0 \, e^{-k_d t}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Del factor (dimensionless sterilization criterion).**

The **Del factor** $\nabla$ is the log-reduction achieved:

$$\nabla = \ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = k_d \cdot t$$

**Step 6 — Converting to base-10 logarithm (often used in practice):**

$$\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{k_d \, t}{2.303}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A batch sterilizer is loaded with medium containing $N_0 = 10^{6}$ spores/mL. The specific death rate at 121 °C is $k_d = 1.0 \; \text{min}^{-1}$. The industry requires a 12-log reduction ($N \leq 10^{-6}$ spores/mL). Calculate the required sterilization time.

**Solution:**

$$\nabla_{required} = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{6}}{10^{-6}}\right) = \ln(10^{12}) = 12 \times 2.303 = 27.636$$

$$t = \frac{\nabla_{required}}{k_d} = \frac{27.636}{1.0} = 27.64 \; \text{min}$$

**Industrial remark:** Typical industrial batch sterilizers are sized for a sterilization time of **20–30 minutes** at 121 °C, corresponding to a 12–15 log reduction in mesophilic spores.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Yield of a Multi-Stage Downstream Process (DSP)

### Objective
Show that the **overall recovery yield** of a downstream process equals the **product of the individual step yields** (a multiplicative quantity). This is a classic KTU 14-mark problem statement.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Let $m_0$ = mass (or activity) of product in the fermentation broth at the start of DSP.**

**Step 2 — Let $Y_i$ be the fractional (decimal) yield of the $i$-th step.**

For example, $Y_1 = 0.90$ means 90 % of the incoming product is recovered after step 1.

**Step 3 — After step 1, the mass recovered is:**

$$m_1 = Y_1 \cdot m_0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the same to step 2:**

$$m_2 = Y_2 \cdot m_1 = Y_2 \cdot Y_1 \cdot m_0$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to $n$ steps:**

$$\boxed{\,m_n = m_0 \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\,}$$

The **overall fractional yield** is:

$$Y_{overall} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \cdot Y_2 \cdot Y_3 \cdots Y_n$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$Y_{overall} \; (\%) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i \times 100$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A fermentation broth contains 100 g of desired antibiotic. It is processed through the following DSP train:
> 1. Centrifugation: $Y_1 = 0.95$
> 2. Extraction: $Y_2 = 0.90$
> 3. Ion-exchange chromatography: $Y_3 = 0.92$
> 4. Crystallization: $Y_4 = 0.88$
> 5. Drying: $Y_5 = 0.97$
> 
> Calculate the **final mass of pure product** obtained.

**Solution — Step 1: Overall yield:**

$$Y_{overall} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.88 \times 0.97$$

Compute stepwise:

$$0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.8550$$

$$0.8550 \times 0.92 = 0.7866$$

$$0.7866 \times 0.88 = 0.6922$$

$$0.6922 \times 0.97 = 0.6714$$

$$Y_{overall} = 0.6714 \;\; \text{i.e.,} \; 67.14 \;\%$$

**Solution — Step 2: Final mass:**

$$m_{final} = m_0 \cdot Y_{overall} = 100 \times 0.6714 = 67.14 \; \text{g}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often *add* individual step yields instead of multiplying. Remember: yields are **multiplicative** in a process train. A 90 % step repeated 10 times gives $(0.9)^{10} = 0.349$, i.e., only ~35 % overall — drastically lower than the intuitive 90 %!

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Balance Around a Two-Phase Liquid–Liquid Extraction (DSP)

### Objective
For an antibiotic being extracted from an aqueous phase (volume $V_a$) into an organic phase (volume $V_o$), derive the **fraction of solute remaining in the aqueous phase** after a single extraction, given the **partition coefficient** $K_D$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of the partition coefficient:**

$$K_D = \frac{C_o}{C_a} = \frac{\text{concentration in organic phase}}{\text{concentration in aqueous phase}}$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on the solute.** Let $W$ = total mass of solute (g) initially in the aqueous phase.

After extraction, fraction $f$ remains in aqueous, fraction $(1 - f)$ goes to organic.

$$C_a = \frac{f \cdot W}{V_a} \qquad ; \qquad C_o = \frac{(1 - f) \cdot W}{V_o}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of $K_D$:**

$$K_D = \frac{C_o}{C_a} = \frac{(1 - f) \cdot W \,/\, V_o}{f \cdot W \,/\, V_a} = \frac{(1 - f)}{f} \cdot \frac{V_a}{V_o}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $f$:**

$$K_D \cdot f = (1 - f) \cdot \frac{V_a}{V_o}$$

$$K_D \cdot f + f \cdot \frac{V_a}{V_o} = \frac{V_a}{V_o}$$

$$f \left( K_D + \frac{V_a}{V_o} \right) = \frac{V_a}{V_o}$$

$$\boxed{\,f = \frac{V_a / V_o}{K_D + V_a / V_o} = \frac{1}{1 + K_D \cdot (V_o / V_a)}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** Higher $K_D$ (solute prefers organic) and higher $V_o/V_a$ ratio both *reduce* $f$, i.e., *improve* extraction efficiency.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Penicillin G is extracted from $V_a = 100$ mL of fermentation broth using $V_o = 50$ mL of butyl acetate. The partition coefficient is $K_D = 35$. Calculate the fraction of penicillin remaining in the aqueous phase after **one** extraction.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{V_o}{V_a} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5$$

$$f = \frac{1}{1 + K_D \cdot (V_o / V_a)} = \frac{1}{1 + 35 \times 0.5} = \frac{1}{1 + 17.5} = \frac{1}{18.5}$$

$$f = 0.0541 \;\; \text{i.e.,} \; 5.41 \;\%$$

So 94.59 % of penicillin partitions into the organic phase in a single extraction — a hallmark of an efficient solvent extraction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — USP vs DSP

| Criterion | Upstream Processing (USP) | Downstream Processing (DSP) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Primary aim** | Prepare a sterile, optimal environment for cells | Recover pure, stable, formulated product |
| **Type of operations** | Mostly physical + thermal (sterilization) | Physical, chemical, biological separations |
| **Dominant cost** | Substrates and utilities (steam, air) | Resins, membranes, solvents (often 60–80 % of cost) |
| **Sterility requirement** | Strict (aseptic) | Sterile only for injectables; non-sterile for industrial enzymes |
| **Scale** | Smaller, fed-batch | Larger continuous columns / centrifuges |
| **Heat sensitivity** | Sterilization tolerates heat | DSP must avoid heat (denaturation of proteins) |
| **Final output** | Healthy, active biomass in fermenter | Purified, formulated, packaged product |
| **Typical operations** | Media prep, sterilization, inoculum build-up | Centrifugation, chromatography, extraction, crystallization, drying |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following block-level flow diagrams map the **interactions between unit operations** in a typical industrial bioprocess plant. They are intended to give a clear engineering picture of material and information flow.

---

## 4.1 Master Flow — Bioprocess Industry at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Material Storage] --> A2[Weighing and Dispensing]
    A2 --> A3[Media Preparation Tank]
    A3 --> A4[Batch or Continuous Sterilizer]
    A4 --> A5[Bioreactor STR CSTR]
    A6[Inoculum Lab Shake Flask] --> A7[Seed Fermenter]
    A7 --> A5
    A8[Compressed Air Filter 0.2 um] --> A5
    A5 --> A9[Fermented Broth]
    A9 --> B1[Centrifugation or Microfiltration]
    B1 --> B2[Cell Disruption if Intracellular]
    B2 --> B3[Concentration UF Evaporation]
    B3 --> B4[Purification IEC HIC AC SEC]
    B4 --> B5[Polishing Crystallization Drying]
    B5 --> B6[Formulation Excipient Mixing]
    B6 --> B7[QC Release]
    B7 --> B8[Packaged Finished Product]
```

---

## 4.2 Detailed Upstream Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U0[Start of Upstream] --> U1[Water Purification PW WFI]
    U1 --> U2[Carbon Source Molasses Glucose]
    U1 --> U3[Nitrogen Source Soybean CSL]
    U1 --> U4[Minerals Vitamins Antifoam]
    U2 --> U5[Mixing and pH Adjustment Tank]
    U3 --> U5
    U4 --> U5
    U5 --> U6{Choose Sterilization Mode}
    U6 -->|Batch| U7[Batch Sterilizer 121 C 15 min]
    U6 -->|Continuous| U8[HTST Sterilizer 140 C 30 s]
    U7 --> U9[Sterile Media Hold Vessel]
    U8 --> U9
    U9 --> U10[Production Bioreactor]
    I0[Working Cell Bank Lyophilized Vial] --> I1[Agar Slant Activation]
    I1 --> I2[Shake Flask Culture]
    I2 --> I3[Seed Fermenter 1]
    I3 --> I4[Seed Fermenter 2]
    I4 --> U10
    AS0[Air Compressor] --> AS1[Pre Filter Cyclone]
    AS1 --> AS2[Heater]
    AS2 --> AS3[Sterile Filter 0.2 um]
    AS3 --> U10
    U10 --> Uend[End of Upstream Sterile Inoculated Bioreactor]
```

---

## 4.3 Detailed Downstream Process Flow — With Decision Branch for Product Location

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Start of DSP Fermented Broth In] --> D1{Product Location}
    D1 -->|Extracellular| D2[Cell Removal Centrifugation MF]
    D1 -->|Intracellular| D3[Cell Harvest]
    D3 --> D4[Cell Disruption Homogenizer Bead Mill]
    D4 --> D5[Removal of Cell Debris Centrifugation MF]
    D5 --> D6[Crude Extract]
    D2 --> D6
    D6 --> D7[Initial Concentration UF or Precipitation]
    D7 --> D8[Primary Purification IEC or Affinity]
    D8 --> D9[Intermediate Polishing HIC or SEC]
    D9 --> D10[Final Polishing Crystallization or Lyophilization]
    D10 --> D11[Formulation Excipient Addition]
    D11 --> D12[Sterile Filtration 0.22 um for Injectables]
    D12 --> D13[QC Tests Identity Purity Sterility Endotoxin]
    D13 --> D14{Pass QC}
    D14 -->|Yes| D15[Aseptic Filling and Lyophilization]
    D14 -->|No| D16[Reject or Reprocess]
    D15 --> D17[Labelling and Secondary Packaging]
    D17 --> D18[Finished Bioproduct Out]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — How USP, Bioreactor, and DSP Interact as a Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USP_Block
        u1[Raw Material Silos] --> u2[Media Kitchen]
        u2 --> u3[Sterilization Skid]
        u3 --> u4[Sterile Hold]
    end
    subgraph Bioreaction_Block
        b1[Inoculum Suite] --> b2[Production Bioreactor STR]
        u4 --> b2
        b2 --> b3[Fermented Broth Storage]
    end
    subgraph DSP_Block
        b3 --> d1[Recovery Skid Centrifuge MF]
        d1 --> d2[Purification Suite Chromatography]
        d2 --> d3[Formulation and Fill Finish]
    end
    subgraph Utilities_Block
        util1[Clean Steam] --> u3
        util1 --> b2
        util2[Process Water] --> u2
        util3[Compressed Sterile Air] --> b2
        util4[Chilled Water Glycol] --> d1
        util4 --> d2
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Sterilization and DSP Unit Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Decide Sterilization Method] --> S1{Plant Scale}
    S1 -->|Small and Batch| S2[Batch Sterilization 121 C 15 min]
    S1 -->|Large and Continuous| S3[HTST Continuous 140 C 30 s]
    D0[Decide DSP Train] --> D1{Product Location}
    D1 -->|Extracellular| D2[Skip Disruption]
    D1 -->|Intracellular| D3[Add Disruption Step]
    D2 --> D4{Purity Target}
    D3 --> D4
    D4 -->|Therapeutic| D5[Multi step Chromatography plus Lyophilization]
    D4 -->|Industrial Enzyme| D6[UF plus Spray Drying]
    D4 -->|Antibiotic| D7[Extraction plus Crystallization]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General):**
> - Do **not** confuse USP and DSP units — these are different stages; mixing them up is the single biggest mistake.
> - Always present answers in a **sequential, numbered, or tabular form** — KTU examiners reward structured presentation.
> - When asked to "outline" unit operations, **list each with one-line engineering purpose**; do not write essays.
> - For derivation-type questions, show **every algebraic step**; partial marks are awarded per step.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. Define Upstream Processing and list any four unit operations involved in it.**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Upstream processing (USP) refers to all preparatory unit operations performed before the actual biological conversion (fermentation) step. It ensures that a sterile, nutritionally adequate medium is delivered to the bioreactor along with a healthy, actively growing inoculum, under controlled environmental conditions.
>
> **Four unit operations of USP:**
> 1. **Media preparation** — weighing, dissolving, and pH adjustment of nutrients.
> 2. **Media sterilization** — batch (121 °C, 15 min) or continuous (HTST 140 °C, 30 s).
> 3. **Air sterilization** — depth filtration of compressed air through 0.2 µm filters.
> 4. **Inoculum (seed) preparation** — graduated scale-up from a lyophilized vial to a production-ready seed fermenter.
>
> *(Additional valid operations: water purification, raw-material QC, CIP/SIP of bioreactor, process control hardware setup.)* **[3 marks]**

---

### **Q2. What is Downstream Processing? Differentiate between intracellular and extracellular product recovery.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Downstream processing (DSP) refers to the recovery, separation, purification, and formulation of the desired product from the fermented broth or cell mass, yielding a saleable end-product meeting specified quality attributes.
>
> **Difference table:**
>
> | Aspect | Intracellular Product | Extracellular Product |
> |:---|:---|:---|
> | Location of product | Inside the cell | In the surrounding broth |
> | Cell disruption step | **Required** (homogenizer, bead mill) | **Not required** |
> | Typical example | Recombinant insulin in *E. coli* | Penicillin from *Penicillium chrysogenum* |
> | DSP complexity | Higher (more unit operations) | Lower (skip disruption) |
> | Initial separation | Harvest cells, then break them | First remove cells, then concentrate broth | **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks). Students must answer **either (a) OR (b)** from each Module's choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the various unit operations involved in upstream processing of an industrial bioprocess. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Introduction to USP** (1 mark)
   - Definition, objective, place in the bioprocess train.
2. **Raw material handling and water system** (1 mark)
   - Substrate procurement, PW/WFI generation, storage.
3. **Media preparation** (1 mark)
   - Mixing, pH adjustment, antifoam addition.
4. **Media sterilization** (2 marks)
   - Batch vs. continuous (HTST); conditions, advantages.
   - First-order death kinetics: $N = N_0 e^{-k_d t}$; Del factor.
5. **Air sterilization and inoculum development** (1 mark)
   - 0.2 µm filtration; graduated seed train (5–10 % volume transfer).
6. **Bioreactor preparation and process control** (1 mark)
   - CIP/SIP; SCADA; PID loops for pH, T, DO, foam.

**(b) What is downstream processing? Explain the major steps in the recovery and purification of an industrial enzyme produced by *Aspergillus niger*. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Definition of DSP** (1 mark)
2. **Solid–liquid separation** (1 mark)
   - Rotary vacuum filtration or centrifugation to remove fungal mycelium.
3. **Concentration** (1 mark)
   - Ultrafiltration (10 kDa MWCO) to reduce volume.
4. **Purification** (2 marks)
   - Ion-exchange chromatography (DEAE-cellulose) followed by size-exclusion chromatography.
5. **Formulation** (1 mark)
   - Addition of stabilizers (e.g., glycerol, BSA), spray drying into powder form.
6. **Quality control and packaging** (1 mark)
   - Activity assay, SDS-PAGE purity, moisture content (Karl Fischer), sterile packaging.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, outline the complete unit operations involved in upstream and downstream processing of a typical bioprocess industry producing an antibiotic. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Block diagram** (3 marks) — must include USP → Bioreactor → DSP blocks with sub-units labeled.
2. **USP units in antibiotic production** (1 mark) — media prep with corn-steep liquor, sterilization, inoculum of *Penicillium chrysogenum*.
3. **Bioreaction stage** (1 mark) — fed-batch STR with pH 6.5, T 25 °C, glucose feeding.
4. **DSP units in antibiotic production** (2 marks):
   - Filtration → solvent extraction (butyl acetate) → back-extraction → activated carbon decolorization → crystallization as potassium salt → drying → packaging.

**(b) The following yields are reported in a 5-step downstream process train: 95 %, 90 %, 92 %, 88 %, 97 %. If the fermentation broth contains 100 g of the desired product, calculate the final mass recovered. Comment on the implication of using more purification steps. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Write the formula for overall yield:**

$$Y_{overall} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

**Step 2: Convert each percentage to a fraction and multiply:**

$$Y_{overall} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.88 \times 0.97$$

$$= 0.855 \times 0.92 \times 0.88 \times 0.97$$

$$= 0.7866 \times 0.88 \times 0.97$$

$$= 0.6922 \times 0.97$$

$$= 0.6714 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 67.14 \;\%$$

**Step 3: Final mass recovered:**

$$m_{final} = 100 \times 0.6714 = 67.14 \; \text{g}$$

**Step 4: Comment on additional purification steps (2 marks):**

Adding more purification steps increases **purity** and removes trace impurities, contaminants, endotoxins, and product variants — which is critical for **therapeutic injectables**. However, each additional step incurs a **multiplicative yield loss** and adds **equipment, resin, labor, and time cost**. Hence, a balance must be struck between **purity** (regulatory requirement) and **overall yield** (process economics). For industrial enzymes and bulk antibiotics, fewer steps are preferred. For injectable biologics, additional polishing chromatography (e.g., SEC, HIC) is mandatory despite the yield penalty.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall (Q-B part b):** Writing a percentage in place of a fraction (e.g., writing "0.95 %" instead of "0.95") gives a final mass of just 0.67 g — a 100-fold error. Always check the units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — "Outline of Unit Operations in USP and DSP of a Bioprocess Industry"**

- **Bioprocess = USP + Bioreaction + DSP** — three-stage integrated industrial workflow.
- **USP** = preparation; **Bioreaction** = biological conversion; **DSP** = recovery and purification.
- **USP unit operations:** raw material QC → media preparation → pH adjustment → media sterilization (batch 121 °C/15 min or HTST 140 °C/30 s) → air sterilization (0.2 µm filter) → inoculum build-up (slants → shake flasks → seed fermenters) → bioreactor CIP/SIP → SCADA-based process control.
- **Sterilization death kinetics** follow first order: $N = N_0 e^{-k_d t}$; **Del factor** $\nabla = k_d t = \ln(N_0/N)$.
- **Bioreactor** is the heart; common types: STR, air-lift, bubble column, packed-bed, photobioreactor.
- **Critical fermentation parameters:** T, pH, DO, agitation (rpm), aeration (vvm), foam, substrate feed.
- **DSP = 4-step logic:** Cell removal → (Disruption if intracellular) → Concentration → Purification → Formulation → QC → Packaging.
- **Cell separation techniques:** centrifugation, microfiltration, flocculation.
- **Cell disruption techniques:** high-pressure homogenizer, bead mill, French press, sonication, lysozyme.
- **Concentration techniques:** evaporation, ultrafiltration, reverse osmosis, precipitation.
- **Purification techniques:** ion-exchange, hydrophobic interaction, affinity (e.g., Ni-NTA), size-exclusion chromatography; liquid–liquid extraction; crystallization.
- **Formulation & finishing:** sterile filtration (0.22 µm), lyophilization, spray drying, encapsulation.
- **Overall downstream yield** is the **product** of individual step yields — always **multiplicative, never additive**.
- **DSP can account for 60–80 %** of total manufacturing cost in a bioprocess plant — a key economic driver.
- **Regulatory compliance:** US-FDA, WHO-GMP, EU-EMA; validated processes and quality-by-design (QbD) frameworks.
- **Industry-specific DSP trains:**
   - Recombinant protein → multi-step chromatography + lyophilization.
   - Antibiotic → solvent extraction + crystallization.
   - Industrial enzyme → ultrafiltration + spray drying.
   - Bioethanol → distillation + molecular sieve dehydration.
- **Common KTU mistakes to avoid:**
   1. Mixing up USP and DSP unit operations.
   2. Adding instead of multiplying step yields.
   3. Forgetting to insert the cell-disruption step for intracellular products.
   4. Stating "121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min" incorrectly (e.g., 110 °C, 30 min).
   5. Confusing MWCO (molecular weight cut-off) with pore size of microfiltration.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "USP = 4S + 1":** Sterile, Substrate, Seed, Steam (or Sterilization), plus Scale-up control. **DSP = 4C:** Collect cells, Concentrate, Clean (purify), Containerize (formulate).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolation and Preservation of Industrially Important Microorganisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Isolation and Preservation of Industrially Important Microorganisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Microorganisms — The Workhorses of Bioprocess Industries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Microorganisms** are defined (KTU 2024 syllabus) as those microbial strains — bacteria, yeasts, moulds, actinomycetes, and recombinant/engineered cells — that are capable of producing commercially valuable products such as antibiotics, enzymes, organic acids, amino acids, vitamins, biofuels, biopolymers, and recombinant therapeutic proteins at industrial scale.

These are not ordinary environmental microbes. They are **elite, genetically stable, GRAS-classified (Generally Regarded As Safe)** strains that have been:
- Isolated from nature
- Screened for the desired property
- Improved via mutation or recombinant DNA technology
- Validated for consistent productivity in large fermenters

> [!NOTE]
> **GRAS (Generally Regarded As Safe):** A U.S. FDA designation indicating that a substance is considered safe based on a long history of safe use in food, or on rigorous scientific evaluation. Examples: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Aspergillus niger*, *Lactobacillus* spp.

---

## 1.2 Isolation — The Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isolation** is the process of separating a single species of microorganism from a mixed natural population (soil, water, food, etc.) and obtaining it in **pure culture** (a culture containing only one species/strain).

**Conceptual Analogy — "Finding a Needle in a Haystack" 🎯**
Imagine a beach with millions of grains of sand, and you need to find ONE specific grain. That is exactly what isolation does — except the "beach" is a gram of soil containing ~10⁹–10¹⁰ bacteria, and the "specific grain" is an organism like *Penicillium chrysogenum* (penicillin producer) hidden among them. We use **serial dilution + selective plating** to literally "spread the haystack thin enough" until single cells are separated and grow into individual, visible colonies.

---

## 1.3 Preservation — The Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preservation** (or **maintenance**) is the process of maintaining viable, genetically stable, and contamination-free microbial cultures over extended periods — from weeks (short-term) to years/decades (long-term) — without loss of their industrially important traits.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Microbial Seed Bank" 🌱**
Just as agricultural scientists store seeds in gene banks at low temperature and low humidity to retain genetic integrity for decades, microbiologists store microbial "seeds" (cells/spores) in **cryobanks, lyophilized vials, or frozen glycerol stocks** so that the same productive strain used today can be revived in the year 2080 with identical performance.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics You Must Memorize

| Metric | Standard Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage temp, refrigeration** | **4 °C** | Slows metabolism; short-term backup |
| **Storage temp, deep freezer** | **–80 °C** | Long-term cryostorage |
| **Storage temp, liquid N₂** | **–196 °C** | Ultra-low, decades-long storage |
| **Lyophilization primary drying** | **–30 to –40 °C** | Sublimation of frozen water |
| **Lyophilization secondary drying** | **20–30 °C** | Removal of bound water |
| **Colony-forming unit (CFU)** | viable cell count basis | Quantifies isolated colonies |
| **Generation time of bacteria** | ~20–30 min | Determines subculture intervals |
| **Soil microbial load** | **10⁹–10¹⁰ cells/g** | Source concentration reference |
| **Target dilution for isolation** | **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸** | Yields countable, well-separated colonies |
| **Pure culture criterion** | Single species, axenic | Mandatory for industrial use |

---

## 1.5 Two-Stage Workflow: Isolation → Preservation

```
Nature (mixed culture)
        │
        ▼
   [ISOLATION]  ──►  Pure culture
        │
        ▼
   [PRESERVATION] ──► Working stock + Master stock
```

This is the **funnel principle** of industrial microbiology: start broad (millions of candidates), progressively narrow to a single elite strain, then lock it in time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Isolation Pipeline — Five Sequential Stages

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the isolation process as a **5-step funnel**:

### Stage 1: Sample (Inoculum) Collection
- **Sources:** Soil (most common — rich in actinomycetes, spore-formers), water (rivers, ponds, marine), air, plant surfaces (phyllosphere), food, dairy products, hot springs (extremophiles), contaminated sites, etc.
- **Soil depth preference:** **5–15 cm below surface**, where microbial density is highest.
- **Storage:** Refrigerate at **4 °C** if not processed within 24 h.

### Stage 2: Enrichment Culture (Selective Growth)
- Sample inoculated into **liquid selective medium** that favors the target organism.
- Manipulates: **carbon source, nitrogen source, pH, aeration, temperature, inhibitors** (e.g., antifungal nystatin for bacteria; antibiotics for fungi).
- **Example:** To enrich penicillin-producing *Penicillium*, use lactose-containing medium at **25 °C** with high O₂.
- Sub-culturing 2–3 times amplifies the target population.

### Stage 3: Plating for Isolation (Separation)
Three standard plating techniques (details in §3):

| Technique | Principle | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Streak plate** (4-quadrant) | Mechanical dilution by loop | Aerobic bacteria, yeasts |
| **Pour plate** | Mixing sample in molten agar | Anaerobes, quantitative CFU |
| **Spread plate** | Spreading diluted sample on solid agar | Quantitative CFU, uniform surface growth |

### Stage 4: Colony Selection & Purification
- Pick well-isolated colonies (from "discrete" zones of the plate).
- Re-streak repeatedly (3–4 times) until **morphologically uniform** colonies are obtained.
- Verify by **microscopy (Gram stain), colony morphology, biochemical tests**.

### Stage 5: Screening (Primary & Secondary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Screening ≠ Isolation.** Isolation gives a *pure culture*; screening evaluates *which* pure culture has the desired industrial property.

- **Primary screening** (qualitative, high-throughput): Tests thousands of isolates using cheap, rapid plate assays. *Example: Antibiotic screening — plug-agar diffusion against test pathogens.*
- **Secondary screening** (quantitative, low-throughput): Tests tens to hundreds of "hits" with precise assays. *Example: HPLC quantification of penicillin titer in shake-flask fermentation.*

---

## 2.2 Preservation Methods — Hierarchical Classification

Preservation techniques are classified by **duration and mechanism**:

### A. Short-Term Preservation (Weeks to Months)
1. **Refrigeration (4 °C)**
   - Slope/agar stab cultures on suitable medium.
   - Mechanism: Reduced metabolic rate, slowed enzymatic activity.
   - Subculture interval: **2–8 weeks** depending on species.
2. **Subculturing (Periodic Transfer)**
   - Manual transfer onto fresh medium at fixed intervals.
   - Risk: **genetic drift, contamination, loss of sporulation**.

### B. Long-Term Preservation (Months to Decades)
3. **Deep Freezing (–80 °C, mechanical freezer)**
   - Cells suspended in **cryoprotectant** (15–25% glycerol or 5–10% DMSO).
   - Cryoprotectants prevent intracellular ice crystal damage.
4. **Cryopreservation in Liquid Nitrogen (–196 °C)**
   - Most reliable long-term method for recalcitrant strains.
   - Two options: **Vapour phase LN₂** (–150 °C) or **liquid phase** (–196 °C).
5. **Lyophilization (Freeze-Drying)** ⭐ Industry standard
   - **Freezing → Primary drying (sublimation) → Secondary drying (desorption) → Vacuum sealing.**
   - Products stable for **10–30+ years** at 4 °C in dark.
6. **Drying Methods**
   - **Silica gel preservation:** Cells dried on sterile silica gel crystals.
   - **Soil culture:** Spores dried in sterile soil.
   - **Paper disc / filter-paper method** (e.g., *Aspergillus* conidia).
7. **Storage in Distilled Water**
   - Used for many fungi; cells remain viable for years.
8. **Oil Overlay (Mineral Oil)**
   - Paraffin oil layered over slant cultures; blocks O₂, slows desiccation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cryoprotectants?** Without glycerol/DMSO, intracellular water forms **ice crystals** that mechanically rupture cell membranes and organelles, killing the cell. Cryoprotectants lower the freezing point and form an amorphous glassy state (**vitrification**) that preserves cellular architecture.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dilution factor (1 tube) | $D = \dfrac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{sample}} + V_{\text{diluent}}}$ | dimensionless | For 1 mL into 9 mL → **D = 0.1 (10⁻¹)** |
| Cumulative dilution (n tubes) | $D_{\text{total}} = D^n = 10^{-n}$ | dimensionless | n = number of serial transfers |
| **CFU per mL** | $\text{CFU/mL} = \dfrac{\text{Number of colonies}}{\text{Volume plated (mL)} \times D_{\text{total}}}$ | cells·mL⁻¹ | Volume plated typically **0.1 mL** |
| **Colony-forming units per gram** (soil) | $\text{CFU/g} = \dfrac{\text{Colonies} \times D_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{plated}} \times m_{\text{sample}}}$ | cells·g⁻¹ | For solid samples |
| Water activity for stability | $a_w < 0.6$ (target for dried cultures) | dimensionless | Below this, microbial metabolism ceases |
| Cryoprotectant (glycerol) | **15–25 % (v/v)** | % | Standard for bacterial/yeast freezing |
| Cryoprotectant (DMSO) | **5–10 % (v/v)** | % | Standard for mammalian/animal cells |
| Sublimation rate (lyophilization) | $m_s = \dfrac{(P_0 - P_s) \cdot A}{R_d}$ | kg·h⁻¹ | $P_0$ = chamber, $P_s$ = surface vapor, $R_d$ = resistance |
| Generation time | $g = \dfrac{t}{n}$ where $n = \dfrac{\log N - \log N_0}{\log 2}$ | h or min | t = time interval, N₀ = initial, N = final |
| Storage viability loss (Arrhenius) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Higher T ⇒ higher inactivation rate k |
| **Log reduction** | $\log R = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | log units | Used to express survival post-preservation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in tables is rendered using LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Master/Working Cell Banks (MCB/WCB) for recombinant insulin, vaccines, mAbs — all rely on cryopreservation/lyophilization.
- **Food industry:** Starter cultures for yogurt, cheese, bread — preserved as freeze-dried pellets (DVS — Direct Vat Set).
- **Enzyme industry:** Industrial enzyme strains stored in industrial gene banks; revival after 5–10 years with full productivity.
- **Bioprospecting / Pharma R&D:** Soil samples from exotic locations (marine sediments, Amazon soil, hot springs) are screened using this isolation pipeline to discover **novel antibiotics** (e.g., teixobactin discovery from *Eleftheria terrae*, 2015).
