<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Lang Factor. Mention its typical value for a bioprocess / pharmaceutical plant and explain why it is higher than for a solid processing plant.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Lang Factor ($f_L$)** is an empirical multiplier used to estimate the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** of a plant from the **Purchased Equipment Cost (PEC)** using the relation:

$$FCI = f_L \times PEC$$

The typical Lang factor for a **bioprocess/pharmaceutical plant is 4.78**, compared to ~3.10 for a solid processing plant.

**Reason for higher value (2 marks):** Bioprocess plants require:
- Sterile cleanrooms (ISO 5, 7, 8 classified areas)
- HEPA filtration and air-handling systems
- Pure water systems (WFI, PW)
- Steam sterilizers (autoclaves)
- Higher-grade stainless steel (SS 316L) for product-contact surfaces
- Validated process control and instrumentation
- Waste decontamination systems (effluent treatment)

All these add significantly to piping, instrumentation, electrical, and building costs, raising the multiplier well above that of a simple solid plant.

> **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark — definition; 1 mark — value; 1 mark — reason]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the 5 P's of GMP/cGMP and briefly explain any two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **5 P's of cGMP** are:
1. **People**
2. **Premises**
3. **Processes**
4. **Products**
5. **Procedures (Paperwork)**

**Brief explanation of two (any 2 of the following):**

- **People:** Every employee must be adequately trained, qualified, and periodically assessed. Training records must be documented and retrievable.

- **Premises:** Buildings must be designed to prevent contamination, with controlled air quality (HEPA filtration), segregated areas, proper cleaning, and pest control.

- **Processes:** All manufacturing processes must be validated (IQ → OQ → PQ) and controlled by written Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) to ensure batch-to-batch consistency.

- **Products:** Each batch must meet predefined specifications for identity, purity, potency, and stability, verified through QC testing.

- **Procedures (Paperwork):** Every activity must be documented — batch records, logbooks, deviation reports, change controls, and audit trails are mandatory.

> **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark — listing the 5 P's; 2 marks — explanation of any 2]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** A new enzyme manufacturing facility has the following major equipment with their purchased costs:

| Equipment | Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|:--|:--:|
| Fermenter (20 m³) | 35 |
| Centrifuge | 12 |
| Ultrafiltration unit | 18 |
| Spray dryer | 22 |
| Packaging line | 8 |

Calculate using Lang's method: **(i)** FCI, **(ii)** Working Capital (assume 15% of FCI), and **(iii)** Total Capital Investment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the different methods of cost estimation. Discuss in detail the Lang factor method and the cost indexing method, including the significance of CEPCI. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Total Purchased Equipment Cost (PEC).**

$$\begin{aligned}
PEC &= 35 + 12 + 18 + 22 + 8 \\
PEC &= ₹95\ Lakhs
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Lang Factor for a bioprocess plant.**

$f_L = 4.78$

**Step 3 — Calculate FCI.**

$$\begin{aligned}
FCI &= f_L \times PEC \\
FCI &= 4.78 \times 95 \\
FCI &= ₹454.10\ Lakhs
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Working Capital (WC = 15% of FCI).**

$$\begin{aligned}
WC &= 0.15 \times 454.10 \\
WC &= ₹68.115\ Lakhs
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate TCI.**

$$\begin{aligned}
TCI &= FCI + WC \\
TCI &= 454.10 + 68.115 \\
TCI &= ₹522.215\ Lakhs \\
TCI &\approx ₹5.22\ Crores
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) FCI = **₹454.10 Lakhs**
- (ii) WC = **₹68.12 Lakhs**
- (iii) TCI = **₹522.22 Lakhs (≈ ₹5.22 Crores)**

> **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark — PEC; 1 mark — Lang factor; 1.5 marks — FCI; 1 mark — WC; 1.5 marks — TCI; 1 mark — final summary]**

---

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Methods of Cost Estimation (in increasing order of accuracy):**

1. **Order-of-Magnitude Estimate (Ratio Method):** Accuracy ±30–50%. Uses cost ratios from similar past plants. Used at the very initial concept stage.

2. **Study Estimate (Factored Estimate):** Accuracy ±25–30%. Based on major equipment cost and Lang factors. Used in feasibility study.

3. **Preliminary Estimate:** Accuracy ±15–25%. Uses Lang factor and factored cost models. Used for budget approval.

4. **Definitive Estimate:** Accuracy ±5–10%. Based on detailed engineering drawings, equipment specifications, vendor quotes.

5. **Detailed Estimate:** Accuracy ±1–5%. Based on complete drawings, specifications, and contracts. Used for actual construction.

---

**Lang Factor Method (Detailed Explanation):**

This method, proposed by H.J. Lang, estimates the Fixed Capital Investment directly from the Purchased Equipment Cost (PEC) by using an empirical multiplier — the Lang factor ($f_L$):

$$FCI = f_L \times PEC$$

The Lang factor is **not universal**; it varies with the **type of process plant**:

| Type of Plant | Lang Factor |
|:--|:--:|
| Solid processing | 3.10 |
| Fluid processing | 4.74 |
| **Bioprocess / Pharmaceutical** | **4.78** |
| Mixed (average) | 3.63 |

**Why bioprocess plants have higher $f_L$:** The stringent requirements of GMP (cleanrooms, sterilizable piping, WFI systems, validated controls, contamination prevention) increase the cost of installation, piping, instrumentation, and buildings disproportionately.

**Advantages of Lang's method:** Simple, fast, requires minimal data.

**Disadvantages:** Inaccurate, doesn't account for site-specific conditions, gives only a single number (no breakdown of costs).

---

**Cost Indexing Method (Detailed Explanation):**

The cost of equipment and plant construction changes with time due to inflation, market conditions, and technology improvements. **Cost indices** convert a past cost to a present cost.

**Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)** is the most widely used industry index, published monthly in *Chemical Engineering* magazine. It includes:

- Equipment index
- Construction labour index
- Buildings index
- Engineering and supervision index

**Formula:**

$$C_{present} = C_{past} \times \left(\frac{I_{present}}{I_{past}}\right)$$

**Significance of CEPCI:**
- Enables comparison of equipment costs across years
- Essential for budget planning and feasibility studies
- Used in vendor negotiations and procurement
- Required for academic and industrial cost estimation

**Other indices include:** Marshall & Swift (M&S) index, Nelson-Farrar refinery index, Engineering News-Record (ENR) construction index.

> **[Marks Distribution: 2 marks — methods overview; 2.5 marks — Lang method with bioprocess relevance; 2.5 marks — CEPCI and cost indexing]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** A pharmaceutical company is investing in a new vaccine plant. The initial investment is **₹8 crores**. The expected annual revenue is **₹5 crores** and the annual operating cost is **₹3.2 crores**. The plant has a useful life of **8 years** with a salvage value of **₹0.5 crores**. The tax rate is **30%** and depreciation is charged using the straight-line method. Calculate: **(i)** Annual depreciation, **(ii)** Taxable income, **(iii)** Net annual profit after tax, **(iv)** Annual cash flow, **(v)** Payback period, and **(vi)** % Return on Investment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the principles and significance of cGMP. Explain the concept of validation (IQ, OQ, PQ) and its importance in a bioprocess plant. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Initial Investment ($I_0$) = ₹8 Crores
- Annual Revenue ($R$) = ₹5 Crores
- Annual Operating Cost (AOC) = ₹3.2 Crores
- Plant Life ($n$) = 8 years
- Salvage Value ($S_N$) = ₹0.5 Crores
- Tax Rate = 30%

---

**(i) Annual Depreciation (Straight-Line Method):**

$$D = \frac{C_i - S_N}{n}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{8 - 0.5}{8} \\
D &= \frac{7.5}{8} \\
D &= ₹0.9375\ Crores\ per\ year
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(ii) Taxable Income (TI):**

First, compute Gross Profit:

$$\begin{aligned}
GP &= R - AOC \\
GP &= 5 - 3.2 \\
GP &= ₹1.8\ Crores
\end{aligned}$$

Then subtract depreciation:

$$\begin{aligned}
TI &= GP - D \\
TI &= 1.8 - 0.9375 \\
TI &= ₹0.8625\ Crores
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(iii) Net Annual Profit After Tax:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NP &= TI \times (1 - tax\ rate) \\
NP &= 0.8625 \times (1 - 0.30) \\
NP &= 0.8625 \times 0.70 \\
NP &= ₹0.60375\ Crores \\
NP &\approx ₹60.375\ Lakhs
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(iv) Annual Cash Flow:**

Cash flow = Net Profit + Depreciation (depreciation is non-cash, so added back)

$$\begin{aligned}
CF &= NP + D \\
CF &= 0.60375 + 0.9375 \\
CF &= ₹1.54125\ Crores
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(v) Payback Period:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PBP &= \frac{TCI}{Annual\ CF} \\
PBP &= \frac{8}{1.54125} \\
PBP &= 5.19\ years
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(vi) % Return on Investment (on TCI):**

$$\begin{aligned}
ROI\ (\%) &= \frac{NP}{TCI} \times 100 \\
ROI\ (\%) &= \frac{0.60375}{8} \times 100 \\
ROI\ (\%) &= 7.55\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:--|:--:|
| (i) Annual Depreciation | ₹0.9375 Cr |
| (ii) Taxable Income | ₹0.8625 Cr |
| (iii) Net Profit After Tax | ₹0.6038 Cr |
| (iv) Annual Cash Flow | ₹1.5413 Cr |
| (v) Payback Period | 5.19 years |
| (vi) % ROI | 7.55% |

**Project Verdict:** ROI of 7.55% is **below** the bioprocess benchmark of 15%, and PBP > 5 years — the project may be **marginally acceptable** but improvements in cost structure are advisable.

> **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark — depreciation; 1 mark — taxable income; 1 mark — net profit; 1 mark — cash flow; 1.5 marks — PBP; 1.5 marks — ROI]**

---

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Principles of cGMP (Current Good Manufacturing Practice):**

cGMP is the contemporary regulatory framework enforced by the **US FDA (21 CFR 210/211)**, **EMA (EudraLex Vol 4)**, **WHO (TRS 986)**, and **India's CDSCO (Schedule T)**. It mandates that manufacturers of pharmaceuticals, biopharmaceuticals, vaccines, and biologicals must use **modern, validated, and well-documented systems** to ensure product quality, safety, efficacy, and purity.

**Key Principles:**

1. **Quality must be built into the product, not just tested into it** — by design, prevention, and process control.

2. **Documentation is paramount** — every action must be traceable through batch records, logbooks, and audit trails.

3. **Personnel must be qualified and trained** — competency must be demonstrated and re-certified periodically.

4. **Facilities must be qualified and controlled** — cleanrooms, air quality, water systems, and environmental monitoring must be validated.

5. **Equipment must be qualified and maintained** — IQ, OQ, PQ, calibration, and preventive maintenance are mandatory.

6. **Materials must be controlled** — raw materials, APIs, excipients, packaging components, and finished products must meet specifications.

7. **Processes must be validated** — manufacturing processes must consistently produce a product meeting predetermined specifications.

8. **Deviations and changes must be controlled** — through formal change control, deviation, and CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) systems.

9. **Complaints and recalls must be handled systematically.**

10. **Continuous improvement through Quality Risk Management (QRM) and Quality by Design (QbD).**

---

**Validation Concept — The IQ/OQ/PQ Framework:**

Validation is **documented evidence that a process, system, or equipment consistently performs as intended**. The classical V-model of validation involves three phases:

**1. Installation Qualification (IQ):**
- Verifies that the equipment/system has been delivered and installed correctly
- Confirms components match purchase specifications
- Verifies utilities (power, water, steam) are connected properly
- Documents calibration of instruments
- Confirms SOPs for operation are in place

**2. Operational Qualification (OQ):**
- Demonstrates that the equipment/system operates as intended across its design range
- Tests upper and lower operating limits
- Verifies alarm systems, interlocks, and safety features
- Confirms cleaning procedures work
- Documents challenge tests

**3. Performance Qualification (PQ):**
- Demonstrates that the equipment/system performs effectively in the **actual production environment** using production materials
- Uses multiple batches (typically 3 consecutive successful batches) to confirm reproducibility
- Confirms the system consistently delivers the desired product quality
- Establishes ongoing process control parameters

**Other types of validation in bioprocess plants:**
- **Process Validation (PV):** Validated fermentation, purification, and fill-finish processes.
- **Cleaning Validation:** Confirms no cross-contamination, no residue carry-over.
- **Analytical Method Validation:** ICH Q2(R1) — accuracy, precision, specificity, linearity, range, LOD, LOQ, robustness.
- **Computer System Validation (CSV):** Required for SCADA, DCS, LIMS, ERP systems.
- **Facility Validation:** HVAC, water systems (WFI, PW), cleanroom classification.

**Importance in Bioprocess Plant:**

- Ensures **patient safety** by preventing contaminated, impure, or sub-potent products
- Ensures **regulatory compliance** (FDA, EMA, WHO approvals)
- Reduces **batch failures** and **production downtime**
- Provides **legal documentation** in case of audits or litigations
- Enables **technology transfer** between sites
- Critical for **biologics** (vaccines, mAbs, gene therapy) where product variability is inherently high

> **[Marks Distribution: 3 marks — cGMP principles; 2 marks — IQ/OQ/PQ explanation; 2 marks — importance in bioprocess]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing TCI with FCI** — Many students use FCI in ROI/PBP formulas instead of TCI. *Always use TCI for ROI and PBP* since working capital is part of the investment.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to add back depreciation in Cash Flow** — Depreciation is non-cash, so Annual CF = Net Profit + Depreciation, NOT just Net Profit.
> 
> 3. **Using wrong Lang factor** — Use 4.78 for bioprocess/pharma, 4.74 for fluid processing, 3.10 for solid processing. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting tax impact on profitability** — KTU examiners frequently test post-tax ROI. Skipping the 30% tax deduction is a common error.
> 
> 5. **Confusing GMP and cGMP** — GMP is a general principle; cGMP emphasizes the *current* and *up-to-date* implementation. Just writing "GMP" without "current" loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Not labeling P&IDs / flow diagrams** — In cGMP questions, students often list "5 P's" but skip the **validation cycle (IQ → OQ → PQ)**, which carries 2–3 marks.
> 
> 7. **PBP unit error** — Always state PBP in **years**, not months.
> 
> 8. **Skipping the discount factor calculation in NPV** — In NPV problems, students often directly sum cash flows. Always show $(1+i)^{-t}$ computation for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

### **Capital Cost Estimation**
- **TCI = FCI + WC** (Total Capital Investment)
- **FCI = $f_L$ × PEC** (Lang method); $f_L$ = 4.78 for bioprocess
- WC ≈ 10–20% of FCI
- FCI has Direct Costs (equipment, piping, instrumentation, buildings) + Indirect Costs (engineering, construction, contingency)
- **Cost Index Formula:** $C_{now} = C_{past} \times (I_{now}/I_{past})$ — use **CEPCI**
- Order of accuracy: Order-of-Magnitude < Study < Preliminary < Definitive < Detailed

### **Operating Cost Estimation**
- **TOC = Direct Production + Fixed Charges + Plant Overhead + General Expenses**
- Direct Production includes raw materials, labor, utilities, maintenance, lab charges
- Fixed Charges include **depreciation, taxes, insurance, rent**
- Plant Overhead ≈ 50–70% of (Labor + Maintenance + Supervision)
- General Expenses = administration + distribution + R&D + finance
- **Cost per kg = TOC / Annual Production**

### **Profitability Analysis**
- **% ROI = (Net Profit / TCI) × 100**; Acceptable if > 15% for bioprocess
- **PBP = TCI / Annual Cash Flow**; Acceptable if < 5 years
- **Annual Cash Flow = Net Profit + Depreciation** (add back non-cash)
- **NPV = Σ[CFₜ / (1+i)ᵗ] − I₀**; Acceptable if NPV > 0
- **IRR** = discount rate at which NPV = 0; Acceptable if IRR > MARR (typically 10–15%)
- **Straight-line depreciation:** D = (Cᵢ − Sₙ) / n

### **GMP and cGMP**
- cGMP = **Current** Good Manufacturing Practice — must use modern, validated systems
- 5 P's: **People, Premises, Processes, Products, Procedures (Paperwork)**
- Key regulations: **21 CFR 210/211 (US FDA)**, **EudraLex Vol 4 (EU)**, **Schedule T (India)**, **WHO TRS 986**, **ICH Q7**
- Validation cycle: **IQ → OQ → PQ** (Installation → Operational → Performance)
- Other validations: Cleaning, Analytical (ICH Q2), Computer System (CSV), Facility
- **Quality must be built into the product, not just tested** — core cGMP philosophy
- Documentation = king — every action, deviation, change must be recorded

### **Memorize These Constants (High-Yield)**
- Lang Factor for **Bioprocess Plant = 4.78**
- Lang Factor for **Solid Processing = 3.10**
- Lang Factor for **Fluid Processing = 4.74**
- WC = **10–20% of FCI**
- Plant Overhead = **50–70% of (Labor + Maintenance + Supervision)**
- Bioprocess ROI benchmark = **> 15%**
- Bioprocess PBP benchmark = **< 5 years**
- Typical MARR for bioprocess = **10–15%**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utilities in a Bioprocess Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Utilities in a Bioprocess Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of a **bioprocess plant**, **utilities** refer to the auxiliary support services, energy carriers, and bulk resources that are *not part of the actual biological reaction or product formation step*, but are **absolutely essential** to sustain fermentation, downstream processing, cleaning, sterilization, and environmental control operations. They form the **engineering backbone** that enables reproducible, sterile, and GMP-compliant manufacturing of biological products such as vaccines, enzymes, antibiotics, monoclonal antibodies, and recombinant proteins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):**
> *"Utilities are the centralized support systems — water (of graded purity), steam (plant and clean), compressed/process air, cooling media, refrigeration, HVAC, electrical power, and waste-handling systems — that are generated, treated, conditioned, and distributed throughout a bioprocess plant to ensure that the core process equipment (fermenters, bioreactors, filtration units, chromatographs) operates under sterile, validated, and energy-efficient conditions."*

The major utilities are classified as:

| S. No. | Utility Class | Examples | End-Use in Bioprocess |
|--------|----------------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Water Systems** | Potable, Process, Purified (PW), Highly Purified (HPW), Water for Injection (WFI) | Media prep, buffer prep, CIP, rinsing, product formulation |
| 2 | **Steam Systems** | Plant (industrial) steam, Clean (pure) steam | Sterilization-in-Place (SIP), humidification |
| 3 | **Air/Gas Systems** | Compressed air, Process air, Oxygen, Nitrogen, CO₂ | Aeration, sparging, inert blanketing, pneumatic valves |
| 4 | **Cooling & Refrigeration** | Chilled water, glycol loops, brine | Fermenter cooling, exotherm removal |
| 5 | **Heating Systems** | Hot water, thermal fluid | Jacket heating, pre-heating |
| 6 | **HVAC** | Grade A/B/C/D air | Cleanrooms, aseptic zones |
| 7 | **Electrical Power** | UPS, DG sets, normal supply | Motors, instrumentation, PLCs |
| 8 | **Effluent Treatment** | ETP/STP | Wastewater neutralization, biodegradation |

> [!NOTE]
> A bioprocess plant is essentially a **microbial / cell culture factory**. Just like a city needs water, electricity, gas, sewage, and roads to function, a bioprocess plant needs these eight utility streams working in synchrony to run a single batch of fermentation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Body of a Bioprocess Plant"

Imagine a **bioprocess plant as a human body**:

- **Fermenter = Heart** → pumps and drives the process.
- **Piping & vessels = Veins/Arteries** → carry fluids.
- **Water (PW/WFI) = Blood Plasma** → the universal solvent, transports nutrients.
- **Steam = Fever/Heat** → kills microbes (sterilization).
- **Compressed Air = Lungs** → supplies oxygen for aerobic microbes.
- **Cooling water = Sweat/Body Coolant** → removes metabolic heat.
- **HVAC = Skin & Breathing** → maintains a clean, temperature-controlled atmosphere.
- **Electricity = Nervous System** → signals every motor, sensor, valve.
- **Effluent Treatment = Kidneys/Liver** → detoxifies waste.

> [!TIP]
> **If any one utility fails, the entire batch is lost.** For example, if chilled water fails during a high-cell-density *E. coli* fermentation, the temperature can rise by 1 °C per minute — plasmids degrade, inclusion bodies form incorrectly, and the entire ₹20-lakh batch is ruined. This is why utilities are called **"Critical Utilities"** and are validated under **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) — ICH Q9, WHO TRS 986, and US FDA Process Validation Guidance (2011)**.

## 1.3 Key Quality Metrics (Memorize for KTU Exams)

- **WFI Conductivity:** ≤ **1.3 µS/cm at 25 °C**
- **WFI Total Organic Carbon (TOC):** ≤ **500 ppb**
- **WFI Microbial Limit:** ≤ **10 CFU/100 mL**
- **Clean Steam:** condensate meets WFI specifications
- **Cleanroom ISO Class 5 (Grade A) Air:** ≤ **3,520 particles ≥0.5 µm/m³**
- **Steam Sterilization Temperature:** **121 °C at 1.1 bar (g)** for **≥15 minutes** (F₀ concept)
- **Compressed Air Dew Point:** ≤ **-40 °C** (for instrument air)
- **Process Air Sterility:** **0.22 µm in-line filter** at point of use

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Utility-by-Utility Theoretical Breakdown

### 🔹 2.1.1 Water Systems — The Lifeblood

Water is consumed in the **largest volume** in any bioprocess plant. The grades are arranged in a **purification hierarchy** (each successive grade must be produced FROM the previous one):

> **Municipal → Potable → Softened → Deionized (DI) → Reverse Osmosis (RO) → Purified Water (PW) → Distillation / Ultrafiltration → Water for Injection (WFI)**

**Generation Pathway:**

1. **Municipal water** → Chlorination + Sand filtration → **Potable water**
2. Potable → **Softener** (ion-exchange removes Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) → Softened water
3. Softened → **Activated Carbon Filter** (removes chlorine, organics) → Decarbonated water
4. Decarbonated → **Two-pass RO** → **Purified Water (PW)**
5. PW → **Multi-effect Distillator (MED)** OR **Vapor Compression Distillator (VCD)** OR **Reverse Osmosis (cold generation)** → **WFI**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between Purified Water and Water for Injection."*
> - **PW** — used for non-sterile product manufacture, CIP final rinse, media prep (non-sterile stage).
> - **WFI** — used for parenteral products, final rinse of sterile equipment, formulation water. Must be **apirogenic** (endotoxin-free, < **0.25 EU/mL** by LAL test).

**Storage & Distribution:**
- WFI stored at **≥ 80 °C** in **stainless steel 316L** tanks (hot loop distribution) to prevent microbial proliferation — this is the **"hot WFI loop"** concept.
- PW can be stored at **room temperature** with periodic sanitization at **65–80 °C**.

---

### 🔹 2.1.2 Steam Systems — The Sterilization Engine

Two grades:

| Property | **Plant Steam (PS)** | **Clean Steam (CS)** |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Source | Boiler, may use softened feed | Dedicated clean steam generator |
| Quality | May contain amines, hydrazine | Condensate meets WFI spec |
| Use | Non-product contact heating, jacket heating | SIP of product-contact piping, fermenters |
| Contaminants | Boiler chemicals, rust | Essentially free of additives |

**Steam Quality Parameters (KTU Must-Know):**
- **Dryness fraction (x):** mass of actual steam / total mass of steam + water (target **≥ 0.95** for SIP)
- **Superheat:** ΔT above saturation temperature (must be **< 5 °C** to avoid degrading gaskets)
- **Non-condensable gases (NCG):** O₂, CO₂, N₂ — must be **< 1%** (cause wet packs in autoclaves, slow sterilization)

**Sterilization F-value (F₀):**
$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}}$$

Where $T_{ref} = 121.1 \, °C$ and $z = 10 \, °C$ (thermal resistance of *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* spores).

**Target:** $F_0 \geq 8$ minutes (pharma-grade overkill design).

---

### 🔹 2.1.3 Compressed Air & Process Air

- **Instrument Air** → for pneumatic valves, dampers → must be **oil-free, dry (dew point ≤ -40 °C), particulate-free**.
- **Process Air** → enters fermenter through a **0.2 µm sterile filter** (hydrophobic PTFE membrane) → critical for aerobic fermentation.

**Compression Equation (work of compression, adiabatic ideal):**
$$W_{ideal} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

Where $\gamma = 1.4$ for air (Cp/Cv).

**Air Demand for Aerobic Fermentation:**
$$OTR = k_La \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

Where OTR = oxygen transfer rate, $k_La$ = volumetric mass transfer coefficient, $C^*$ = dissolved O₂ at saturation, $C_L$ = actual dissolved O₂.

For a typical *S. cerevisiae* fed-batch: **0.5–2.0 vvm** (volume of air per volume of medium per minute).

---

### 🔹 2.1.4 Cooling & Refrigeration

Heat generated in a fermenter comes from:
1. **Microbial metabolism** (catabolic heat) — biggest contributor in high-density cultures.
2. **Agitation** (mechanical → thermal).
3. **Sparging** (gas expansion cooling, but compressor heat gain offsets it).

**Heat Balance on Fermenter Jacket:**
$$Q_{removal} = m_{cw} \cdot C_{p,cw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Typical cooling water flow rate:** **1–3 L per L of broth per minute** (depending on heat load).

**Refrigeration Cycle (Vapor Compression):**
$$COP = \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$$

For bioprocess: chilled water at **6–8 °C**, glycol at **-5 to -10 °C** (for *Pichia pastoris* methanol-induction phases where heat is extreme).

---

### 🔹 2.1.5 HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning)

**Why HVAC is critical in bioprocess:**
- Prevents **cross-contamination** between batches.
- Maintains **aseptic Grade A/B** environment around open vials, sampling ports.
- Controls **temperature (20–24 °C)** and **relative humidity (40–60%)** for personnel comfort & static control.

**Cleanroom Classifications (ISO 14644-1 & WHO/EU GMP):**

| Class | At Rest (≥0.5 µm/m³) | In Operation | Air Changes/hr |
|-------|----------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| ISO 5 / Grade A | 3,520 | 3,520 | HEPA-filtered laminar flow |
| ISO 7 / Grade C | 352,000 | 3,520,000 | 20–40 |
| ISO 8 / Grade D | 3,520,000 | 35,200,000 | 10–20 |

**Pressure Cascade:** Aseptic core maintained at **+15 Pa** positive pressure relative to corridor → corridor at +10 Pa → general area at 0 Pa → corridors outside at -5 Pa (for containment of pathogens).

---

### 🔹 2.1.6 Electrical Power & Instrumentation

- **Critical loads** (PLCs, fermenter controllers, cold rooms) on **UPS + battery backup** (typically 30 min autonomy) + **Diesel Generator** redundancy.
- **Voltage:** 415 V, 3-phase for motors; 230 V, 1-phase for instruments.
- **Earthing:** < 1 Ω (for safety & signal integrity).

---

### 🔹 2.1.7 Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)

Biological effluent from a bioprocess plant contains:
- **Spent media** (high BOD/COD — 5,000–30,000 mg/L).
- **CIP chemicals** (NaOH, HNO₃).
- **Biomass** (killed cells).
- **Trace solvents, antibiotics** (if API plant).

**Treatment train:** Equalization → Neutralization (pH 6–8) → Biological treatment (Activated Sludge / SBR / MBR) → Clarification → Polishing (RO) → Discharge (PCB norms).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit | KTU Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer for cooling/heating | kW or kJ/h | Cooling water flow calculation |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat of steam | kJ | Steam consumption for SIP |
| 3 | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum 10^{(T - 121.1)/10}$ | Equivalent sterilization time at 121.1 °C | minutes | SIP validation |
| 4 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}RT_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Ideal adiabatic compression work | J/kg | Compressor sizing |
| 5 | $OTR = k_La(C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate | mmol O₂/L/h | Aeration design |
| 6 | $OUR = q_O_2 \cdot X$ | Oxygen uptake rate | mmol O₂/L/h | Fermentation scale-up |
| 7 | $P/V = k \cdot (N)^{3.0} \cdot (D_i)^{2.1}$ (impeller) | Power number correlation | W/m³ | Agitator scale-up |
| 8 | $P_{vapor} = P_{atm} \cdot e^{(-\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_b - 1/T)}$ | Antoine-type vapor pressure | Pa | Sterilization kinetics |
| 9 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius (death rate of microbes) | 1/min | Thermal death kinetics |
| 10 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | — | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |
| 11 | $D_{log\,reduction} = \log_{10}(N_0/N)$ | Decimal reduction value | log units | Sterilization effectiveness |
| 12 | $N_{ACH} = \dfrac{Q_{air}}{V_{room}}$ | Air changes per hour | 1/h | HVAC design |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Most utility-related problems reduce to two core equations — (1) $Q = mC_p\Delta T$ for cooling/heating, and (2) $Q = m\lambda$ for steam. Master them in 5 different problem contexts (SIP, fermenter cooling, CIP rinse heating, WFI still feed, humidification).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

- A **50,000 L monoclonal antibody bioreactor** consumes approximately:
  - **80,000 L/day** of WFI
  - **12,000 kg/day** of clean steam
  - **~2,400 kW** continuous cooling load
  - **0.9 vvm** sterile air = **2,160 m³/h**
  - **2.5 MW** total electrical demand

This is why utility systems are **CAPEX-heavy** (~25–35% of total plant cost) but absolutely non-negotiable for **product quality and regulatory compliance**.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The following are **exhaustively solved KTU-numerical style problems** that frequently appear in the 14-mark Part B questions. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Cooling Water Flow Rate for a Fermenter (Heat Balance)

**Problem Statement:**
A 10,000 L working volume *E. coli* fed-batch fermentation generates **metabolic heat at a rate of 2.5 × 10⁶ kJ/h**. The cooling water enters the jacket at **18 °C** and must not exceed **26 °C** on exit. Calculate:
(a) The required cooling water mass flow rate (kg/h).
(b) The volumetric flow rate (m³/h), given cooling water density ρ = 995 kg/m³.
(c) The actual exit temperature if water flow is limited to 80 m³/h.

**Given:**
- $Q = 2.5 \times 10^6 \, kJ/h$
- $C_p = 4.18 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ (for water)
- $T_{in} = 18 \, °C$, $T_{out, max} = 26 \, °C$
- $\rho = 995 \, kg/m^3$

**Solution:**

### (a) Mass Flow Rate

Start with the heat balance:
$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Rearrange for $\dot{m}$:
$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

Substitute:
$$\dot{m} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6 \, kJ/h}{4.18 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K) \times (26 - 18) \, K}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{4.18 \times 8}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{33.44}$$

$$\dot{m} = 74,760 \, kg/h$$

### (b) Volumetric Flow Rate
$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{74,760}{995} = 75.14 \, m^3/h$$

### (c) Actual Exit Temperature at 80 m³/h

Convert volumetric to mass flow:
$$\dot{m}_{actual} = 80 \times 995 = 79,600 \, kg/h$$

Rearrange energy balance for $T_{out}$:
$$T_{out} = T_{in} + \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_{actual} \cdot C_p}$$

$$T_{out} = 18 + \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{79,600 \times 4.18}$$

$$T_{out} = 18 + \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{3.327 \times 10^5}$$

$$T_{out} = 18 + 7.51 = 25.51 \, °C$$

Since 25.51 °C < 26 °C, the design is **safe**.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Steam Consumption for Sterilization-in-Place (SIP)

**Problem Statement:**
A 5,000 L fermenter contains 4,000 L of medium. It must be sterilized from **25 °C to 121 °C** (hold at 121 °C for 20 min), then cooled back to 30 °C. Using saturated steam at **1.5 bar (g)** ($\lambda = 2,200 \, kJ/kg$, $C_p \approx 4.18 \, kJ/kg\cdot K$). Estimate:
(a) Steam required for **heating** phase (assume 90% heat exchange efficiency, 15% heat losses).
(b) Steam required if $F_0$ target is 12 min and average hold temperature is 121.4 °C (use $z=10$).

**Solution:**

### (a) Heating Phase Steam

Heat to be supplied:
$$Q_{heat} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{heat} = 4,000 \times 4.18 \times (121 - 25)$$

$$Q_{heat} = 4,000 \times 4.18 \times 96 = 1.605 \times 10^6 \, kJ$$

Apply efficiency & loss correction (only 85% of latent heat effectively transferred, 15% lost):
$$Q_{effective} = \frac{1.605 \times 10^6}{0.85} = 1.888 \times 10^6 \, kJ$$

Steam mass:
$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q_{effective}}{\lambda} = \frac{1.888 \times 10^6}{2,200} = 858.2 \, kg$$

### (b) F₀ Validation Check

$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T - 121.1)/z}$$

At $T = 121.4 \, °C$ and $\Delta t = 20 \, min$:

$$F_0 = 20 \times 10^{(121.4 - 121.1)/10}$$

$$F_0 = 20 \times 10^{0.03} = 20 \times 1.0715 = 21.43 \, min$$

Since $F_0 = 21.43 > 12$ min target, **sterilization overkill is achieved** ✓

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Compressed Air Requirement for Aerobic Fermenter

**Problem Statement:**
A 20,000 L fermenter operates at **0.8 vvm** with **0.21 bar (g)** air sparging pressure above atmospheric. Calculate:
(a) Standard volumetric air flow in m³/min.
(b) Compressor power required if compression is from 1.013 bar to 2.5 bar (abs), inlet temp 30 °C, η_isentropic = 0.7.
(c) Compressed air storage tank volume (for 5 min reserve).

**Solution:**

### (a) Volumetric Air Flow

$$\dot{V} = 0.8 \times 20,000 = 16,000 \, L/min = 16 \, m^3/min$$

### (b) Compressor Power (Adiabatic)

$$W_{ideal} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

Plug in: $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287 \, kJ/(kg\cdot K)$, $T_1 = 303 \, K$, $P_1 = 1.013 \, bar$, $P_2 = 2.5 \, bar$:

Exponent: $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$

Pressure ratio: $\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{2.5}{1.013} = 2.468$

$$2.468^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln(2.468)} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 0.9034} = e^{0.2581} = 1.2944$$

$$W_{ideal} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 0.287 \times 303 \times (1.2944 - 1)$$

$$W_{ideal} = 3.5 \times 0.287 \times 303 \times 0.2944 = 89.62 \, kJ/kg$$

Mass flow rate of air:
$$\rho_{air} = \frac{P_1}{R T_1} = \frac{101,300}{287 \times 303} = 1.165 \, kg/m^3$$

$$\dot{m} = 1.165 \times 16 = 18.64 \, kg/min = 0.311 \, kg/s$$

Total ideal power:
$$P_{ideal} = 89.62 \times 0.311 = 27.87 \, kW$$

Actual shaft power:
$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta_{isen}} = \frac{27.87}{0.7} = 39.81 \, kW \approx 40 \, kW$$

### (c) Storage Tank Volume

For 5 minutes reserve:
$$V_{tank} = 16 \times 5 = 80 \, m^3$$

Recommended with **20% safety margin**: $V = 96 \, m^3$.

---

## 3.4 Problem 4 — HVAC Air Change Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A Grade B aseptic processing room measures **12 m × 8 m × 3.5 m** (L × B × H). Per WHO GMP, minimum **20 air changes per hour** are required. Calculate:
(a) Required supply air flow in m³/h.
(b) HEPA filter area if face velocity is 0.45 m/s.
(c) Cooling coil duty if supply air at 20 °C, 50% RH enters the room and leaves at 24 °C, 50% RH.

**Solution:**

### (a) Air Flow

$$V_{room} = 12 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 336 \, m^3$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 336 \times 20 = 6,720 \, m^3/h = 1.867 \, m^3/s$$

### (b) HEPA Filter Area

$$A_{HEPA} = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_{face}} = \frac{1.867}{0.45} = 4.15 \, m^2$$

Use **two filters** of ~2.1 m² each (typical 610 × 610 mm modules in parallel).

### (c) Cooling Coil Duty (Sensible)

$$Q = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T$$

$\rho_{air} \approx 1.2 \, kg/m^3$, so:
$$\dot{m} = 1.867 \times 1.2 = 2.24 \, kg/s$$

$$Q = 2.24 \times 1.005 \times (24 - 20) = 9.0 \, kW$$

This is the sensible heat gain to be removed.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Plant Steam vs Clean Steam

| Parameter | **Plant Steam** | **Clean Steam** |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Source feed water | Boiler feed (softened + deaerated) | Purified water (RO/DI) |
| Generation | Industrial boiler | Clean steam generator (CSG) — typically uses plant steam as heat source |
| Additives | Yes — amines, hydrazine, phosphates for corrosion control | None — meets WFI condensate specs |
| Endotoxin content | High (boiler chemicals) | < 0.25 EU/mL (WFI quality) |
| Use case | Non-product contact heating, HVAC, CIP pre-heat | SIP of product-contact surfaces, fermenters, vessels |
| Distribution material | Carbon steel (with rust allowance) | Stainless steel 316L, sanitary fittings |
| Cost | Low | High (~5–7× plant steam cost) |
| Regulatory status | Not validated for product contact | Validated under cGMP |

---

## 3.6 Master Validation Equation — Thermal Death of Microbial Load

For sterilization of a fermenter containing initial spore count $N_0$:

$$N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

Where the Arrhenius-dependent specific death rate:
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Sterilization design criterion: achieve $N < 10^{-3}$ (probability of contamination < 1 in 1,000 batches):

$$t_{sterilization} = \frac{\ln(N_0 / 10^{-3})}{k}$$

**Example:** If $N_0 = 10^6$ spores/batch and $k = 2.5 \, min^{-1}$ at 121 °C:

$$t = \frac{\ln(10^6 / 10^{-3})}{2.5} = \frac{\ln(10^9)}{2.5} = \frac{20.72}{2.5} = 8.29 \, min$$

This matches with the F₀ ≥ 8 min requirement **directly**.

## 4.2 Schematic Description (for narrative answer in KTU)

The diagram represents a **utility island layout** where:

1. The **Boiler House** generates Plant Steam (PS) from softened feed water. Plant Steam splits into two streams — one goes to **Clean Steam Generator (CSG)** for SIP duty, and the other serves **non-product contact heating** (CIP, HVAC humidification, heat exchanger pre-heat).
2. The **Water Treatment Block** follows a strict hierarchy — Municipal → Softened → RO → PW → Distillation → WFI. WFI is stored at **80 °C** in a hot loop to prevent microbial growth.
3. The **Compressed Air Block** includes suction filters, an oil-free compressor, refrigerated dryer, desiccant twin-tower dryer, carbon tower for oil vapor removal, an air receiver, and a final **0.2 µm sterile in-line filter** just before the fermenter sparger.
4. The **Chilled Water Plant** supplies 6–8 °C water to fermenter jackets, CIP heat removal coils, and HVAC cooling coils.
5. The **HVAC system** uses **terminal HEPA filters (H14 grade)** to deliver Grade A air to aseptic zones, with 20+ air changes per hour and a positive pressure cascade.
6. The **ETP** processes all spent media, biomass, and CIP effluents through biological treatment, achieving PCB discharge norms.

> [!TIP]
> **Drawing Tip for KTU Exam:** If asked to draw a utility diagram in the exam, use **rectangular blocks** (not pictorial equipment) labeled with the unit operation, connect with **single-direction arrows**, and **label each stream** (e.g., "Plant Steam 5 bar (g)", "PW 25 °C", "WFI 80 °C"). A clean labeled block diagram scores higher than an artistic sketch.

## 🔖 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Plant Steam and Clean Steam with respect to source, quality, application, and regulatory status.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Plant Steam** | **Clean Steam** |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Source** | Industrial boiler using softened feed water | Clean Steam Generator (CSG) using Purified Water as feed |
| **Quality** | May contain boiler additives (amines, hydrazine, phosphates); condensate not suitable for product contact | Condensate meets WFI specifications — apyrogenic, low TOC, low conductivity |
| **Application** | Non-product contact heating — jacket pre-heat, HVAC humidification, CIP solution heating | Sterilization-in-Place (SIP) of product-contact piping, fermenters, transfer lines |
| **Regulatory status** | Not validated for cGMP product contact | Validated under cGMP/ICH Q9, listed in critical utility matrix |
| **Material of construction** | Carbon steel piping | Stainless Steel 316L sanitary piping with orbital welding |

> **[3 marks: 1 for source, 1 for application, 1 for regulatory difference]**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is WFI stored and distributed at 80 °C in a hot loop? What material is used for the loop, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

WFI (Water for Injection) is stored and circulated in a **hot loop at ≥ 80 °C** to:
1. **Prevent microbial proliferation** — temperatures above 65 °C are lethal to *Pseudomonas*, *E. coli*, and most vegetative pathogens. The high temperature provides a continuous self-sanitization effect without the need for chemical sanitization.
2. **Eliminate biofilm formation** — biofilms are a major source of endotoxin (LPS) contamination in water systems.
3. **Reduce validation complexity** — a continuously hot loop requires less frequent sanitization cycles.

**Material of construction:** **Stainless Steel 316L** (low carbon variant) with:
- **Electropolished internal surfaces** (Ra ≤ 0.4 µm)
- **Orbital TIG welding** (no crevices)
- **Sanitary Tri-Clover fittings** (no dead legs)
- **Slope of ≥ 1%** to ensure self-drainability

> **[3 marks: 1 for microbial reason, 1 for material, 1 for design features]**

---

## 🔖 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU ESE pattern, Module 2 offers internal choice. Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat labeled schematic, describe the **various grades of water** used in a bioprocess plant. Explain the **generation pathway** from municipal water to WFI, listing the unit operations involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2,000 L fermenter contains 1,500 L of broth that must be heated from **28 °C to 121 °C** for sterilization. Saturated clean steam at **2 bar (g)** has a latent heat of **2,200 kJ/kg**. Assuming **80% steam utilization efficiency** and **10% heat loss to surroundings**, calculate:
   - (i) Total heat required (kJ)
   - (ii) Mass of steam required (kg)
   - (iii) If the hold time is 25 min at 121.3 °C, calculate the F₀ value and comment on whether sterilization is acceptable against a target F₀ of 12 min. **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Water Grades & Generation Pathway (7 marks)**

**Grades of Water in a Bioprocess Plant (in increasing purity):**

1. **Potable Water** — municipal-grade, used for initial rinsing, garden, toilets.
2. **Softened Water** — Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ removed by ion-exchange, used as boiler feed.
3. **Purified Water (PW)** — produced by RO + ion-exchange + UV (185 nm for TOC reduction), used for media/buffer prep, CIP final rinse, non-sterile product manufacture.
4. **Water for Injection (WFI)** — produced by distillation (MED/VCD) or RO (with final ultrafilter), used for parenteral products, final rinse of sterile equipment, formulation.

**Generation Pathway (Block Diagram form):**

```
Municipal Water
      ↓
Chlorination + Multimedia Sand Filter (removes turbidity, suspended solids)
      ↓
Activated Carbon Filter (removes chlorine, organics, color)
      ↓
Water Softener (ion-exchange Na⁺ for Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺)
      ↓
Degasifier (removes CO₂)
      ↓
Two-Pass Reverse Osmosis (removes 99% dissolved ions, organics, microbes)
      ↓
UV Lamp (185 nm for TOC, 254 nm for microbial control)
      ↓
  ┌────────────────┬─────────────────────┐
  ↓                ↓                     ↓
PW Storage      Single-Pass RO        Multi-Effect
(Ambient)       (Cold WFI - rare)     Distillator (MED)
                                      or Vapor Compression
                                            ↓
                                  WFI Storage at 80 °C
```

**Key Specifications (must be memorized):**

| Parameter | PW Limit | WFI Limit |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| Conductivity (25 °C) | ≤ 1.3 µS/cm (in some) | ≤ 1.3 µS/cm |
| TOC | ≤ 500 ppb | ≤ 500 ppb |
| Microbial count | ≤ 100 CFU/mL | ≤ 10 CFU/100 mL |
| Endotoxin | Not specified | ≤ 0.25 EU/mL (LAL) |

> **[7 marks: 2 for listing grades, 3 for pathway, 2 for specs]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Total Heat Required:**

$$Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q = 1,500 \times 4.18 \times (121 - 28)$$

$$Q = 1,500 \times 4.18 \times 93 = 583,110 \, kJ$$

**(ii) Mass of Steam (with efficiency and losses):**

$$Q_{steam\,supplied} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta_{utilization} \times (1 - heat\,loss)}$$

$$Q_{steam\,supplied} = \frac{583,110}{0.80 \times 0.90} = \frac{583,110}{0.72} = 809,875 \, kJ$$

$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q_{steam\,supplied}}{\lambda} = \frac{809,875}{2,200} = 368.1 \, kg$$

**(iii) F₀ Calculation:**

$$F_0 = \Delta t \times 10^{(T - 121.1)/z}$$

$$F_0 = 25 \times 10^{(121.3 - 121.1)/10}$$

$$F_0 = 25 \times 10^{0.02} = 25 \times 1.0471 = 26.18 \, min$$

**Comment:** Since $F_0 = 26.18 \, min > 12 \, min$ target, the sterilization is **acceptable with a comfortable safety margin** (~2.18× the target). The overkill approach protects against any cold spots in the vessel.

> **[7 marks: 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 2 for part (iii) calculation, 1 for comment]**

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role and design of HVAC systems** in a biopharmaceutical/bioprocess facility. Explain the **pressure cascade concept**, **air changes per hour** requirement, and the function of **HEPA filters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Rushton-turbine agitated fermenter** of 25,000 L total volume is aerated at **1.0 vvm** with process air at 30 °C, 1.2 bar (abs). Calculate:
   - (i) The mass of air required per hour.
   - (ii) The volumetric flow rate at fermentation conditions.
   - (iii) The power required by a two-stage reciprocating compressor to deliver this air at 4 bar (abs) with an isentropic efficiency of 70%. Assume ambient conditions: 1.013 bar, 30 °C, and air behaves as an ideal gas with γ = 1.4, R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question B

**(a) HVAC in Bioprocess Plant (7 marks)**

HVAC = Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning. In a bioprocess plant, HVAC is **not a comfort system** — it is a **contamination control system** and a **regulatory requirement** under:
- WHO TRS 986 (Annex 2, 2014)
- EU GMP Annex 1 (2022 revision)
- US FDA Aseptic Processing Guidance (2004)
- ISO 14644-1 (Cleanroom classification)

**Functions:**
1. **Filtration** — HEPA filters (H14 grade, 99.97% efficiency at 0.3 µm) remove particulates, microbes, and spores.
2. **Temperature control** — 20–24 °C for aseptic zones (operator comfort + low metabolic activity of contaminants).
3. **Humidity control** — 40–60% RH (low RH = less microbial growth; high RH = static & corrosion).
4. **Pressure cascade** — Aseptic core at +15 Pa, corridor at +10 Pa, gowning room at 0 Pa, ensuring airflow is always **outward** from clean to less clean zones.

**Air Changes per Hour (ACH):**

| Grade | ACH (minimum) |
|-------|---------------|
| A (ISO 5) | Laminar flow (0.45 m/s ± 20%) |
| B (ISO 7) | 20–40 |
| C (ISO 8) | 20 |
| D (unclassified) | 10 |

**HEPA Filter Function:**
- Made of **borosilicate microfibers** in a corrugated aluminum separator.
- Captures particles by **3 mechanisms**: interception, impaction, diffusion.
- Tested in-situ with **DOP/PAO aerosol** (poly-alpha-olefin, 0.3 µm) for **99.97% retention**.

> **[7 marks: 2 for roles, 2 for pressure cascade, 2 for ACH, 1 for HEPA]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Volumetric flow at standard conditions:**

$$\dot{V}_{NTP} = 1.0 \times 25,000 = 25,000 \, L/min = 1,500 \, m^3/h$$

**(ii) Mass of air per hour:**

Using ideal gas at NTP-like conditions (considering conditions 30 °C, 1.2 bar):
$$\rho = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{287 \times 303} = 1.380 \, kg/m^3$$

$$\dot{m} = 1.380 \times 1,500 = 2,070 \, kg/h = 0.575 \, kg/s$$

**(iii) Compressor Power:**

$$W_{ideal} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

$P_1 = 1.013 \, bar$, $P_2 = 4 \, bar$, $T_1 = 303 \, K$, $\gamma = 1.4$

Pressure ratio = $4 / 1.013 = 3.949$

Exponent $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$

$3.949^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln(3.949)} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.3735} = e^{0.3924} = 1.4806$

$$W_{ideal} = 3.5 \times 0.287 \times 303 \times (1.4806 - 1)$$

$$W_{ideal} = 3.5 \times 0.287 \times 303 \times 0.4806 = 146.5 \, kJ/kg$$

$$\dot{W}_{ideal} = 146.5 \times 0.575 = 84.24 \, kW$$

$$\dot{W}_{shaft} = \frac{84.24}{0.70} = 120.3 \, kW \approx 120 \, kW$$

(Add ~5% mechanical losses → ~126 kW motor selected.)

> **[7 marks: 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 3 for part (iii) including Arrhenius & efficiency]**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Utility Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Examiner specifically looks for $kJ$, $kg/h$, $m^3/h$. Writing just "120" without "kW" → **-1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Plant Steam with Clean Steam** in SIP calculations. Plant steam CANNOT be used for SIP of product-contact lines.
> 3. **In F₀ calculation**, many students forget to subtract the reference temperature exponent correctly. Always write: $10^{(T-121.1)/10}$.
> 4. **In compressor problems**, students use the wrong $P_1$ — it must be **inlet pressure (absolute)**, not gauge. Convert $1 \, bar(g) = 2 \, bar(a)$.
> 5. **For HVAC**, do not write just "20 air changes" — specify "20 air changes **per hour**" and reference the regulatory standard (WHO/EU GMP).
> 6. **WFI vs PW confusion** — WFI is **apyrogenic**, PW is not necessarily so. Endotoxin < 0.25 EU/mL is the defining difference.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **8 utility classes**: Water, Steam, Air/Gas, Cooling, Heating, HVAC, Power, ETP.
- ✅ **Water hierarchy**: Potable → Softened → RO → PW → Distillation → WFI.
- ✅ **WFI hot loop at 80 °C** in 316L SS electropolished piping — prevents biofilm.
- ✅ **Plant steam ≠ Clean steam** — CSG needed for SIP of product-contact parts.
- ✅ **F₀ ≥ 8 min** for sterilization; $F_0 = \Delta t \times 10^{(T-121.1)/10}$.
- ✅ **0.22 µm in-line filter** is the last barrier before fermenter sparger.
- ✅ **Dew point of instrument air ≤ -40 °C**; oil-free compressor mandatory.
- ✅ **Compressor power equation** uses absolute pressures, $γ = 1.4$ for air.
- ✅ **Cooling water formula**: $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ → use this for jacket design.
- ✅ **HVAC pressure cascade** is **+15 Pa aseptic core**; airflow always from clean → less clean.
- ✅ **Grade A**: laminar flow 0.45 m/s, **Grade B**: 20–40 ACH, **Grade C**: 20 ACH, **Grade D**: 10 ACH.
- ✅ **HEPA H14** = 99.97% at 0.3 µm (DOP/PAO tested).
- ✅ **OTR = $k_La(C^* - C_L)$** — central equation for aeration.
- ✅ **ETP stages**: Equalize → Neutralize → Activated sludge → Clarify → RO → Discharge.
- ✅ **Critical utilities validated** under cGMP, ICH Q9, WHO TRS 986.
- ✅ **Single biggest cost driver**: WFI generation and clean steam (5–7× plant steam cost).
- ✅ **Total utility CAPEX** = 25–35% of bioprocess plant cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Processes for the Manufacture with the Important Engineering Problems Involved in the Manufacture of the Following Products with a Flow Diagram, Reactions and Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Industrial Processes for the Manufacture with the Important Engineering Problems Involved in the Manufacture of the Following Products with a Flow Diagram, Reactions and Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Bioprocess Technology — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Bioprocess Technology** is the applied branch of biochemical engineering that deals with the design, operation, and optimization of processes in which **living cells (microorganisms, plant cells, animal cells, or enzymes)** are used to manufacture commercially valuable products. The discipline integrates microbiology, biochemistry, chemical engineering, and process economics to scale laboratory biological reactions into robust, sterile, and economically viable industrial manufacturing operations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this module specifically targets **secondary metabolites, beverages, organic acids, and primary metabolites of industrial importance**, where the emphasis is on:
- The choice of **microbial workhorse** (bacteria, yeast, mould).
- The **biochemical reaction stoichiometry** (substrate → product).
- The **physico-chemical conditions** of fermentation (pH, T, pO₂, agitation).
- The **flow diagram** of upstream + downstream processing.
- The **engineering problems** that compromise yield, productivity, or sterility.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Anchored to the KTU Syllabus

| Term | Definition (KTU-aligned) |
|---|---|
| **Fermentation** | A biochemical process in which microorganisms transform organic substrates into products via enzymatic pathways, with or without molecular oxygen. |
| **Inoculum** | A small, actively growing, axenic (pure) culture used to seed a large production fermenter. |
| **Upstream Processing (USP)** | All preparatory steps: media formulation, sterilization, inoculum development, and fermenter design. |
| **Downstream Processing (DSP)** | Recovery and purification of the product: separation, extraction, chromatography, crystallization, drying. |
| **Catabolite Repression** | Inhibition of enzyme synthesis by rapidly metabolized carbon sources (e.g., glucose suppresses lactase). |
| **Product Inhibition** | Accumulation of product slows the catalytic/inherent reaction rate, a key engineering concern. |
| **Submerged Fermentation (SmF)** | Microbes grown in a liquid nutrient broth inside a stirred-tank reactor. |
| **Solid-State Fermentation (SSF)** | Microbes grown on a moist solid substrate with little or no free water. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Mandatory Products:** Ethanol, Penicillin (Antibiotic), Citric Acid, Beer (Brewing), and Lactic Acid are the canonical industrial products examined. Each product must be studied in terms of (i) the producing organism, (ii) the biochemical reaction, (iii) process conditions, (iv) the flow diagram, and (v) the engineering problems that limit scale-up.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Industrial Kitchen" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a chef asked to bake **1,00,000 loaves of bread** in a single shift. You cannot simply multiply the home recipe. You must:
> - **Buy raw material in tons** (substrate).
> - **Control yeast activity** so it neither starves nor dies (fermentation condition).
> - **Manage heat** so the ovens don't explode (heat-transfer problem).
> - **Sterilize every surface** to keep out wild moulds (sterility problem).
> - **Cool, slice, pack and ship** the loaves (downstream processing).

A **bioprocess engineer** faces the same challenges but with *microorganisms* as the chefs. The "kitchen" is the **fermenter**; the "baker" is the **microbial cell**; the "loaves" are the **product** (ethanol, penicillin, citric acid, beer, lactic acid). The engineering problems are identical: **heat removal, mass transfer of O₂, foam control, sterility, scale-up, and product recovery yield**.

## 1.4 Fermentation Modes Used in Industry (KTU Classification)

1. **Batch Fermentation** — Closed system; all nutrients added at t=0; product harvested at the end. *Example: most antibiotic runs (penicillin).*
2. **Fed-Batch Fermentation** — Nutrients are fed continuously or in pulses to extend productivity and avoid catabolite repression. *Example: bakers' yeast, recombinant protein, lysine.*
3. **Continuous (Chemostat) Fermentation** — Fresh medium added and product withdrawn at constant rate at steady state. *Example: single-cell protein (SCP), lactic acid at industrial scale.*

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU loves this distinction:** Examiners frequently test the ability to match a *product* to its *fermentation mode*. Penicillin = Batch (feedback inhibition by the antibiotic). SCP = Continuous (high cell density required). Penicillium citric acid = SSF/SmF. Beer = Batch (yeast harvested after attenuation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Industrial Bioprocess Framework

Every industrial bioprocess, irrespective of product, follows a **four-stage engineering chain**:

**Stage 1 → Inoculum Development (USP-1)**
- Working stock → Agar slant → Test-tube culture → Shake flask → Seed fermenter → Production fermenter.
- Goal: build a *physiologically uniform, axenic, high-viability* population.

**Stage 2 → Medium Formulation & Sterilization (USP-2)**
- Carbon source (molasses, glucose, sucrose, starch hydrolysate).
- Nitrogen source (corn-steep liquor, soy meal, ammonium salts, urea).
- Minerals (P, K, Mg, S, trace Fe/Zn/Mn).
- pH buffer (CaCO₃).
- Sterilization: batch (121 °C, 20 min) or continuous (HTST at 135 °C, 5–10 s).

**Stage 3 → Production Fermentation (Bioreactor)**
- Type: Stirred-tank reactor (STR), airlift, bubble column, packed-bed.
- Mode: Batch / Fed-Batch / Continuous.
- Critical controls: T, pH, pO₂ (Dissolved Oxygen Tension, DOT), agitation speed, foam.

**Stage 4 → Downstream Processing (DSP)**
- Cell separation (centrifugation, filtration, microfiltration).
- Cell disruption (if intracellular product: homogenizer, bead mill).
- Concentration (evaporation, ultrafiltration).
- Purification (solvent extraction, ion exchange, chromatography).
- Polishing (crystallization, spray-drying, freeze-drying).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Problems (KTU 2024 Hot-List)** — these seven problems are tested in nearly every Part-B question of this module:
> 1. **Sterility maintenance** (microbial contamination).
> 2. **Heat removal** (fermentation is exothermic; Q = m·Cp·ΔT must equal U·A·ΔT_lm).
> 3. **Oxygen mass transfer** in aerobic fermentations (k_La is the rate-limiting parameter).
> 4. **Foam control** (mechanical + chemical antifoams like silicone).
> 5. **Raw-material cost & seasonal variability** (molasses price fluctuates).
> 6. **Product inhibition / catabolite repression** (glucose suppresses α-amylase, penicillin, etc.).
> 7. **Scale-up of mixing and mass transfer** (geometric similarity, P/V, k_La matching).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Biomass Yield (Y_X/S)** | $Y_{X/S} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g biomass per g substrate consumed. |
| 2 | **Product Yield (Y_P/S)** | $Y_{P/S} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | g product per g substrate consumed. |
| 3 | **Productivity (Q_P)** | $Q_{P} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{final}} \,-\, P_{\text{initial}}}{t_{\text{fermentation}}}$ | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ — defines the economy of the plant. |
| 4 | **Specific Growth Rate (μ)** | $\mu \;=\; \dfrac{1}{X}\,\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | h⁻¹; in Monod kinetics, $\mu = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_s+S}$. |
| 5 | **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR)** | $OTR \;=\; k_{L}a\,(C^{*}-C_{L})$ | mol O₂·L⁻¹·h⁻¹; equals OUR at steady state. |
| 6 | **Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient** | $k_{L}a \;=\; k\,\left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)^{\alpha}\,u_{s}^{\beta}$ | Function of power input and superficial gas velocity. |
| 7 | **Degree of Reduction Balance** | $\sum n_i \gamma_i \;(\text{reactants}) \;=\; \sum n_j \gamma_j \;(\text{products})$ | Used to balance C-H-O-N fermentations. |
| 8 | **Overall Heat Removal** | $Q_{\text{gen}} \;=\; U\,A\,\Delta T_{lm}$ | Cooling coil/jacket design. |
| 9 | **Theoretical Ethanol Yield (Gay-Lussac)** | $C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \rightarrow 2\,C_{2}H_{5}OH + 2\,CO_{2}$ | 0.511 g ethanol / g glucose (100 % of theoretical). |
| 10 | **Citric Acid Theoretical Yield** | $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + 3\,O_{2} \rightarrow 2\,C_{6}H_{8}O_{7} + 3\,H_{2}O$ | 1.073 g citric acid / g sucrose. |
| 11 | **Beer Original Extract (OE)** | $OE = \dfrac{\text{Plato}^{\circ} \text{ of wort}}{1}$ | Determines final alcohol by attenuation. |
| 12 | **Apparent Attenuation** | $AA\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{(OE - RE)}{OE} \times 100$ | RE = Real Extract of finished beer. |
| 13 | **Doubling Time** | $t_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | h; for Penicillium ≈ 2–4 h. |
| 14 | **Reynolds Number (impeller)** | $Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho\,N\,D_{i}^{2}}{\mu_{\text{visc}}}$ | Determines flow regime in fermenter. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** Questions on oxygen transfer (k_La) and biomass yield (Y_X/S) appear almost every semester. Memorize the OTR equation and the Monod form of μ.

## 2.3 The "Why & How" Behind Each Process

- **Why is Penicillin produced in batch, not continuous?** Because penicillin is a *secondary metabolite* — its synthesis is repressed by glucose and requires phosphate limitation, conditions naturally met in a stationary phase batch run.
- **Why is ethanol distillation necessary?** Because at 30 °C, ethanol–water forms an *azeotrope* at 95.6 % (v/v); simple distillation cannot exceed this. Azeotropic/entrainer distillation or molecular sieves are needed to reach 99.9 %.
- **Why is Aspergillus niger used in trays for citric acid (Koji process)?** Because *SSF* on wheat bran or beet pulp enables very high titres (>150 g/L) with low energy and low contamination risk.
- **Why is lactic acid produced in a calcium-carbonate-buffered fermenter?** Because lactic acid drops the pH to ~3, which is *lethal to Lactobacillus*. CaCO₃ neutralizes it in-situ to Ca-lactate, which is later acidified.
- **Why does beer wort need to be boiled with hops?** For *isomerization of α-acids → iso-α-acids* (the bittering compounds) and for sterilization, protein coagulation, and Maillard colour formation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Industrial Process Details

> [!NOTE]
> For each product, the KTU 2024 evaluator expects you to articulate: **organism + raw material + biochemical reaction + process conditions (T, pH, pO₂, time) + flow diagram + recovery steps + engineering problems**. The exhaustive treatment below follows exactly that structure.

---

## 3.1 Product 1 — ETHANOL (Ethyl Alcohol) by Fermentation

### 3.1.1 Producing Organism
- **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** (baker's yeast) — the workhorse of the brewing/distilling industry. GRAS status (Generally Recognized As Safe).
- Alternative: *Zymomonas mobilis* (faster, but less ethanol-tolerant and not GRAS).

### 3.1.2 Raw Material
- **Sugarcane molasses** (preferred in India/Kerala), beet molasses, or **corn-starch hydrolysate** (USA, Brazil).
- Molasses composition: ~50 % total sugars (sucrose, glucose, fructose), 15 % water, 10 % ash, 5 % non-sugars.

### 3.1.3 Biochemical Reaction (Gay-Lussac, 1815)
- **Glucose pathway:** Glycolysis → Pyruvate → Acetaldehyde (via PDC) → Ethanol (via ADH).
- **Net stoichiometry:**

$$
C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}}\; 2\,C_{2}H_{5}OH \;+\; 2\,CO_{2} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -235\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}
$$

- **Theoretical yield:** 0.511 g ethanol per g glucose.
- **Industrial practical yield:** 90–95 % of theoretical → ~0.46–0.48 g ethanol per g glucose.

### 3.1.4 Process Conditions
- **pH:** 4.5 – 5.0 (controlled with H₂SO₄ or NH₄OH).
- **Temperature:** 30 – 35 °C (mesophilic optimum; >38 °C → stuck fermentation).
- **pO₂:** Trace oxygen (micro-aerobic) — strictly anaerobic, but a tiny O₂ pulse needed for sterol/unsaturated-fatty-acid synthesis to maintain membrane integrity.
- **Fermentation time:** 36 – 72 h.
- **Substrate concentration:** 16 – 22 % w/v sugars (above 22 % → osmotic stress).
- **Inoculum size:** 5 – 10 % v/v of active yeast slurry.

### 3.1.5 Engineering Problems Specific to Ethanol
1. **Ethanol inhibition** — product itself is toxic; >120 g/L halts growth. *Solution: vacuum fermentation, membrane cell-recycle, extractive fermentation.*
2. **CO₂ stripping** — large volumes of CO₂ cause foaming and stripping of volatile flavours.
3. **Sterility** — open molasses tanks attract wild yeast, *Acetobacter*, and lactic acid bacteria.
4. **Sugar concentration ceiling** — high gravity (VHG) brewing above 25 % sugars requires very-high-gravity-tolerant strains.

### 3.1.6 Downstream Processing
1. **Centrifugation / settling** → recovery of yeast cream (sold as bakers' yeast or recycled).
2. **Beer column distillation** → 95 % v/v azeotrope.
3. **Azeotropic distillation** with benzene/cyclohexane/ethyl ether (older plants) or **molecular-sieve adsorption** (modern) → 99.9 % absolute ethanol.
4. **Dehydration** → Anhydrous ethanol (used as fuel and pharma-grade).
5. **Spent wash** → anaerobic digestion to biogas (a key circular-economy feature).

---

## 3.2 Product 2 — PENICILLIN (β-Lactam Antibiotic)

### 3.2.1 Producing Organism
- **Penicillium chrysogenum** (formerly *P. notatum*) — aerobic filamentous fungus.
- Modern industrial strains are mutants of **NRRL 1951** × multiple UV/chemical mutagenesis cycles; today's strains yield **>50 g/L** (vs Fleming's 2 mg/L).

### 3.2.2 Raw Material
- **Corn-steep liquor** (CSL) — primary nitrogen/vitamin source; cheap, complex.
- **Lactose** (or glucose fed-batch) — slow-release sugar to avoid catabolite repression.
- **Mineral salts:** KH₂PO₄, MgSO₄, NaCl, FeSO₄, CuSO₄ (trace).
- **Phenylacetic acid** (PAA) or phenoxyacetic acid (POA) — **side-chain precursors** that direct the synthesis toward Penicillin G or V respectively.

### 3.2.3 Biochemical Reaction
- Penicillin is synthesized by a **non-ribosomal peptide synthetase** — **ACV synthetase** (ACVS) → tripeptide δ-(L-α-aminoadipyl)-L-cysteinyl-D-valine (ACV) → **isopenicillin N synthetase (IPNS)** → isopenicillin N → **acyltransferase (IAT)** → Penicillin G (benzyl-penicillin).

$$
\text{ACV} \;\xrightarrow{\text{IPNS, O}_{2}}\; \text{Isopenicillin N} \;\xrightarrow{\text{IAT, PAA}}\; \text{Penicillin G}
$$

### 3.2.4 Process Conditions
- **pH:** 6.5 – 7.0 (controlled with H₂SO₄/NaOH).
- **Temperature:** 25 – 27 °C (lower than ethanol; *Penicillium* is mesophilic but sensitive to heat).
- **pO₂:** **High** — 30 – 50 % saturation (strictly aerobic; O₂ is a substrate for IPNS).
- **Agitation:** 200 – 500 rpm with Rushton turbines (high viscosity, mycelial pellets).
- **Fermentation time:** 7 – 10 days (long batch).
- **Mode:** **Fed-batch** — glucose fed to maintain ~1 g/L residual; lactose as base.
- **Inoculum:** Spore suspension → seed fermenter → production fermenter.

### 3.2.5 Engineering Problems Specific to Penicillin
1. **Shear stress** on hyphae — high agitation damages mycelia → pellet morphology chosen.
2. **Oxygen demand is huge** (10× higher than ethanol) → k_La must be maintained at 200–400 h⁻¹.
3. **Catabolite repression** of IPNS by glucose — fed-batch essential.
4. **Foaming** — severe due to proteins in CSL and mycelial growth → mechanical foam breaker + antifoam.
5. **Heat removal** — metabolic heat ~15 kW/m³.
6. **Product degradation** — penicillin is β-lactamase-sensitive and acid-labile → must be kept cold.
7. **Phage contamination** in *Streptomyces* processes (less common in *Penicillium*).

### 3.2.6 Downstream Processing
1. **Filtration** (rotary vacuum drum) → cell-free broth.
2. **Cooling to 2 °C** → acidification to pH 2.0–2.5 → extraction into **butyl acetate / amyl acetate**.
3. **Back-extraction into aqueous buffer** at pH 7.0 → penicillin salt.
4. **Activated-carbon treatment** → decolorization.
5. **Crystallization** (K-/Na-penicillin) → drying → final product.
6. For semi-synthetic penicillins (ampicillin, amoxicillin) → cleavage of side chain by **penicillin acylase** → 6-APA + new side chain.

---

## 3.3 Product 3 — CITRIC ACID

### 3.3.1 Producing Organism
- **Aspergillus niger** — filamentous fungus. Tolerates low pH, high sugar, and produces citric acid as the dominant organic acid.
- Alternative: *Candida lipolytica* (yeast) for n-alkane substrates.

### 3.3.2 Raw Material
- **Beet/cane molasses** (most common).
- **Corn starch / glucose syrup** (purified).
- **Sucrose or glucose** (pure-substrate fermentation).
- **Mineral salts:** KH₂PO₄, MgSO₄, NH₄NO₃, Mn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺ (trace).

### 3.3.3 Biochemical Reaction
- **Glycolysis** → Pyruvate → mitochondrial **pyruvate carboxylase** + **citrate synthase** → Citrate.
- **Net stoichiometry (from sucrose):**

$$
C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} \;+\; 3\,O_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_{6}H_{8}O_{7} \;+\; 3\,H_{2}O \quad (\text{stoichiometric yield} \;\approx\; 1.073\; \text{g·g}^{-1})
$$

- **Net stoichiometry (from glucose):**

$$
C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,O_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; C_{6}H_{8}O_{7} \;+\; 2\,H_{2}O
$$

- Industrial yield: **70 – 80 %** of theoretical.

### 3.3.4 Process Conditions
- **pH:** **Below 2.0** — auto-acidification (unique feature, prevents contamination).
- **Temperature:** 28 – 30 °C.
- **pO₂:** High — 40 – 60 % saturation.
- **Agitation:** 200 – 400 rpm; aeration 0.5 – 1.0 vvm.
- **Fermentation time:** 5 – 9 days.
- **Mode:** Surface (Koji trays) historically; today **submerged batch** dominates.
- **Critical trace metal balance:** **Mn²⁺ limitation** (<2 ppb) is essential to shift metabolism toward citrate (high Mn → oxalic acid).

### 3.3.5 Engineering Problems Specific to Citric Acid
1. **Mycelial morphology** — must be **pellet form**, not dispersed hyphae (affects mass transfer and downstream filtration).
2. **Trace metal control** — Mn, Fe, Zn at ppb levels must be chelated (EDTA, ferrocyanide).
3. **Severe acid corrosion** of fermenter and pipelines → **stainless-steel 316L or fibre-glass lining** required.
4. **Oxygen demand** very high (Q_O₂ ≈ 5–10 mmol O₂·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹).
5. **Foam** — must be controlled without silicone antifoam (antifoam contaminates downstream).
6. **Strain degeneration** — long-term cultures lose productivity → master working cell bank (MWCB) protocol.
7. **Waste mycelium disposal** — high BOD/COD load.

### 3.3.6 Downstream Processing (Lime–Sulfuric Process)
1. **Filtration** → mycelium separated (sent to animal feed).
2. **Citric acid precipitated as calcium citrate** by adding Ca(OH)₂ / CaCO₃ at pH 7.
3. **Filtration** → calcium-citrate cake.
4. **Acidification with H₂SO₄** → gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) filtered off; free citric acid in solution.
5. **Activated-carbon purification** → decolorization.
6. **Vacuum evaporation** → concentrated liquor.
7. **Crystallization** → citric acid monohydrate crystals.
8. **Drying** → final product.

> [!TIP]
> Alternative: **solvent extraction** with trilaurylamine in kerosene avoids gypsum and gives a cleaner effluent. The **lime–sulfuric** process dominates because of its simplicity and reagent cost.

---

## 3.4 Product 4 — BEER (Brewing)

### 3.4.1 Producing Organism
- **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** (ale yeast, top-fermenting, 18 – 22 °C) and *S. carlsbergensis / S. pastorianus* (lager yeast, bottom-fermenting, 8 – 14 °C).

### 3.4.2 Raw Material
- **Malted barley** (the principal starch source; "malting" = controlled germination that activates amylases).
- **Adjuncts** (rice, corn grits, sorghum) — cheaper starch fillers; up to 30 % of grist.
- **Hops** (*Humulus lupulus*) — bittering + preservative (iso-α-acids).
- **Water** — the dominant ingredient; profile (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, HCO₃⁻) dictates beer style.
- **Yeast** — pitched at ~10⁶ cells·mL⁻¹.

### 3.4.3 Biochemical Reaction (during fermentation)
- Malt amylases convert starch → maltose + maltotriose + dextrins (in mashing).
- Yeast ferments maltose and maltotriose:

$$
C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} \;+\; H_{2}O \;\longrightarrow\; 4\,C_{2}H_{5}OH \;+\; 4\,CO_{2}
$$

- Wort dextrins are unfermentable, giving body and residual sweetness.

### 3.4.4 Process Conditions
- **Mashing:** 65 – 67 °C (β-amylase optimum); 1 h.
- **Wort boiling:** 100 °C, 60 – 90 min with hops.
- **Wort cooling:** 18 – 22 °C (ale) or 8 – 12 °C (lager).
- **Fermentation T:** 18 – 22 °C (ale) / 8 – 12 °C (lager).
- **pH:** 4.0 – 4.5.
- **Time:** 5 – 7 days primary + 2 – 4 weeks lagering (cold maturation).
- **Attenuation limit:** 75 – 85 % of wort extract.

### 3.4.5 Engineering Problems Specific to Beer
1. **Off-flavours** — diacetyl (butter-scotch), DMS (cooked corn), acetaldehyde → lagering removes them.
2. **Stuck fermentation** — yeast flocculation too early → adjuncts and Ca²⁺ balance must be tuned.
3. **Oxidation** — staling aldehydes form rapidly; O₂ exclusion critical in packaging.
4. **Haziness** — proteins, polyphenols, β-glucans; need finings (PVPP, silica gel, isinglass).
5. **Hop utilization** — only ~25 % of α-acids isomerize in boiling; too long → polyphenol extraction; too short → low bitterness.
6. **Foam stability** — proteases and lipids in wort affect head retention.
7. **Brewery effluents** — high BOD/COD, must be anaerobically digested.

### 3.4.6 Downstream Processing
1. **Maturation / Lagering** at 0 – 2 °C.
2. **Filtration** (kieselguhr / DE filter, then PVPP).
3. **Carbonation** to ~2.5 g CO₂/L.
4. **Packaging** — kegs, bottles, cans (with N₂ blanket to exclude O₂).
5. **Pasteurization** (tunnel for bottles; flash for kegs) → 60 °C, 15 min PU.

---

## 3.5 Product 5 — LACTIC ACID

### 3.5.1 Producing Organism
- **Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. *bulgaricus*** — homofermentative, thermophilic, GRAS.
- Alternative: *L. casei*, *L. plantarum*, *L. rhamnosus* — mesophilic alternatives.

### 3.5.2 Raw Material
- **Glucose syrup**, **cane/beet sugar**, **corn starch hydrolysate**, **molasses**, **whey lactose** (dairy side-stream).
- **Yeast extract**, **corn-steep liquor** for nitrogen.

### 3.5.3 Biochemical Reaction (Homofermentative)

$$
C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;\xrightarrow{\text{LDH}}\; 2\,C_{3}H_{6}O_{3} \quad (\text{lactic acid, theoretical yield} = 1.0\;\text{g·g}^{-1})
$$

- Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway → pyruvate → lactate (via **lactate dehydrogenase**, LDH).
- The product exists as L-(+)-lactic acid (desired for PLA bioplastics) or D-(-)-lactic acid.

### 3.5.4 Process Conditions
- **pH:** 5.5 – 6.5 maintained by **CaCO₃** neutralization (auto-control of pH is impossible without buffer).
- **Temperature:** 45 – 50 °C (thermophilic *L. delbrueckii*) or 30 – 37 °C (mesophilic).
- **pO₂:** Strictly **anaerobic** / microaerophilic.
- **Time:** 48 – 96 h.
- **Mode:** Batch or continuous (with cell recycle).
- **Inoculum:** 5 – 10 % v/v of 18-h culture.

### 3.5.5 Engineering Problems Specific to Lactic Acid
1. **Product inhibition** — undissociated lactic acid is highly toxic; pH control with CaCO₃ is mandatory.
2. **Low productivity** — neutralization produces calcium lactate, not the free acid.
3. **Recovery complexity** — recovery from Ca-lactate requires H₂SO₄ → gypsum waste (1 kg gypsum per kg lactic acid).
4. **Nutrient cost** — yeast extract is expensive; research on cheap complex N-sources.
5. **Optical purity** — for PLA, only L- or D-isomer desired; racemic mixtures unusable.
6. **Phage contamination** in continuous culture — rotational strain management.

### 3.5.6 Downstream Processing
1. **Cell removal** by ultrafiltration or centrifugation.
2. **Acidification with H₂SO₄** → CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum) precipitate; crude lactic acid in solution.
3. **Activated-carbon decolorization**.
4. **Evaporation** → 50 – 88 % syrup.
5. **Esterification with methanol → methyl lactate → distillation → hydrolysis** (for high-purity grades).
6. **Ion-exchange purification** → food/pharma grade.
7. **Crystallization / spray-drying** of free acid or salts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering problem = the "gypsum problem."** A modern alternative is **electrodialysis** (in-situ lactic acid recovery from the fermentation broth) or **reactive extraction** with tri-n-octylamine in 1-octanol, which avoids the stoichiometric gypsum by-product.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Process Master-Table (KTU Viva Favourite)

| Parameter | Ethanol | Penicillin | Citric Acid | Beer | Lactic Acid |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Organism** | *S. cerevisiae* | *P. chrysogenum* | *A. niger* | *S. cerevisiae / S. pastorianus* | *L. delbrueckii* |
| **Substrate** | Molasses/glucose | CSL + lactose | Molasses/sucrose | Malt + adjuncts | Glucose/whey |
| **Mode** | Batch (continuous in SCP) | Fed-batch | Batch (SmF/SSF) | Batch | Batch/continuous |
| **T (°C)** | 30–35 | 25–27 | 28–30 | 8–22 | 45–50 |
| **pH** | 4.5–5.0 | 6.5–7.0 | < 2.0 | 4.0–4.5 | 5.5–6.5 (CaCO₃) |
| **O₂** | Microaerobic | High (30–50 %) | High (40–60 %) | Anaerobic | Anaerobic |
| **Time** | 36–72 h | 7–10 d | 5–9 d | 5–7 d + lager | 48–96 h |
| **Product Type** | Primary metabolite | Secondary metabolite | Primary metabolite | Beverage (primary) | Primary metabolite |
| **Biggest Eng. Problem** | Ethanol inhibition | O₂ demand & shear | Mycelial morphology & trace metals | Off-flavour removal | Gypsum waste & product inhibition |
