# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination** pattern. Part-A questions carry **3 marks** (≈ 50–80 word answer). Part-B questions carry **14 marks** with an internal choice (Q-A or Q-B). Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the three engineering problems that you would consider most critical during the industrial production of ethanol by fermentation. Justify each in one line.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Ethanol (product) inhibition** — concentrations >120 g·L⁻¹ stop yeast growth and fermentation, so the process must be stopped well before this ceiling (limiting productivity to ~10 % w/v substrate).
- **(ii) Maintenance of strict anaerobiosis / microaerophily** — trace O₂ is required for sterol synthesis in yeast membrane, but excess O₂ shifts metabolism to acetic acid and biomass (lowering ethanol yield).
- **(iii) Sterility of molasses and fermenter** — wild yeasts, *Acetobacter*, and lactic-acid bacteria compete for sugars and produce off-flavours; the entire USP must be sterile.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Why is penicillin produced in a fed-batch mode rather than continuous culture? Give the metabolic basis.**

**Model Answer:**
Penicillin is a **secondary metabolite** whose synthesis begins only in the *idiophase* (after trophophase growth ceases). Its biosynthesis is **repressed by glucose and high phosphate** (catabolite repression). A fed-batch mode lets the operator **limit glucose** (fed slowly to keep residual < 1 g·L⁻¹) and **maintain phosphate limitation**, thereby extending the productive stationary phase. In a continuous chemostat, the cells would be locked in balanced growth and idiophase productivity would be lost. Hence fed-batch is industrially essential.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose either Q-A or Q-B
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Q-A.** Describe the **industrial production of ethanol by fermentation from sugarcane molasses** with reference to:  
**(a)** The producing organism, raw-material composition, and biochemical reaction with theoretical yield.  
**(b)** A labelled flow diagram of the entire process, process conditions, key engineering problems, and downstream recovery up to absolute ethanol.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Producing Organism:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (a budding yeast, GRAS). A thermotolerant strain is selected for tropical Indian conditions. Cells are ellipsoidal, 5–10 μm, and reproduce by budding. *(1 mark)*

**Raw Material:** Sugarcane molasses, a viscous by-product of sugar refining containing ~50 % w/v total sugars (sucrose, glucose, fructose), 10 % ash, 15 % water, plus organic non-sugars. It is **fortified with ammonium sulphate** (nitrogen source) and **superphosphate** (phosphorus), and the pH is corrected to 4.5–5.0 with H₂SO₄. *(1 mark)*

**Biochemical Reaction:**

$$
C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Zymase enzyme complex}}\; 2\,C_{2}H_{5}OH \;+\; 2\,CO_{2} \;+\; \text{energy (2 ATP)}
$$

- The pathway proceeds through **glycolysis** (Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas) to pyruvate, then via **pyruvate decarboxylase (PDC)** to acetaldehyde, and finally **alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH)** reduces acetaldehyde to ethanol.
- **Theoretical yield (Gay-Lussac):** 0.511 g ethanol per g glucose (or 0.485 g ethanol per g sucrose after hydrolysis adjustment). *(1 mark)*
- **Industrial yield:** 90–95 % of theoretical = ~0.46–0.48 g ethanol per g glucose. *(1 mark)*

**Inoculum Development:** Working stock on agar slant → loop transfer to shake flask with sterile molasses → 18–24 h → transfer to 5 % v/v seed fermenter → 12–18 h active yeast slurry → pitch into production fermenter at 5–10 % v/v. *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Process Conditions in the Fermenter:**
- **Stirred-tank reactor**, 50,000–500,000 L working volume.
- **Temperature:** 30 ± 1 °C (cooling via jacket/coils).
- **pH:** 4.5–5.0 (online pH control with H₂SO₄/NH₄OH).
- **Dissolved O₂:** 0.5–2 % saturation (microaerobic; nitrogen sparging if needed).
- **Time:** 36–72 h.
- **Sugar:** 16–22 % w/v initial; high gravity (VHG) at 25 % w/v is challenging.
- **Agitation:** 50–200 rpm with Rushton turbines; antifoam (silicone) dosed automatically. *(1 mark)*

**Labelled Flow Diagram** (must be drawn by the student): see Section 4.1 for the Mermaid schematic; key labels are:

**USP:** Molasses receipt → clarification → fortification → sterilization (121 °C, 20 min batch, or HTST continuous) → cooling → pitching → fermentation.

**Bioreactor:** STR with pH, T, DOT, foam probes; agitator and sparger.

**DSP:** Centrifugation → yeast recovery → beer column (95 % azeotrope) → azeotropic/molecular-sieve dehydration (99.9 % absolute ethanol) → denaturation (if for industrial use).

**Spent wash:** anaerobic digestion → biogas; treated effluent discharge. *(1 mark for diagram + 1 mark for labels + 1 mark for conditions)*

**Key Engineering Problems:**
1. **Product inhibition by ethanol** → use of *S. cerevisiae* strains with higher ethanol tolerance (>15 % v/v) or **in-situ vacuum stripping / pervaporation** for continuous ethanol removal.
2. **Sterility maintenance** — open molasses handling introduces wild yeasts and bacteria; **HTST continuous sterilization** and **closed transfer lines** are essential.
3. **Osmotic stress at high sugar** → use of osmotolerant strains or fed-batch sugar addition.
4. **Heat removal** — fermentation is exothermic (ΔH ≈ -92 kJ per mol glucose); jacket cooling water at 20 °C sized by Q = U·A·ΔT_lm. *(1 mark)*

**Recovery Yield:** ~95 % of ethanol leaves the fermenter in the beer; rectification column gives 95 % azeotrope; the final 5 % water is removed by **molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite)** in modern plants, replacing carcinogenic benzene used in the old azeotropic process. *(1 mark)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Q-B.** Describe the **industrial production of penicillin (Penicillin G)** with reference to:  
**(a)** The producing organism, fermentation medium, and key biochemical steps.  
**(b)** A labelled flow diagram, process conditions, engineering problems, and downstream recovery of crystalline Penicillin G.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Producing Organism:** *Penicillium chrysogenum* (synonym *P. notatum*), a filamentous fungus that grows as branching septate hyphae. Industrial strains are **polyploid mutants** (e.g., Panlabs P-2 lineage, Wisconsin Q-176) generated by repeated **UV and N-methyl-N′-nitro-N-nitrosoguanidine (NTG) mutagenesis** and **protoplast fusion**; modern strains yield 50–80 g·L⁻¹. *(1 mark)*

**Fermentation Medium Composition (typical, g·L⁻¹):**
- **Corn-steep liquor (CSL):** 30–50 (complex N + vitamins + amino acids).
- **Lactose:** 30–60 (slow sugar; avoids glucose repression; maintains pH naturally).
- **Glucose syrup:** fed-batch, 0.5–1 g·L⁻¹ residual.
- **Phenylacetic acid (PAA):** 5–10 (side-chain precursor; gives benzyl-penicillin G).
- **KH₂PO₄:** 1–3 (kept limiting).
- **MgSO₄·7H₂O:** 0.5, **NaCl:** 1, trace FeSO₄, CuSO₄, MnSO₄. *(1 mark)*

**Biochemical Pathway (in three discrete enzymatic stages):**
1. **ACV Synthetase (ACVS)** — a single multifunctional non-ribosomal peptide synthetase catalyses the synthesis of the tripeptide **δ-(L-α-aminoadipyl)-L-cysteinyl-D-valine (ACV)** from L-α-aminoadipic acid, L-cysteine, and L-valine (with ATP hydrolysis). *(1 mark)*
2. **Isopenicillin N Synthase (IPNS)** — an oxygen- and Fe²⁺-dependent oxidase that cyclizes ACV to **isopenicillin N** (the first bioactive β-lactam ring is formed here). Requires **molecular O₂** and **ascorbate**. *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{ACV} \;+\; O_{2} \;\xrightarrow{\text{IPNS}}\; \text{Isopenicillin N} \;+\; 2\,H_{2}O
$$

3. **Isopenicillin N Acyltransferase (IAT)** — exchanges the L-α-aminoadipyl side chain for **phenylacetyl (from PAA)** to form **Penicillin G (benzyl-penicillin)**. *(1 mark)*

**Why fed-batch?** Glucose excess **represses ACVS and IPNS** transcription; thus lactose is provided as the main C-source (slowly hydrolysed by β-galactosidase to glucose) and glucose is fed-batch to maintain residual < 1 g·L⁻¹, extending the **idiophase** of penicillin synthesis. *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Process Conditions:**
- **Fermenter type:** Stirred-tank reactor (STR), 100,000 – 200,000 L working volume.
- **Temperature:** 25 – 27 °C (lower than ethanol because *Penicillium* is more heat-sensitive and penicillin is thermally labile).
- **pH:** 6.5 – 7.0 (controlled with H₂SO₄ and NaOH; falls naturally as CSL acids accumulate).
- **Dissolved O₂:** 30 – 50 % saturation; air flow 0.5 – 1.0 vvm; **k_La** must be 200 – 400 h⁻¹.
- **Agitation:** 200 – 500 rpm with **Rushton turbines**; the mycelial broth is highly viscous and **non-Newtonian (pseudoplastic)**.
- **Time:** 7 – 10 days (long batch).
- **Mode:** Fed-batch. *(1 mark)*

**Labelled Flow Diagram (Section 4.2 Mermaid):**
USP: Working stock → agar slant → spore suspension → seed fermenter 1 (40 L) → seed fermenter 2 (4000 L) → production fermenter.
DSP: Rotary vacuum drum filter → cooled cell-free broth → acidification to pH 2.0 with H₂SO₄ → counter-current extraction in **Podbielniak extractor** with **butyl acetate** (or amyl acetate) → back-extraction into aqueous phosphate buffer at pH 7.0 → activated-carbon treatment → **crystallization as K-penicillin (K-salt)** by adding K-acetate → filtration → vacuum drying → Penicillin G powder. *(2 marks for diagram + labels)*

**Engineering Problems:**
1. **Oxygen transfer is the rate-limiting step** — high mycelial viscosity lowers k_La; mechanical agitation must be balanced against hyphal shear damage.
2. **Foaming** is severe due to CSL proteins and mycelial morphology → mechanical foam breaker + silicone antifoam (polyglycol type).
3. **Catabolite repression** by glucose → strict fed-batch.
4. **Phosphate inhibition** at > 5 mM → KH₂PO₄ kept low.
5. **Acid/thermal lability** of the β-lactam ring → broth cooled to 2–5 °C immediately after harvest; recovery done within 8 h.
6. **Strain degeneration** — productivity drops after 8–10 sub-cultures; master working cell banks (MWCB) maintained cryogenically.
7. **Allergen control** — penicillin is a potent human allergen; closed reactors, dedicated pipelines, and worker PPE are mandatory. *(2 marks)*

**Yield and Quality:** Industrial titre 30 – 80 g·L⁻¹; final recovery yield 60 – 70 % of titre; purity ≥ 96 % as per USP/BP pharmacopoeia. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 2 — Industrial Processes):**
> - **Pitfall 1 — Confusing primary and secondary metabolites.** Penicillin is a **secondary metabolite** (idiophase product). Ethanol, lactic acid, and citric acid are **primary metabolites** (trophophase products). Examiners deduct 2 marks if you wrongly classify them.
> - **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting the engineering problem.** A flow diagram drawn correctly but without an accompanying list of engineering problems (sterility, O₂ transfer, heat removal, foam, inhibition) loses 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question. *Always* close your answer with an explicit "Engineering Problems" section.
> - **Pitfall 3 — Wrong reaction stoichiometry.** Students often write ethanol fermentation as "C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂" without specifying that this is the **Gay-Lussac / net reaction** of the Embden–Meyerhof pathway. State the **enzyme catalysts** (zymase, pyruvate decarboxylase, alcohol dehydrogenase) for full marks.
> - **Pitfall 4 — Citric-acid mass balance error.** The correct sucrose reaction is **C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ + 3 O₂ → 2 C₆H₈O₇ + 3 H₂O**, *not* the glucose form. Examiners test this.
> - **Pitfall 5 — Missing side-chain precursor for penicillin.** Without mentioning **phenylacetic acid (PAA)** → Penicillin G (benzyl) or phenoxyacetic acid → Penicillin V (phenoxymethyl), the answer is incomplete. The acyltransferase step is the most-tested sub-reaction.
> - **Pitfall 6 — Beer bitterness chemistry.** Students write "hops add flavour" — that is incomplete. You must say **α-acids isomerize to iso-α-acids in boiling wort**, which give bitterness and have antimicrobial activity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 2 — Industrial Bioprocess Manufacture — Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

**Universal Concepts (apply to all five products):**
- Industrial bioprocess = USP (media, inoculum, sterilization) + Bioreactor (T, pH, pO₂, agitation) + DSP (separation, purification, drying).
- **Engineering problems common to all:** sterility, heat removal, oxygen transfer (where aerobic), foam control, raw-material cost, scale-up, product inhibition, waste treatment.

**Ethanol — Quick Recall:**
- Organism: *S. cerevisiae*. Substrate: molasses. Reaction: glucose → 2 ethanol + 2 CO₂. Yield: 0.511 g·g⁻¹ theoretical. Mode: Batch. Recovery: distillation → 95 % azeotrope → molecular sieve → 99.9 %.

**Penicillin — Quick Recall:**
- Organism: *P. chrysogenum*. Substrate: CSL + lactose + PAA. Reaction: ACV → isopenicillin N (IPNS) → penicillin G (IAT). Mode: Fed-batch (7–10 d). Recovery: filtration → extraction (butyl acetate) → back-extraction → crystallization as K/Na salt. Engineering problem: **O₂ transfer & catabolite repression**.

**Citric Acid — Quick Recall:**
- Organism: *A. niger*. Substrate: molasses. Reaction: sucrose + 3 O₂ → 2 citric acid + 3 H₂O. pH < 2 (unique). Mode: Submerged batch. Recovery: Ca(OH)₂ precipitation → H₂SO₄ acidification → gypsum removal → crystallization. Engineering problem: **trace-metal control (Mn²⁺) & mycelial morphology**.

**Beer — Quick Recall:**
- Organism: *S. cerevisiae* (ale) or *S. pastorianus* (lager). Substrate: malted barley + hops. Reaction: maltose → 4 ethanol + 4 CO₂. Mode: Batch + lagering. Recovery: filtration + carbonation + pasteurization. Engineering problem: **off-flavour removal (diacetyl, DMS) and oxidation control**.

**Lactic Acid — Quick Recall:**
- Organism: *L. delbrueckii*. Substrate: glucose/whey. Reaction: glucose → 2 lactic acid. Mode: Anaerobic batch with CaCO₃. Recovery: H₂SO₄ acidification → gypsum removal → ion-exchange. Engineering problem: **gypsum waste + product inhibition**.

**Master Equations to Memorize:**
- $Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / \Delta S$; $Q_{P} = \Delta P / \Delta t$; $OTR = k_{L}a (C^{*} - C_{L})$; $\mu = \mu_{\max} S / (K_{s}+S)$.
- Gay-Lussac ethanol: $C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \to 2\,C_{2}H_{5}OH + 2\,CO_{2}$.
- Citric acid: $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + 3\,O_{2} \to 2\,C_{6}H_{8}O_{7} + 3\,H_{2}O$.
- Lactic acid: $C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \to 2\,C_{3}H_{6}O_{3}$.

**Mnemonic for Process Conditions (T, pH, O₂):**
- **E**thanol — 30 °C, pH 5, micro-O₂.
- **P**enicillin — 26 °C, pH 7, high O₂.
- **C**itric — 30 °C, pH 2, high O₂.
- **B**eer — 18 °C (ale) / 10 °C (lager), pH 4.3, no O₂.
- **L**actic — 47 °C, pH 6 (with CaCO₃), no O₂.

**Quick Engineering-Problem Cheat:**
- "**S**terility, **H**eat, **O**xygen, **F**oam, **C**ost, **I**nhibition, **S**cale-up" → acronym **SHOFCIS** — remember this in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary Metabolites - Organic Acids (Citric and Lactic Acid), Amino Acids (Glutamic Acid, Lysine), Alcohols (Ethanol, Acetone-Butanol)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Primary Metabolites - Organic Acids (Citric and Lactic Acid), Amino Acids (Glutamic Acid, Lysine), Alcohols (Ethanol, Acetone-Butanol)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Primary Metabolites?

**Primary Metabolites** are low-molecular-weight compounds that are **directly involved in the normal growth, development, and reproduction** of a microorganism. They are produced during the **trophophase** (the active growth phase) of the fermentation cycle and are essential for the cell's primary metabolic functions such as energy production, biosynthesis of macromolecules, and cellular maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Primary metabolites are the small molecules produced as intermediates or end-products of the central metabolic pathways (glycolysis, TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway) during the active growth phase. They are produced in stoichiometric amounts tied to microbial growth, unlike secondary metabolites which are produced after the growth phase has slowed down (idiophase).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Contrast (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> | Feature | Primary Metabolite | Secondary Metabolite |
> |---|---|---|
> | Production Phase | Trophophase (growth) | Idiophase (stationary) |
> | Relation to Growth | Coupled with growth | Dissociated from growth |
> | Function | Essential for survival | Ecological/Defensive |
> | Examples | Ethanol, Citric Acid, Glutamate | Penicillin, Streptomycin |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Bakery vs. The Perfume Shop

Imagine a **bakery** that runs 24/7 to make bread (essential daily product, made in **large volumes** during peak hours = **Primary Metabolite**).
Now imagine the **same bakery** has a small corner shop making specialty perfumes — only after bread orders slow down does the perfumer start crafting fragrances (luxury, small batch, post-growth = **Secondary Metabolite**).

The microbes behave exactly like this bakery:
- During the **trophophase** (rush hour), they produce **primary metabolites** like ethanol, lactic acid, and amino acids — compounds essential for their own life processes (energy, building blocks).
- During the **idiophase** (slow hours), they shift to producing **secondary metabolites** like antibiotics.

## 1.3 Classification of Primary Metabolites Covered in This Module

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups primary metabolites into three major industrial categories:

1. **Organic Acids** — Citric Acid, Lactic Acid
2. **Amino Acids** — Glutamic Acid (L-Glutamate), L-Lysine
3. **Alcohols** — Ethanol, Acetone-Butanol-Ethanol (ABE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Milestone (Examiner's Favourite):**
> The **first industrial amino acid fermentation** was L-Glutamic Acid, developed by **Kinoshita et al. (1957)** using *Corynebacterium glutamicum*. This discovery launched the entire modern amino acid industry, and glutamate (as MSG — Monosodium Glutamate) remains one of the highest-volume fermentation products in the world (over **3.4 million tonnes/year** globally).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industrial Citric Acid Production

### 2.1.1 Overview
Citric acid (2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid) is the most widely produced organic acid by fermentation (**>2 million tonnes/year**). It is a key intermediate of the **TCA (Krebs) cycle** and serves as a natural acidulant in food and beverages.

### 2.1.2 Producer Organism
- ***Aspergillus niger*** — the industrial workhorse (most common)
- Other producers: *Candida guilliermondii*, *Yarrowia lipolytica*

### 2.1.3 Industrial Production Process (Submerged Fermentation)
1. **Substrate**: Molasses (sucrose), glucose syrup, or corn steep liquor
2. **Inoculum**: Spores of *A. niger* (vegetative inoculum preferred for industrial scale)
3. **Fermentation Conditions**:
   - pH: **1.5 – 2.5** (highly acidic)
   - Temperature: **28 – 30 °C**
   - Aeration: High dissolved oxygen (O₂ critical)
   - Duration: 5 – 14 days
4. **Morphology**: Critical — **pellet form** (1–2 mm) preferred over filamentous growth for better mass transfer
5. **Recovery**: Precipitation as **calcium citrate** using Ca(OH)₂ → re-crystallization with H₂SO₄ → activated carbon treatment → crystallization

### 2.1.4 Biochemical Pathway
$$\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Glycolysis}} \text{Pyruvate} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate dehydrogenase}} \text{Acetyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{Citrate synthase}} \text{Citrate}$$

**Yield (typical industrial):** **0.8 – 0.9 kg citric acid per kg of substrate** consumed.

### 2.1.5 Why Does *A. niger* Overproduce Citrate?
- Low pH inhibits aconitase (the next enzyme in TCA), creating a **metabolic bottleneck**.
- This causes citrate to accumulate in the medium rather than being processed further.

---

## 2.2 Industrial Lactic Acid Production

### 2.2.1 Overview
Lactic acid (2-hydroxypropanoic acid) is used in food preservation, pharma, and increasingly in **biodegradable PLA (polylactic acid)** bioplastics.

### 2.2.2 Producer Organisms
| Type | Organism | Products | Industrial Use |
|------|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Homofermentative** | *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus* | Only Lactic Acid | Preferred for industrial PLA |
| **Homofermentative** | *Lactobacillus casei* | Lactic Acid | Food grade |
| **Heterofermentative** | *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* | Lactic Acid + CO₂ + Ethanol | Less preferred |

### 2.2.3 Fermentation Conditions
- pH: **5.5 – 6.5** (neutralized using CaCO₃ or NaOH)
- Temperature: **37 – 45 °C**
- Mode: Batch, fed-batch, or continuous
- Substrate: Glucose, maltose, whey permeate, molasses

### 2.2.4 Pathway
For homofermentative LAB:
$$\text{Glucose} + 2\text{ADP} + 2\text{Pi} \xrightarrow{\text{Glycolysis}} 2\text{Lactate} + 2\text{ATP}$$

**Theoretical yield: 1.0 g lactic acid per g glucose** (homofermentative pathway).

> [!NOTE]
> **Optical Isomer Distinction (Critical for KTU Exam):**
> - **L(+)-Lactic acid** → produced by *L. delbrueckii*, *L. casei* (preferred for human use)
> - **D(–)-Lactic acid** → produced by *Lactobacillus lactis*, * Sporolactobacillus*
> - **DL-racemic mix** → from some heterofermentative LAB

---

## 2.3 Industrial L-Glutamic Acid Production

### 2.3.1 Overview
L-Glutamate is the most produced amino acid in the world, primarily marketed as **MSG (Monosodium Glutamate)** — the famous "Umami" taste enhancer.

### 2.3.2 Producer Organism
- ***Corynebacterium glutamicum*** (the canonical industrial strain, Gram-positive)
- Other: *Brevibacterium flavum*, *Brevibacterium lactofermentum*

### 2.3.3 Fermentation Conditions
- pH: **7.0 – 8.0** (slightly alkaline)
- Temperature: **30 – 35 °C**
- Aeration: **High O₂ demand** (biotin-limited medium is the key trick)
- Substrate: Glucose, sucrose, molasses, or starch hydrolysate

### 2.3.4 The Biotin-Limitation Trick (Most Asked Concept)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Secret to Glutamate Overproduction:**
> When *C. glutamicum* is grown in a medium with **sub-optimal biotin** (≤ 1 µg/L), the cell wall becomes **leaky**. This allows glutamate (which is normally retained intracellularly) to **leak out** into the medium. The cell then continues producing glutamate to maintain equilibrium, leading to massive accumulation. Adding **Tween-40 (a fatty acid ester)** or **penicillin** can also induce this leakiness — this is the industrial "trick" to convert a tightly regulated intracellular product into an extracellular one.

### 2.3.5 Pathway
$$\alpha\text{-ketoglutarate} + \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{NADPH} \xrightarrow{\text{Glutamate dehydrogenase}} \text{L-Glutamate}$$

This reaction occurs in the **TCA cycle** branch point, where α-ketoglutarate is the immediate precursor.

**Yield: ~0.5 g glutamate per g glucose** (industrial).

---

## 2.4 Industrial L-Lysine Production

### 2.4.1 Overview
L-Lysine is the **second most-produced amino acid** globally, used primarily as a **feed supplement** for swine and poultry (it is the first limiting amino acid in cereal-based diets).

### 2.4.2 Producer Organism
- ***Corynebacterium glutamicum*** (auxotrophic mutants)
- Key mutations:
  - **Hom⁻ (homoserine auxotroph)** — blocks the threonine/methionine branch
  - **Leu⁻** — blocks leucine branch
  - **AEC-resistant** (S-(2-aminoethyl)-L-cysteine resistant) — feedback-resistant aspartokinase

### 2.4.3 Pathway
Lysine is synthesized via the **Aspartate (DAP/Aspartokinase) Pathway**:
$$\text{Aspartate} \xrightarrow{\text{Aspartokinase}} \beta\text{-Aspartyl-P} \rightarrow \text{Asparagine semialdehyde} \rightarrow \text{Lysine}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Branch Point Concept (High-Yield for KTU):**
> Aspartate semialdehyde is the **central branch point** — it can be channelled into:
> 1. **Lysine branch** (toward DAP → Lysine)
> 2. **Threonine/Methionine branch** (toward Homoserine)
> 3. **Isoleucine branch** (from threonine)
>
> Auxotrophic mutations (Homo- and Thr-) **block the competing branches**, forcing carbon flux toward lysine — this is metabolic engineering at its classical best.

### 2.4.4 Fermentation Conditions
- pH: **6.5 – 7.5**
- Temperature: **30 °C**
- Mode: Fed-batch with **excess threonine + methionine limitation** to relieve feedback inhibition
- Yield: **0.4 – 0.5 g/g glucose**

---

## 2.5 Industrial Ethanol Production

### 2.5.1 Overview
Ethanol is the **largest-volume industrial fermentation product** worldwide, used in beverages, pharma, solvents, and increasingly as a **biofuel (gasoline additive)**.

### 2.5.2 Producer Organism
- ***Saccharomyces cerevisiae*** — primary industrial yeast
- *Zymomonas mobilis* — alternative bacterium (higher specific productivity, lower ethanol tolerance)
- *Schizosaccharomyces pombe* — for some African traditional beverages

### 2.5.3 Fermentation Conditions
- pH: **4.0 – 5.0**
- Temperature: **30 – 35 °C** (mesophilic)
- Substrate:
  - **First generation**: Sugarcane molasses, sugar beet (Brazil, India)
  - **Second generation**: Lignocellulosic biomass (corn stover, bagasse)
- Anaerobic / micro-aerobic conditions

### 2.5.4 Pathway
$$\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Embden-Meyerhof}} 2\text{Pyruvate} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate decarboxylase}} 2\text{Acetaldehyde} \xrightarrow{\text{Alcohol dehydrogenase}} 2\text{Ethanol} + 2\text{CO}_2$$

### 2.5.5 Stoichiometry and Yields
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$$

- **Theoretical yield: 0.51 g ethanol per g glucose** (this is the maximum based on Gay-Lussac stoichiometry)
- Industrial yield: **0.40 – 0.48 g/g** (90–95% of theoretical)
- **Pasteur Effect**: Presence of O₂ suppresses fermentation; anaerobic conditions favour ethanol production

---

## 2.6 Industrial Acetone-Butanol-Ethanol (ABE) Production

### 2.6.1 Overview
The ABE process is the **oldest industrial fermentation** using a strict anaerobe. It produces three solvents in a fixed ratio: **Acetone : Butanol : Ethanol = 3 : 6 : 1** (by mass).

### 2.6.2 Producer Organism
- ***Clostridium acetobutylicum*** — Gram-positive, endospore-forming, **strict anaerobe**
- *Clostridium beijerinckii* — alternative industrial strain
- Historical note: Process developed by **Chaim Weizmann (1910–1914)** in Manchester, UK

### 2.6.3 Fermentation Phases (Two Distinct Phases — Critical for KTU)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two-Phase ABE Fermentation:**
>
> | Phase | pH Trend | Dominant Products | Duration |
> |-------|----------|-------------------|----------|
> | **Acidogenesis Phase** | pH drops from 6.0 → 4.5 | Acetic acid, Butyric acid, H₂, CO₂ | 24–36 h |
> | **Solventogenesis Phase** | pH stabilizes at 4.5–5.0 | Acetone, Butanol, Ethanol | 36–72 h |
>
> **The pH switch is the key metabolic trigger** — when acids accumulate, pH falls, and a global regulatory shift activates solvent-producing enzymes.

### 2.6.4 Pathway (Clostridial)
$$\text{Glucose} \rightarrow \text{Pyruvate} \rightarrow \text{Acetyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{Thiolase}} \text{Acetoacetyl-CoA} \rightarrow \text{Butyryl-CoA} \rightarrow \text{Butanol}$$

The **solventogenic shift** is associated with the induction of key enzymes:
- Acetoacetate decarboxylase (Adc)
- CoA transferase (CtfA/B)
- Aldehyde/alcohol dehydrogenases

### 2.6.5 Product Toxicity — The Bottleneck
> [!WARNING]
> **Butanol Toxicity Problem:**
> Butanol is **highly toxic to C. acetobutylicum** at concentrations above **~13 g/L** (1.3% w/v). This toxicity is the **single biggest bottleneck** of the ABE industry. Modern strategies include:
> 1. **In situ product recovery (ISPR)** — gas stripping, pervaporation
> 2. **Tolerant strain engineering** — using *C. beijerinckii* BA101
> 3. **Fed-batch cultivation** — keeping butanol below inhibitory levels

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations most frequently asked in KTU board exams.**

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Gay-Lussac (Theoretical Ethanol Yield) | $Y_{E/S} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.511$ | g ethanol per g glucose |
| Citric Acid Stoichiometry | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 1.5 O_2 \rightarrow C_6H_8O_7 + 2 H_2O$ | Mass yield: 0.81 g/g |
| Lactic Acid Stoichiometry (Homo) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2 \, CH_3CHOHCOOH$ | Theoretical: 1.0 g/g |
| Glutamate Stoichiometry | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + NH_3 \rightarrow C_5H_9NO_4 + CO_2$ | Yield: 0.50 g/g |
| Product Yield Coefficient ($Y_{P/S}$) | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{S_i - S_f}$ | g product per g substrate |
| Volumetric Productivity | $Q_P = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{t}$ | g/L·h |
| Specific Productivity | $q_P = \dfrac{Q_P}{X}$ | g/g·h (X = biomass) |
| Growth Yield ($Y_{X/S}$) | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g cells per g substrate |
| Doubling Time | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}}$ | hours |
| Specific Growth Rate | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_S + S}$ (Monod) | h⁻¹ |
| Stoichiometric ABE Ratio (mass) | Acetone : Butanol : Ethanol = $3 : 6 : 1$ | Total ~ 20 g/L |
| Mass Balance (closed fermenter) | $\Delta S_{consumed} = \Delta X \cdot Y_{XS}^{-1} + \Delta P \cdot Y_{PS}^{-1} + \Delta S_{maintenance}$ | g/L |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Metabolite | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Food & Beverage | Citric acid, Lactic acid, Ethanol, MSG | Acidulants, preservatives, beverages |
| Pharmaceutical | Lactic acid, Glutamate, Lysine | Solvents, IV solutions, supplements |
| Bioplastics | Lactic acid (L-isomer) | PLA — biodegradable plastic |
| Animal Feed | L-Lysine, L-Threonine | Swine/poultry feed supplementation |
| Biofuel | Ethanol | E10, E85 gasoline blends |
| Cosmetics | Lactic acid, Citric acid | pH adjusters, AHAs in skin care |
| Industrial Solvents | Acetone, Butanol | Paints, lacquers, explosives (historical) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac Equation)

We start with the **balanced stoichiometric reaction** for yeast fermentation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2 \, C_2H_5OH + 2 \, CO_2$$

**Step 1 — Identify molecular masses.**

$$M_{glucose} = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = 180 \, g/mol$$

$$M_{ethanol} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 24 + 6 + 16 = 46 \, g/mol$$

$$M_{CO_2} = 1(12) + 2(16) = 12 + 32 = 44 \, g/mol$$

**Step 2 — From the stoichiometry, 1 mole of glucose produces 2 moles of ethanol.**

$$n_{ethanol} = 2 \times n_{glucose}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of ethanol produced from 180 g of glucose.**

$$m_{ethanol} = 2 \times 46 = 92 \, g$$

**Step 4 — Compute the theoretical mass yield coefficient.**

$$Y_{E/S} = \dfrac{m_{ethanol}}{m_{glucose}} = \dfrac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \, g/g$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{Y_{E/S,\,theoretical} = 0.511 \, \text{g ethanol per g glucose}}$$

**Step 5 — Industrial efficiency calculation example.**
If an industrial plant produces **46 g/L ethanol** from **100 g/L glucose** initially (with 6 g/L residual):

$$Y_{E/S,\,actual} = \dfrac{46}{100 - 6} = \dfrac{46}{94} = 0.4894 \, g/g$$

$$\eta_{process} = \dfrac{0.4894}{0.5111} \times 100\% = 95.7\%$$

This matches the typical **industrial ethanol fermentation efficiency of 90–95%** of theoretical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Citric Acid Theoretical Yield from Glucose

Balanced reaction:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 1.5 \, O_2 \longrightarrow C_6H_8O_7 + 2 \, H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Molecular masses.**

$$M_{citric\,acid} = 6(12) + 8(1) + 7(16) = 72 + 8 + 112 = 192 \, g/mol$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric yield.**

$$Y_{C/S} = \dfrac{1 \times 192}{1 \times 180} = 1.067 \, g/g$$

**Step 3 — Apply maintenance and growth correction.**
In practice, *A. niger* uses some glucose for cell mass and maintenance:

$$Y_{C/S,\,actual} = Y_{C/S,\,theoretical} \times \eta \approx 0.81 \, g/g$$

where $\eta \approx 0.75$ accounts for biomass synthesis, CO₂ loss via TCA, and oxalate byproduct formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the actual yield *lower* than theoretical?**
> *A. niger* has a tendency to produce **oxalic acid** (an unwanted byproduct) especially under unfavourable conditions (e.g., high Mn²⁺, trace metal imbalance). This diverts carbon away from citrate and is a key process-control concern.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: L-Lysine Yield Using Corynebacterium

**Step 1 — Pathway mass balance (simplified):**
$$1 \text{ Glucose} + 2 \text{ NH}_3 + 4 \text{ NADPH} \rightarrow 1 \text{ Lysine} + 2 \text{ CO}_2 + 4 \text{ NADP}^+$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance (per mole of glucose).**

$$M_{lysine} = 6(12) + 14(1) + 2(14) + 2(16) = 146 \, g/mol$$

$$Y_{Lys/S,\,theoretical} = \dfrac{146}{180} = 0.811 \, g/g$$

**Step 3 — Industrial reality.**
Carbon is also lost to biomass, CO₂, and other byproducts (e.g., trehalose). The **auxotrophic feedback-resistant mutants** achieve:

$$Y_{Lys/S,\,industrial} = 0.40 - 0.50 \, g/g$$

$$\eta_{lysine} \approx \dfrac{0.45}{0.811} \approx 55\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is lysine yield so low?**
> The aspartate pathway has **two major competing branches** (threonine/methionine and isoleucine). Even with mutations, some carbon leaks into these branches. Modern systems biology and metabolic engineering have pushed yields toward 0.55 g/g in optimized strains.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: ABE Fermentation Mass Balance

**Step 1 — Overall stoichiometry (per 100 g glucose consumed):**
The standard ABE fermentation yields approximately:

$$\text{Glucose} \rightarrow 30 \, g \, \text{Butanol} + 15 \, g \, \text{Acetone} + 5 \, g \, \text{Ethanol} + 50 \, g \, \text{CO}_2 + \text{biomass}$$

Wait — but the **classical ABE ratio** is **3 : 6 : 1 (A : B : E)**. Let us normalize.

If total solvents = 20 g/L, then:
- Acetone = $3/10 \times 20 = 6 \, g/L$
- Butanol = $6/10 \times 20 = 12 \, g/L$
- Ethanol = $1/10 \times 20 = 2 \, g/L$

**Step 2 — Glucose requirement.**

For 12 g/L butanol, the theoretical stoichiometry is:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow C_4H_9OH + 2 \, CO_2 + H_2$$

$$Y_{B/S,\,theoretical} = \dfrac{74}{180} = 0.411 \, g/g$$

**Step 3 — Actual industrial yield.**

$$Y_{B/S,\,actual} = \dfrac{12}{60} = 0.20 \, g/g \quad (\text{when } S_{i} = 60 \, g/L, S_{f} = 0)$$

$$\eta_{ABE} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.411} \approx 48.7\%$$

This is much lower than ethanol because of butanol toxicity and metabolic complexity.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of All Six Fermentations

| Feature | Citric Acid | Lactic Acid | Glutamic Acid | Lysine | Ethanol | ABE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Organism** | *A. niger* | *Lb. delbrueckii* | *C. glutamicum* | *C. glutamicum* (mutant) | *S. cerevisiae* | *C. acetobutylicum* |
| **Type** | Mold | Bacterium | Bacterium | Bacterium | Yeast | Strict anaerobe |
| **Substrate** | Molasses | Glucose/Whey | Glucose | Glucose | Sugarcane/Corn | Starch/Molasses |
| **T (°C)** | 28–30 | 37–45 | 30–35 | 30 | 30–35 | 30–37 |
| **pH** | 1.5–2.5 | 5.5–6.5 | 7.0–8.0 | 6.5–7.5 | 4.0–5.0 | 4.5–6.0 (drops) |
| **Aeration** | Aerobic | Microaerophilic | Aerobic | Aerobic | Microaerophilic | Strict anaerobic |
| **Theoretical Yield (g/g)** | 1.07 | 1.00 | 0.50 | 0.81 | 0.511 | 0.41 (butanol) |
| **Industrial Yield (g/g)** | 0.80–0.90 | 0.90–0.95 | 0.45–0.50 | 0.40–0.50 | 0.40–0.48 | 0.20 (butanol) |
| **Mode** | Batch/SSF | Batch/Fed-batch | Fed-batch | Fed-batch | Batch/Continuous | Batch/Fed-batch |
| **Recovery** | Ca-citrate ppt. | Ca-lactate ppt. | Crystallization | Ion exchange | Distillation | Distillation |
| **Key Trick** | Low pH blocks aconitase | — | Biotin limitation | Auxotrophy | Pasteur effect | pH switch to solventogenesis |
| **Discovery Year** | 1917 (Currie) | 1880s | 1957 (Kinoshita) | 1958 (Kinoshita) | Prehistoric | 1910s (Weizmann) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Calculating Volumetric Productivity

A 50,000 L fermenter is used to produce glutamic acid with *C. glutamicum*.
- Initial glutamate concentration: $P_i = 0 \, g/L$
- Final glutamate concentration: $P_f = 92 \, g/L$
- Fermentation time: $t = 60 \, h$

**Step 1 — Calculate the final mass of glutamate.**

$$m_P = 92 \, g/L \times 50{,}000 \, L = 4{,}600{,}000 \, g = 4{,}600 \, kg$$

**Step 2 — Calculate volumetric productivity.**

$$Q_P = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{t} = \dfrac{92 - 0}{60} = 1.533 \, g/L \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Total throughput in tonnes/year (assuming 300 working days).**

$$M_{annual} = 4{,}600 \, kg \times \dfrac{300 \times 24}{60} = 4{,}600 \times 120 = 552{,}000 \, kg = 552 \, \text{tonnes/year}$$

This single fermenter yields ~552 tonnes of MSG per year — illustrating the **scale of industrial amino acid fermentation**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Fermentation Process Flow for Primary Metabolites

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SUBSTRATE_PREP ["SUBSTRATE PREPARATION UNIT"]
        raw[Raw Material e.g. Molasses or Glucose]
        stch[Starch Hydrolysis if needed]
        ntr[Nutrient Addition CSL, Vitamins]
        raw --> stch --> ntr
    end
```
