Proteins)) Therapeutics Insulin Erythropoietin mAbs Growth Hormone tPA Interferons Coagulation Factors Vaccines Hepatitis B HBsAg HPV VLPs Influenza HA Industrial Enzymes Detergents Subtilisin Starch Amylase Biofuel Cellulase Cheese Chymosin Textile Pectinase Diagnostics Streptavidin HRP Luciferase GFP variants Agriculture Bt Cry toxins Snowmax Antifungal chitinase Specialty Spider silk Recombinant collagen Transgenic antibodies in goat milk ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Each Part A question below is to be answered in **~1.5 pages (~150 words)**. Model answers follow KTU valuation conventions: definition → explanation → example/figure. --- ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Modeled)] > **"List any three major therapeutic proteins produced by recombinant DNA technology. State the host organism used for each."** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > 1. **Recombinant Human Insulin** — Host: *Escherichia coli* (specifically the K-12 strain). Used for Type 1 and advanced Type 2 diabetes mellitus; produced as proinsulin, enzymatically cleaved to mature insulin. > > 2. **Recombinant Erythropoietin (EPO)** — Host: **Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cells**. Glycosylated form is mandatory for in vivo half-life; used in anemia associated with chronic kidney disease and chemotherapy. > > 3. **Recombinant Hepatitis B Surface Antigen (HBsAg)** — Host: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (baker's yeast). Used as the first recombinant subunit vaccine; expressed as 22-nm virus-like particles. --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modeled)] > **"Why is *E. coli* the preferred host for industrial enzyme production rather than mammalian cells?"** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > *E. coli* is preferred for industrial enzymes due to: > 1. **Low cost** — Simple defined media (glucose + salts) compared to expensive DMEM/FBS required for mammalian cells. > 2. **High growth rate** — Doubling time ~20 min vs. ~24 h for CHO cells. > 3. **Scalability** — Established high-cell-density fermentation (>100 g/L DCW) protocols. > 4. **Genetic tractability** — Well-characterized plasmids (pET, pBAD), strong T7 promoter system. > 5. **Industrial enzymes do not require human PTMs** — Activity, thermostability, and substrate specificity are the only criteria, eliminating the need for glycosylation. > > Example: Subtilisin for detergent applications is produced in *B. licheniformis* or *B. subtilis* at >10 g/L titer. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Structure:** Part B questions of 14 marks usually have sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**, OR are structured as comprehensive essays with subsections. Internal choice is provided between two questions (OR option). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] > **(a)** Describe in detail the **production of recombinant human insulin** in *E. coli*, covering the cloning strategy, fermentation, and downstream processing. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Compare the **expression systems** of *E. coli*, yeast, and CHO cells for therapeutic protein production. Tabulate the differences and justify why CHO is the dominant host for monoclonal antibodies. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Answer for (a):** > [!NOTE] > **1. Cloning Strategy (3 marks)** > - The human insulin gene is a 51-residue heterodimer: **A-chain (21 aa)** and **B-chain (30 aa)** connected by **C-peptide** (31 aa, excised in vivo). > - The **full-length proinsulin cDNA** was chemically synthesized and cloned into the **pBR322 plasmid** under the **trp (tryptophan) promoter**. > - Eli Lilly's process used **separate expression** of A and B chains in two *E. coli* strains; chains were purified separately and recombined in vitro via **disulfide reshuffling**. > - Modern process: proinsulin is expressed as a single polypeptide with a **trypsin cleavage site**, processed enzymatically in vitro. > **2. Fermentation (2 marks)** > - High-cell-density fed-batch fermentation in **defined media** with glucose feeding. > - Trp promoter induced by **tryptophan starvation**. > - Inclusion bodies form intracellularly — recovered by **centrifugation after cell disruption** (French press/lysozyme). > **3. Downstream Processing (2 marks)** > - Solubilization of inclusion bodies in **8 M urea + β-mercaptoethanol** (denaturation + reduction). > - **In vitro refolding** by dilution into redox buffer (GSH/GSSG). > - **Trypsin digestion** to cleave C-peptide. > - **Reverse-phase HPLC** polishing; formulation as **Zn-insulin hexamer** for subcutaneous depot effect. #### **Model Answer for (b):** > **Comparison Table (4 marks)** | Feature | *E. coli* | Yeast | CHO Cells | |---------|-----------|-------|-----------| | **Doubling Time** | 20–30 min | 90 min | 18–24 h | | **Cost/L** | **Low (USD 2–5)** | Low (USD 5–10) | High (USD 30–100) | | **Glycosylation** | None | High-mannose (immunogenic) | **Human-like, sialylated** | | **Titer (typical)** | 1–5 g/L | 1–10 g/L | 1–8 g/L | | **Scale** | >100,000 L | >50,000 L | <25,000 L | | **Regulatory Track Record** | Well-known | Good | **Dominant (mAbs)** | | **Best For** | Insulin, IFN-α, GH | HBsAg, HSA | mAbs, EPO, tPA | > **Justification (3 marks):** > CHO cells dominate mAb production (>70% of approved mAbs) because: > 1. **Human-compatible N-glycosylation** — critical for Fc-receptor binding, antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), and complement activation. > 2. **High volumetric productivity** in optimized fed-batch (5–8 g/L). > 3. **Regulatory familiarity** — FDA-approved CHO-derived products since 1986 (tPA). > 4. **Robustness** in chemically defined, serum-free suspension culture. > 5. **Stable genomic integration** of transgenes via DHFR/GS selection systems. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model] > **(a)** Discuss the **industrial applications of recombinant enzymes** in the detergent, food, and textile industries, with specific examples. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **role of recombinant proteins in vaccine production**. Describe in detail the production of the recombinant Hepatitis B vaccine. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Answer for (a):** > **1. Detergent Industry (2.5 marks)** > - **Subtilisin Carlsberg** (alkaline serine protease) from *B. licheniformis*, cloned into *B. subtilis* for overproduction. > - Engineered to be **bleach-stable** (Met residue replaced), **thermostable**, and active in alkaline pH (9–11) and cold water (30 °C). > - Used in **Tide®, Ariel®** at 0.5–2% w/w; **global market >USD 2 billion/year**. > - Recombinant **lipases** (e.g., from *Thermomyces lanuginosus*) complement for fat removal. > **2. Food Industry (2.5 marks)** > - **Chymosin (rennet)** from *Rhizomucor miehei* or calf stomach: **first food enzyme produced recombinantly (1990)**; >50% of global cheese production now uses recombinant chymosin (e.g., **Chy-Max®**). > - Eliminates calf-slaughtering dependency and religious (Kosher, Halal) concerns. > - **α-Amylase** in starch processing, brewing (dextrose equivalent control). > - **Pectinase** in fruit juice clarification (apple, grape). > - **Lactase** in lactose-hydrolyzed milk and ice cream (Lactaid®). > **3. Textile Industry (2 marks)** > - **Cellulase** (e.g., endoglucanase from *Trichoderma reesei*) — "biopolishing" of cotton, denim finishing (stone-wash effect without pumice stones). > - **Laccase** — bleach clean-up, decolorization. > - **Pectate lyase** — bioscouring of cotton replacing harsh NaOH. #### **Model Answer for (b):** > **1. Recombinant Vaccines — Concept (2 marks)** > Recombinant subunit vaccines use **cloned pathogen antigens** expressed in heterologous hosts. They are **safer** than live-attenuated vaccines (no risk of reversion to virulence) and **scalable** in microbial fermentation. > Examples: HBsAg (Hepatitis B), HPV L1 VLP (cervical cancer), recombinant HA (influenza — Flublok®). > **2. Hepatitis B Vaccine Production (5 marks)** | Step | Details | |------|---------| | **Gene** | Surface antigen gene (S gene, 226 aa) from HBV | | **Vector** | Plasmid with **ADH1 promoter** for yeast expression | | **Host** | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (Merck's Recombivax HB®) or *Hansenula polymorpha* (GSK's Engerix-B®) | | **Fermentation** | Fed-batch in defined media, 30 °C, pH 5.0, ~72 h | | **Harvest** | Cell disruption (bead mill) → membrane filtration | | **Purification** | Multiple chromatographic steps: IEX, HIC, SEC, immunoaffinity | | **Formulation** | Adsorption onto **aluminum hydroxide adjuvant** (0.5 mg Al/dose) | | **Dose** | 10–20 µg HBsAg per adult dose, 3 doses at 0, 1, 6 months | | **Efficacy** | **>95% seroconversion** in healthy adults | > **Mechanism:** HBsAg self-assembles into **22-nm virus-like particles (VLPs)** that mimic the virus surface but contain **no genetic material**, making them non-infectious yet highly immunogenic. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (2023–2024 KTU Evaluator Feedback):** > > 1. **Confusing "expression vector" with "cloning vector."** Expression vectors **must** have a promoter + RBS + start codon. Cloning vectors need only an MCS (multiple cloning site). Examiners deduct 1 mark for using these terms interchangeably. > > 2. **Stating "yeast performs glycosylation" without specifying "high-mannose type."** The glycan structure matters — high-mannose glycans are **immunogenic in humans**, a critical drawback addressed only by *P. pastoris* with humanized glycosylation mutants (GlycoFi technology). > > 3. **Writing "Insulin is produced in yeast"** — this is a frequently-incorrect statement. The original Humulin® and all approved recombinant human insulin today (rapid-acting analogs) use *E. coli* or *S. cerevisiae* with subsequent enzymatic modification. **Always specify the host correctly.** > > 4. **Skipping downstream processing in production questions.** ESE answers on insulin/EPO/mAbs that omit the **chromatography purification step** lose ~2–3 marks even if upstream is correct. > > 5. **Forgetting the "Why not E. coli" for EPO question.** Always cite **glycosylation → in vivo half-life → receptor binding**. Single-cause answers receive only partial credit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision notes for last-day exam preparation:** ### **Core Concepts** - ☐ **Recombinant protein** = protein produced from a cloned gene in a heterologous host. - ☐ **First recombinant drug** = Human Insulin (Humulin®, 1982, Eli Lilly, *E. coli*). - ☐ **First recombinant vaccine** = Hepatitis B (HBsAg in yeast, 1986). - ☐ **First recombinant food enzyme** = Chymosin (1990, *K. lactis* or *E. coli*). ### **Host Selection Rule** - ☐ **Need glycosylation?** → CHO (mammalian) or engineered *P. pastoris*. - ☐ **No PTMs, high yield needed?** → *E. coli* (insulin, GH, IFN). - ☐ **Cheap eukaryotic expression?** → *S. cerevisiae* (HBsAg). - ☐ **Very high yield + secretion?** → *P. pastoris* (HSA). - ☐ **Native complex folding?** → Transgenic animals (ATryn®). ### **Therapeutic Blockbusters to Memorize** - ☐ **Insulin** — *E. coli*, diabetes. - ☐ **EPO (Epogen®)** — CHO, anemia. - ☐ **tPA (Activase®)** — CHO, stroke. - ☐ **mAb (Humira®)** — CHO, autoimmune diseases. - ☐ **Factor VIII (Recombinate®)** — CHO, hemophilia A. - ☐ **HBsAg (Engerix-B®, Recombivax®)** — yeast, hepatitis B. - ☐ **Darbepoetin (Aranesp®)** — hyper-glycosylated EPO analog (2 extra N-glycans, 3× longer half-life). - ☐ **Somatotropin (Genotropin®)** — *E. coli*, growth hormone deficiency. ### **Industrial Enzymes to Memorize** - ☐ **Subtilisin** → Detergents (*B. licheniformis* in *B. subtilis*). - ☐ **Chymosin** → Cheese (*R. miehei* in *A. niger* or *K. lactis*). - ☐ **α-Amylase** → Starch (*B. amyloliquefaciens* in *B. subtilis*). - ☐ **Cellulase** → Bioethanol, textile (*T. reesei*). - ☐ **Phytase** → Animal feed (*A. niger*). ### **Key Quantitative Anchors** - ☐ Insulin titer in *E. coli*: ~1–2 g/L. - ☐ mAb titer in CHO fed-batch: **3–8 g/L** (modern processes). - ☐ HSA in *P. pastoris*: up to **10 g/L**. - ☐ Global mAb market: >USD 150 billion (2023). - ☐ Recombinant enzymes = **75% of global enzyme market**. ### **Conceptual “Why” Chains** - ☐ *E. coli* produces **inclusion bodies** → require denaturation + refolding. - ☐ EPO **without glycosylation** is clinically useless (100× shorter half-life). - ☐ Yeast glycans are **high-mannose** → cleared by hepatic ASGPR receptors. - ☐ Recombinant Factor VIII **eliminated HIV transmission risk** in hemophiliacs (post-1992). ### **Crystallographic/Quality Facts** - ☐ Inclusion body recovery: **30–70%** of total cellular protein. - ☐ Refolding efficiency: typically **5–40%**. - ☐ Overall process yield: **20–50%** for full DSP. - ☐ Bioreactor working volume: **70–85%** of total volume. ### **Regulatory & Historical** - ☐ **1982** — Humulin approved. - ☐ **1986** — Recombivax HB (Hep B vaccine) approved. - ☐ **1987** — Activase (tPA) approved — first CHO-derived therapeutic. - ☐ **1998** — Herceptin (trastuzumab) launched. - ☐ **2010** — Lucentis (ranibizumab) — first mAb fragment (Fab). - ☐ All recombinant biologics must follow **cGMP, ICH Q5A–Q5D** guidelines. ### **Common Confusions to Avoid** - ☐ **mRNA ≠ recombinant protein.** mRNA vaccines (e.g., COVID-19) deliver gene transcripts; recombinant protein vaccines (e.g., Hep B) deliver the antigen itself. - ☐ **Monoclonal antibody vs. polyclonal antibody** — recombinant mAbs are defined, single-epitope; polyclonals are mixtures. - ☐ **CHO is not the only mammalian host.** HEK293, NS0, SP2/0 also used, but CHO dominates. > **Final exam mantra:** *"For every recombinant therapeutic question — STATE THE HOST, EXPLAIN THE PTM REQUIREMENT, AND DESCRIBE THE PURIFICATION."* This single sentence covers ~70% of KTU valuation expectations for this topic.

topics.title: Production of Xanthan Gum and Nisin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production of Xanthan Gum and Nisin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Xanthan Gum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Xanthan Gum** is a high-molecular-weight, anionic, branched heteropolysaccharide (exopolysaccharide, EPS) produced extracellularly by the aerobic Gram-negative bacterium *Xanthomonas campestris* through submerged fermentation. It consists of a cellulose-like β-(1→4)-D-glucose backbone with trisaccharide side chains containing D-mannose and D-glucuronic acid residues, often partially acetylated and pyruvylated.

**Key Identity Card:**
| Property | Value |
|---|---:|
| **Chemical Class** | Heteropolysaccharide (EPS) |
| **Producing Organism** | *Xanthomonas campestris* |
| **Primary Carbon Source** | D-Glucose, Sucrose, Starch Hydrolysate |
| **Typical Yield** | **20–30 g/L** (industrial) |
| **Viscosity (1% solution)** | **~3000 cP** |
| **E-Number (Food)** | E415 |
| **Global Production (2023)** | > 1,00,000 tonnes/year |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Molecular Sponge":**
> Imagine a single strand of spaghetti (the cellulose backbone) with tiny branched "trees" (the trisaccharide side chains) sticking out. When you drop this into water, the side chains grab water molecules and the chains tangle with each other — like a bowl of noodles where every noodle is grabbing its neighbor with velcro hooks. **Even a tiny amount (0.1–0.5%) turns water into a thick, pourable gel.** This is why xanthan gum is the "secret weapon" in salad dressings, toothpaste, ice cream, and even oil-drilling mud — it makes things *viscous* without changing their flavor.

---

## 1.2 Nisin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Nisin** is a **Class I lantibiotic** (lanthipeptide bacteriocin) — a polycyclic antibacterial peptide of **34 amino acid residues** (M.W. ≈ **3354 Da**) produced naturally by certain strains of *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis*. It contains the unusual thioether amino acids **lanthionine** and **methyl-lanthionine** and is primarily active against Gram-positive bacteria, including spore-formers.

**Key Identity Card:**
| Property | Value |
|---|---:|
| **Chemical Class** | Lantibiotic (Class I Bacteriocin) |
| **Producing Organism** | *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* |
| **Amino Acid Residues** | **34** |
| **Molecular Weight** | **3,354 Da** |
| **Isoelectric Point (pI)** | **~8.86** |
| **E-Number (Food)** | E234 |
| **Common Variants** | **Nisin A** and **Nisin Z** |
| **Regulatory Status** | **GRAS** (FDA, 1988) |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Key for Bacteria":**
> Picture a microscopic key made of 34 beads in a special pattern. This key only fits into locks (the **Lipid II** molecule) found on the surface of Gram-positive bacteria. Once inserted, the key drills a hole in the bacterial membrane — the bacterium **leaks its guts out** and dies. The beauty: this key is **completely harmless to humans** because human cells don't have Lipid II locks. This is why nisin sits in your cheese, your canned soup, and your beer — silently guarding them from spoilage bacteria like *Clostridium botulinum* (the botulism culprit).

---

## 1.3 Why These Two? — KTU Syllabus Significance

> [!NOTE]
> These two bioproducts represent the **two major pillars** of modern food/pharma bioprocess technology:
> 1. **Xanthan Gum** → *Polysaccharide Biopolymer* (structure-function exopolysaccharide)
> 2. **Nisin** → *Antimicrobial Peptide* (Class I bacteriocin / lantibiotic)
>
> Together they cover **Gram-negative aerobic fermentation** and **Gram-positive lactic acid fermentation** — the two industrially dominant bioprocess archetypes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Xanthan Gum — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Biosynthesis Pathway (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Xanthan biosynthesis is one of the most **energy-expensive** microbial polymerizations known. The pathway follows **5 key stages:**

1. **Sugar Uptake & Phosphorylation**
   - Glucose enters the cell via the **Entner–Doudoroff (ED) pathway** (not EMP).
   - Glucose → **Glucose-6-P** → **6-Phosphogluconate** → Pyruvate + Glyceraldehyde-3-P.
   - This ED pathway is critical — blocking it kills xanthan production.

2. **Nucleotide-Sugar Precursor Formation**
   - Glucose-6-P → **UDP-Glucose** (via UDP-Glc pyrophosphorylase)
   - UDP-Glucose → **UDP-Galactose** (via UDP-galactose-4-epimerase)
   - UDP-Glucose → **dTDP-Rhamnose** (via dTDP-glucose-4,6-dehydratase)
   - Glucose-6-P → **GDP-Mannose** (via phosphomannose isomerase + GDP-mannose pyrophosphorylase)
   - UDP-Glucose → **UDP-Glucuronic Acid** (via UDP-glucose dehydrogenase)

3. **Acetylation & Pyruvylation (Modification Step)**
   - Acetyl-CoA + Mannose → **O-Acetyl-Mannose** (by acetyltransferase)
   - Phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) + Mannose → **Pyruvyl-Mannose** (by pyruvyltransferase)

4. **Polymerization & Export**
   - **Acyl carrier protein (ACP)** acts as an "assembly line scaffold."
   - Sequential glycosyltransferases (called **GumD, GumE, GumH, GumI, GumJ, GumK, GumL, GumM**) attach sugars.
   - The growing polymer is **exported simultaneously** via an **ABC transporter / Wzx-Wzy-like system**.

5. **Extracellular Assembly & Release**
   - The mature pentasaccharide repeat unit is:
     $$\text{Glc}-\beta(1,4)-\text{Glc}-\beta(1,4)-\text{Glc}-\underbrace{\overbrace{\text{Man-(Ac)}}^{side chain}}_{\text{branch on every alternate Glc}}-\beta(1,3)-\text{GlcA}-\beta(1,2)-\text{Man-(Pyr)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it Matters:** The *X. campestris* gum gene cluster (**gum B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, L, M**) spans **~16 kb** and is one of the largest known operons for EPS production — making it a **favourite cloning target** in metabolic engineering.

### 2.1.2 Fermentation Process Engineering

| Parameter | Optimal Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Fermentation Type** | Submerged, **Aerobic**, Batch / Fed-batch | High O₂ demand → must control DOT > 30% |
| **Temperature** | **28–30 °C** | Above 35 °C → reduced yield, cell lysis |
| **pH** | **6.8–7.2** (controlled) | Below 5 → EPS degradation by glycanases |
| **Dissolved O₂** | **> 30% saturation** (sometimes 60%) | Most critical parameter |
| **C-Source** | Glucose / Sucrose (2–4%) | High C/N ratio favours EPS over biomass |
| **N-Source** | Yeast extract + (NH₄)₂SO₄ | Organic N preferred for high viscosity |
| **C:N Ratio** | **High (20:1 to 30:1)** | Drives carbon flux to EPS |
| **Inoculum** | 5–10% v/v, mid-log phase | Reduces lag phase |
| **Fermentation Time** | **48–96 h** | Two-phase: trophophase → idiophase |
| **Stirring** | Impeller tip speed **< 2.5 m/s** | High shear → fragmented, low-viscosity gum |
| **Working Volume** | 70–80% of vessel | Headspace for O₂ transfer |
| **Typical Yield (Xanthan)** | **0.7–1.0 g xanthan / g glucose** | Theoretical max ≈ 1.0 g/g |

### 2.1.3 Downstream Processing (Recovery) — 6-Stage Cascade

1. **Heat Treatment (Pasteurization):** 80–90 °C for 10–15 min → inactivates glycanases & cells.
2. **Cell Removal:** Centrifugation (≥ 10,000 × g) or microfiltration (0.2 µm).
3. **Concentration:** Vacuum evaporation or ultrafiltration (10 kDa MWCO).
4. **Precipitation:** Addition of **2–3 volumes of isopropanol/ethanol** (or KCl at 0.5–1%) — xanthan is **insoluble in alcohols**.
5. **Washing & Drying:** Alcohol wash → fluidized-bed / vacuum drying at 50–60 °C.
6. **Milling & Sieving:** Particle size 60–80 mesh for food-grade product.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "Xanthan is recovered by ethanol precipitation" — **correct**, but they forget the **pasteurization step BEFORE precipitation**. Without it, residual glycanases degrade the polymer → low viscosity product → **-2 marks lost in 14-mark questions**.

---

## 2.2 Nisin — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Structure & Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nisin belongs to Class I Lantibiotics** (lanthionine-containing antibiotic peptides). The class is defined by the post-translational modification converting serine/threonine into **lanthionine** and **methyl-lanthionine** via **LanB** (dehydratase) and **LanC** (cyclase).

**Nisin Variants — KTU Exam Hot Topic:**

| Variant | Discovery Year | Key Mutation | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nisin A** | 1944 (Rogers) | Wild-type | Original, food-grade |
| **Nisin Z** | 1989 (Mulders) | His→Asn at position 27 | More soluble at pH 6.0, **predominant variant in industry** |
| Nisin F | 2007 | — | Clinical (anti-mastitis) research |
| Nisin Q | 2002 | — | Isolated from *L. lactis* 61-14 |

### 2.2.2 Mode of Action — The **Dual Mechanism**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nisin is a smart antibacterial:**

1. **Step 1 — Lipid II Binding (Specificity):** Nisin binds the pyrophosphate-sugar moiety of **Lipid II** (bacterial cell wall precursor) via its **N-terminal rings (A, B)**. *This is what gives nisin its Gram-positive specificity.*

2. **Step 2 — Pore Formation (Lysis):** The **C-terminal rings (C, D, E)** then insert into the cytoplasmic membrane → form a **transmembrane pore** of 0.5–1.0 nm diameter.

3. **Step 3 — Efflux of Ions & ATP:** K⁺, Mg²⁺, and ATP leak out → dissipation of membrane potential (Δψ) and pH gradient (ΔpH) → **bacterial cell death**.

4. **Step 4 — Spore Outgrowth Inhibition:** At sub-MIC levels, nisin prevents **germination of *Bacillus* and *Clostridium* spores** by binding to sulfhydryl groups on spore membranes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Killer Fact (KTU Board Exam 2023):** Nisin is **NOT active against Gram-negative bacteria** because the outer membrane (LPS layer) blocks access to Lipid II. **Exception:** When combined with **EDTA** (chelates Mg²⁺ → disrupts LPS), nisin becomes effective against *E. coli* and *Salmonella*.

### 2.2.3 Biosynthesis Pathway

The **nisin gene cluster** (~14 kb) on the nisin-sucrose conjugative transposon contains **11 genes:**

| Gene | Function |
|---|---|
| **nisA** | Structural gene (pre-pro-nisin) |
| **nisB** | Dehydratase (Ser/Thr → Dha/Dhb) |
| **nisC** | Cyclase (Lan/Mes formation) |
| **nisP** | Leader peptide cleavage (extracellular) |
| **nisR, nisK** | Two-component regulation (auto-induction) |
| **nisI, nisF, nisE, nisG** | Immunity (self-protection) |

**Auto-Induction (Quorum Sensing):**
- External nisin (≥ 10⁻¹¹ M) → **NisK** (sensor kinase) phosphorylates **NisR** (response regulator).
- **NisR-P** activates promoters **PnisA & PnisF** → strong expression of structural + biosynthetic genes.
- This is the **basis of NICE (Nisin-Controlled gene Expression) system** used widely in biotech.

### 2.2.4 Fermentation Process Engineering

| Parameter | Optimal Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Fermentation Type** | Submerged, **Micro-aerobic / Anaerobic** | Lactic acid bacterium — O₂ can inhibit growth |
| **Temperature** | **30 °C** | Optimum for *L. lactis* growth & nisin production |
| **pH** | **6.0–6.5** (controlled via NaOH) | Lactic acid lowers pH → must neutralize |
| **C-Source** | Glucose / Lactose (1–2%) | Represses nisin at high glucose (>2%) — *glucose effect* |
| **N-Source** | Yeast extract, peptone, casein hydrolysate | Complex N → highest nisin titers |
| **Buffer** | **β-Glycerophosphate (0.2 M)** | Maintains pH without inhibiting cells |
| **Inoculum** | 2–5% v/v, **mid-log phase** | Synchronizes quorum-sensing auto-induction |
| **Fermentation Time** | **12–18 h** (much faster than xanthan!) | High nisin demand → must extract fast |
| **Typical Yield (Nisin)** | **40–100 mg/L** (wild-type) | Up to 3,000 mg/L in engineered strains |
| **Specific Activity** | **40 IU/µg** (Nisin A) | Measured by agar diffusion bioassay |

### 2.2.5 Downstream Processing (Recovery) — 4-Stage Cascade

1. **pH Adjustment & Cell Removal:** Lower broth to pH 3.5 with H₃PO₄ → centrifuge / microfilter to remove cells.
2. **Concentration:** **Ultrafiltration (1 kDa MWCO)** or nanofiltration.
3. **Purification:** Cation-exchange chromatography (pH < pI of 8.86 → nisin is +vely charged) **OR** solvent precipitation (cold acetone/ethanol at 4 °C).
4. **Drying & Formulation:** **Spray-drying** (preferred) or freeze-drying (lyophilization) with carriers (non-fat milk solids, NaCl) for commercial "Nisaplin" 2.5% w/w formulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Nisin is **highly cationic** at acidic pH (because pI = 8.86). Forgetting the pH-charge relationship means students incorrectly suggest anion exchange. **Use cation exchange at pH 3–4.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table for the KTU 2024 ESE — these are the "must-know" numerical relationships.**

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Productivity** | $P = \dfrac{C_{final} - C_{initial}}{t_{f}}$ | g/L·h | Used for both xanthan & nisin |
| **Yield (Substrate → Product)** | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S}$ | g/g | For xanthan: 0.7–1.0 g/g glucose |
| **Yield (Biomass)** | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | g/g | For *X. campestris*: ~0.4 g/g |
| **Specific Growth Rate** | $\mu = \dfrac{\ln(X_{2}/X_{1})}{t_{2}-t_{1}}$ | h⁻¹ | For *L. lactis*: ~0.6 h⁻¹ |
| **Doubling Time** | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | h | For *L. lactis*: ~1.15 h |
| **Krebs (Specific Productivity)** | $q_{P} = \dfrac{1}{X} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | g P / g X·h | Nisin: ~10 mg/g·h |
| **Volumetric OTR** | $OTR = k_{L}a \cdot (C^{*} - C_{L})$ | mmol O₂/L·h | Critical for xanthan |
| **Power Number (Rushton)** | $P_{o} = N_{p} \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ | W | For impeller design |
| **Thixotropic Index** | $TI = \dfrac{\eta_{low\,shear}}{\eta_{high\,shear}}$ | — | Xanthan solutions: TI > 1 |
| **Nisin Activity** | $1 \, IU = 1 \, \mu g \, pure\, nisin$ | IU | Pure nisin = 40 IU/µg |
| **Mass Balance (Overall)** | $V \cdot \dfrac{dC_{P}}{dt} = q_{P} \cdot X \cdot V$ | g/h | Fed-batch modelling |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Xanthan Gum — Worked Numerical Derivations

### 3.1.1 Problem: Calculating Xanthan Productivity

**Given:**
- A stirred-tank fermenter working volume: $V = 50{,}000$ L
- Final xanthan concentration: $C_P = 22$ g/L
- Initial xanthan: negligible ($C_0 \approx 0$)
- Fermentation time: $t_f = 72$ h
- Substrate (glucose) consumed: $\Delta S = 28$ g/L
- Final biomass: $X_f = 6.5$ g/L

**Find:** (i) Product yield $Y_{P/S}$, (ii) Volumetric productivity $P_v$, (iii) Overall product mass.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Product Yield $Y_{P/S}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{P/S} &= \frac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S} \\
&= \frac{C_P - C_0}{S_0 - S_f} \\
&= \frac{22 - 0}{28} \\
\therefore Y_{P/S} &= 0.786 \, \text{g xanthan / g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is **78.6% of the theoretical maximum** (~1.0 g/g) — a *good industrial yield*.

**Step 2 — Volumetric Productivity $P_v$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= \frac{C_P - C_0}{t_f} \\
&= \frac{22 - 0}{72} \\
&= 0.306 \, \text{g/L·h} \\
\therefore P_v &\approx 0.31 \, \text{g/L·h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Product Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_P &= C_P \times V \\
&= 22 \, \text{g/L} \times 50{,}000 \, \text{L} \\
\therefore M_P &= 1{,}100{,}000 \, \text{g} = 1100 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Check:** 1100 kg xanthan in 72 h from one 50,000 L fermenter is *consistent* with a mid-scale commercial batch.

---

### 3.1.2 Problem: OTR for Xanthan Fermentation

**Given:**
- $k_L a = 220 \, \text{h}^{-1}$ (volumetric mass transfer coefficient)
- $C^* = 8.0 \, \text{mg/L}$ (saturation DO at 30 °C, 1 atm)
- $C_L = 2.5 \, \text{mg/L}$ (operating dissolved oxygen)

**Find:** Oxygen transfer rate OTR.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OTR &= k_L a \cdot (C^{*} - C_L) \\
&= 220 \, \text{h}^{-1} \times (8.0 - 2.5) \, \text{mg/L} \\
&= 220 \times 5.5 \\
\therefore OTR &= 1210 \, \text{mg/L·h} \\
&\approx 37.8 \, \text{mmol O}_2\text{/L·h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** *X. campestris* has an OUR (oxygen uptake rate) of **30–40 mmol/L·h** during the idiophase. The OTR (37.8 mmol/L·h) matches — so the system is **NOT oxygen-limited**. If OTR < OUR, viscosity drops & yield collapses.

---

## 3.2 Nisin — Worked Numerical Derivations

### 3.2.1 Problem: Specific Growth Rate & Doubling Time

**Given:**
- *L. lactis* biomass at $t_1 = 2$ h: $X_1 = 0.30$ g/L
- Biomass at $t_2 = 5$ h: $X_2 = 1.65$ g/L
- Final nisin titre: $C_P = 65$ mg/L at $t_f = 12$ h

**Find:** (i) Specific growth rate $\mu$, (ii) Doubling time $t_d$, (iii) Specific productivity $q_P$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Specific Growth Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{\ln(X_2 / X_1)}{t_2 - t_1} \\
&= \frac{\ln(1.65 / 0.30)}{5 - 2} \\
&= \frac{\ln(5.5)}{3} \\
&= \frac{1.7047}{3} \\
\therefore \mu &= 0.568 \, \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Doubling Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_d &= \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} \\
&= \frac{0.6931}{0.568} \\
\therefore t_d &\approx 1.22 \, \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific Nisin Productivity (using average biomass $\bar{X}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X} &= \frac{X_1 + X_2}{2} = \frac{0.30 + 1.65}{2} = 0.975 \, \text{g/L} \\
q_P &= \frac{1}{\bar{X}} \cdot \frac{C_P - 0}{t_f} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.975} \cdot \frac{65 \, \text{mg/L}}{12 \, \text{h}} \\
&= \frac{5.417}{0.975} \\
\therefore q_P &= 5.56 \, \text{mg nisin / g biomass·h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Benchmark:** A well-optimized *L. lactis* fermentation should have $q_P$ in the range **5–15 mg/g·h**. Our result of 5.56 is on the lower end — suggesting room for optimization (e.g., reducing glucose repression).

---

### 3.2.2 Problem: Nisin Activity Unit Conversion

**Given:** A 1 L fermentation broth yields 80 mg of nisin. Nisin has a specific activity of $40 \, IU/\mu g$.

**Find:** Total activity in **International Units (IU)** of the broth.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total nisin} &= 80 \, \text{mg} = 80{,}000 \, \mu g \\
\text{Total activity} &= 80{,}000 \, \mu g \times 40 \, \frac{IU}{\mu g} \\
\therefore \text{Total activity} &= 3{,}200{,}000 \, IU = 3.2 \times 10^{6} \, IU
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reference:** "Nisaplin" commercial product contains **2.5% w/w nisin** = **$10^6$ IU/g**. So 1 L of our broth ≈ **3.2 g Nisaplin equivalent.**

---

## 3.4 Stoichiometric Model — Xanthan Production (Simplified)

**Overall biomass + xanthan formation from glucose:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{NH}_4^{+} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Xanthan (C}_{35}\text{H}_{49}\text{O}_{29}\text{)}_n + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Energy Yield:** Approximately **12 ATP equivalents** per xanthan repeat unit. The high energy cost explains why:
> - Carbon catabolite repression must be **avoided** (don't use fructose initially).
> - High C:N ratio **diverts** carbon to polymer, not biomass.
> - Aeration is **mandatory** — it fuels the TCA cycle supplying biosynthetic precursors.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Xanthan gum. Mention any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Xanthan gum is a high-molecular-weight **heteropolysaccharide** produced by the bacterium *Xanthomonas campestris* under aerobic submerged fermentation. It has a β-(1,4)-D-glucose backbone with trisaccharide side chains of mannose and glucuronic acid, often acetylated and pyruvylated.

> **Two Industrial Applications:**
> 1. **Food Industry (E415):** Used as a thickener, stabilizer and emulsifier in salad dressings, ice cream, bakery products and gluten-free foods.
> 2. **Oil & Gas Industry:** Used as a **viscosifier in drilling muds** to suspend rock cuttings and in **enhanced oil recovery (EOR)** for mobility control.

>  *(Other valid applications: cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, agricultural sprays, textile printing pastes.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a lantibiotic? Briefly explain how nisin acts against Gram-positive bacteria.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Lantibiotic:** A lantibiotic is a **Class I bacteriocin** containing the unusual thioether amino acids **lanthionine** and **β-methyl-lanthionine**, produced by Gram-positive bacteria through extensive **post-translational modification** of a precursor peptide.

> **Mechanism of Action of Nisin:**
> 1. **Binding Step:** The N-terminal rings (A and B) of nisin specifically bind to **Lipid II**, the membrane-bound peptidoglycan precursor of Gram-positive bacteria.
> 2. **Pore Formation:** The C-terminal rings (C, D, E) insert into the cytoplasmic membrane, forming a **transmembrane pore of ~0.5–1.0 nm diameter**.
> 3. **Cell Death:** The pore causes efflux of **K⁺, Mg²⁺ and ATP**, leading to collapse of the membrane potential (Δψ) and pH gradient (ΔpH), resulting in cell death.
> 4. **Spore Inhibition:** Nisin also prevents **germination of bacterial spores** (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacillus*) by interacting with sulfhydryl groups on the spore membrane.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule: Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Internal choice is mandatory.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Xanthan Gum Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the **production of Xanthan gum** by *Xanthomonas campestris* in detail, covering the **organism, raw materials, fermentation conditions and downstream processing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **biosynthesis pathway** of xanthan gum and explain why **dissolved oxygen** is considered the most critical process parameter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Production of Xanthan Gum — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

| Step | Detail | Marks |
|---|---|---:|
| **1. Organism** | *Xanthomonas campestris* pv. campestris — Gram-negative, aerobic, rod-shaped bacterium; produces yellow mucoid colonies; non-pathogenic strain selected for industry (e.g., NRRL B-1459). | 1 |
| **2. Carbon Source** | D-Glucose, sucrose, corn syrup, starch hydrolysate; concentration 2–4%. | 0.5 |
| **3. Nitrogen Source** | Yeast extract, peptone, ammonium sulphate. | 0.5 |
| **4. Inoculum Preparation** | Sequential seed train: slant → flask → lab fermenter → production fermenter; 5–10% inoculum. | 0.5 |
| **5. Fermentation Conditions** | Temperature 28–30 °C, pH 6.8–7.2 (controlled with NaOH/H₃PO₄), aeration 0.5–1.0 vvm, DOT > 30%, impeller tip speed < 2.5 m/s, fermentation 48–96 h. | 1.5 |
| **6. Pasteurization** | Heat broth to 80–90 °C for 10–15 min — inactivates glycanases & kills cells. | 0.5 |
| **7. Cell Removal** | Centrifugation (10,000 × g) or microfiltration (0.2 µm). | 0.5 |
| **8. Concentration** | Vacuum evaporation or ultrafiltration (10 kDa MWCO). | 0.5 |
| **9. Precipitation** | Add 2–3 volumes of isopropanol (or ethanol); xanthan is insoluble in alcohols. | 0.5 |
| **10. Drying & Milling** | Fluidized bed drying at 50–60 °C → grinding → 60–80 mesh sieve. | 0.5 |
| **11. Final QC** | Viscosity, purity, heavy metal tests, microbial limits. | 0.5 |

**(b) Biosynthesis Pathway & Critical Role of Dissolved Oxygen (7 marks):**

**Biosynthesis Pathway (4 marks):**

1. **Glucose enters** the cell and is catabolized via the **Entner–Doudoroff (ED) pathway** (not the EMP pathway), yielding pyruvate + glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, which feed into the TCA cycle.

2. **Nucleotide-sugar precursors are synthesized:**
   - UDP-glucose, UDP-galactose
   - GDP-mannose
   - dTDP-rhamnose
   - UDP-glucuronic acid

3. **Modifications:** Acetyl-CoA acetylates mannose (→ O-acetyl mannose); PEP ketolates mannose (→ pyruvyl mannose) via **acetyltransferase** and **pyruvyltransferase** enzymes.

4. **Polymerization & Export:** The pentasaccharide repeat unit is assembled on an **undecaprenyl-phosphate lipid carrier** (similar to LPS/O-antigen pathway) by sequential **glycosyltransferases** (GumD, GumE, GumH, GumI, GumJ, GumK, GumL, GumM). The polymer is simultaneously exported.

5. **Final Structure (3-mers → pentamer side chains):**
   $$\text{Inner: } \beta(1,4)\text{-D-Glc backbone} \;\;\;\; \text{Outer: } \beta\text{-D-Man-(Ac)}-(1,3)\text{-}\beta\text{-D-GlcA-(}1,2\text{)-}\alpha\text{-D-Man-(Pyr)}$$

**Why Dissolved O₂ is the Most Critical Parameter (3 marks):**

- *X. campestris* has a **high oxygen demand** due to:
  1. The **ED pathway** (replaces EMP) is energetically less efficient — it yields only **1 ATP per glucose** instead of 2.
  2. The TCA cycle runs at **maximum flux** to provide precursors (acetyl-CoA, PEP, UDP-sugars) → consumes large O₂.
  3. Biomass yield is low → most carbon is diverted to polysaccharide, requiring oxidative phosphorylation for ATP.
- **Oxygen limitation** → glucose catabolism shifts to by-products (e.g., pyruvate overflow) → xanthan yield drops by 40–60%.
- **Consequence of poor DOT:** Cell lysis, accumulation of reducing sugars, loss of pyruvyl groups, lower viscosity.
- **Solution:** Maintain DOT > 30% saturation via high aeration (0.5–1.0 vvm), Rushton turbines, and **oxygen-enriched air** when needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Loss Points):**
> 1. ✗ Writing "EMP/glycolysis" instead of **Entner–Doudoroff** pathway → **−2 marks**
> 2. ✗ Forgetting the **modification step** (acetylation + pyruvylation) → **−1.5 marks**
> 3. ✗ Missing the role of **undecaprenyl-P lipid carrier** in polymerization → **−1 mark**
> 4. ✗ Omitting quantitative DOT threshold (> 30%) → **−1 mark**
> 5. ✗ Not explaining **shear sensitivity** of fermentation → **−1 mark**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Nisin Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **production of nisin** by *Lactococcus lactis* in detail, including **organism characteristics, fermentation conditions and downstream processing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **structure, classification, and mode of action of nisin**. Why is nisin effective only against Gram-positive bacteria? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Production of Nisin — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

| Step | Detail | Marks |
|---|---|---:|
| **1. Organism** | *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* — Gram-positive, facultatively anaerobic, coccus. Industrial strain: ATCC 11454 (Nisin A producer), or L. lactis IO-1 (Nisin Z). | 1 |
| **2. Media Carbon Source** | Glucose, lactose or sucrose (1–2%) — kept **low to avoid catabolite repression**. | 0.5 |
| **3. Nitrogen Source** | Yeast extract, casein hydrolysate, peptone — complex nitrogen essential for high titres. | 0.5 |
| **4. Buffer System** | **β-Glycerophosphate (0.2 M)** is used to maintain pH without inhibiting *L. lactis* (Na₂HPO₄ buffers inhibit growth). | 0.5 |
| **5. Inoculum** | Mid-log phase, 2–5% v/v. | 0.5 |
| **6. Fermentation** | Submerged batch/fed-batch, 30 °C, pH 6.0–6.5 (auto-controlled with 5N NaOH to neutralize lactic acid), microaerobic, 12–18 h. | 1.5 |
| **7. Auto-Induction** | Initial nisin produced → binds **NisK sensor kinase** → **NisR** activation → strong induction of nisA, nisB, nisC genes (quorum sensing). | 0.5 |
| **8. pH Adjustment** | Lower broth to pH 3.5 with H₃PO₄ → stabilizes nisin (active at low pH) and precipitates cell debris. | 0.5 |
| **9. Cell Removal** | Centrifugation or microfiltration (0.2 µm). | 0.5 |
| **10. Concentration** | Ultrafiltration (1 kDa MWCO) or nanofiltration. | 0.5 |
| **11. Purification** | **Cation-exchange chromatography** at pH 3.5 (nisin is positively charged since pI = 8.86). | 0.5 |
| **12. Formulation & Drying** | Spray-dried with non-fat milk solids or NaCl carrier → "Nisaplin" 2.5% w/w. | 0.5 |

**(b) Structure, Classification & Mechanism of Nisin (7 marks):**

**Classification (2 marks):**
- Nisin is a **Class I lantibiotic** (lanthipeptide bacteriocin).
- Contains 34 amino acid residues; molecular weight ≈ 3354 Da.
- Contains unusual residues: **lanthionine**, **β-methyl-lanthionine**, **dehydroalanine (Dha)**, and **dehydrobutyrine (Dhb)**.
- The peptide contains **5 ring structures (A, B, C, D, E)** formed by thioether crosslinks.

**Structure (2 marks):**
- N-terminal rings (A, B) → **Lipid II binding domain** (specificity)
- C-terminal rings (C, D, E) → **Membrane-penetrating domain** (pore formation)
- Variants: **Nisin A** (wild-type, His-27) and **Nisin Z** (Asn-27, more soluble at pH 6.0).

**Mode of Action (2 marks):**
1. **Step 1:** Nisin N-terminal binds to **Lipid II** (undecaprenyl-PP-MurNAc-pentapeptide-GlcNAc), the membrane-bound peptidoglycan precursor.
2. **Step 2:** C-terminal inserts into the cytoplasmic membrane → forms a **stable 8-helix transmembrane pore** of diameter 0.5–1.0 nm.
3. **Step 3:** Ions (K⁺, Mg²⁺), ATP and amino acids leak out → collapse of proton motive force (PMF) → cell death.
4. **Step 4:** At sub-MIC concentrations, nisin inhibits **spore outgrowth** by binding sulfhydryl groups on inner spore membrane.

**Why Gram-Positive Specific (1 mark):**
- Gram-negative bacteria possess an **outer membrane (LPS layer)** that physically blocks nisin from accessing Lipid II in the inner membrane.
- **Exception:** Combining nisin with **EDTA** (a divalent cation chelator) destabilizes the LPS layer → nisin can reach the inner membrane → becomes effective against *E. coli*, *Salmonella* spp.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Loss Points):**
> 1. ✗ Confusing lantibiotic with **Class II bacteriocin** (pediocin, etc.) → **−1.5 marks**
> 2. ✗ Writing "Nisin binds to DNA/RNA" instead of Lipid II → **−3 marks** (a killer mistake)
> 3. ✗ Forgetting the **dual mechanism** (specific binding + non-specific pore) → **−2 marks**
> 4. ✗ Not mentioning **spore outgrowth inhibition** (often a 2-mark sub-part) → **−2 marks**
> 5. ✗ Saying "EDTA makes nisin bactericidal" without explaining LPS disruption → **−1 mark**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Last-Minute KTU 2024 ESE Revision — Pin This List**

### 🔑 Xanthan Gum — Must-Know Facts
- ✅ Producing organism: ***Xanthomonas campestris*** (Gram-negative, aerobic)
- ✅ Biosynthesis: **Entner–Doudoroff pathway** (NOT EMP)
- ✅ Five nucleotide-sugar precursors: UDP-Glc, UDP-Gal, GDP-Man, dTDP-Rha, UDP-GlcA
- ✅ Repeat unit: **Pentasaccharide** with β(1→4)-glucan backbone + trisaccharide side chain
- ✅ Critical parameter: **Dissolved O₂ > 30%** saturation
- ✅ Fermentation time: **48–96 h** (long!)
- ✅ pH: **6.8–7.2** (controlled)
- ✅ Recovery: Heat → Centrifuge → UF → **Isopropanol precipitation** → Drying → Milling
- ✅ E415; **GRAS** since 1969
- ✅ Applications: Food thickener, **oil drilling mud**, cosmetics, gluten-free baking
- ✅ Highest viscosity at **low shear** (pseudoplastic, thixotropic)

### 🔑 Nisin — Must-Know Facts
- ✅ Producing organism: ***Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis*** (Gram-positive, facultative anaerobe)
- ✅ Class: **Lantibiotic (Class I bacteriocin)** — contains **lanthionine**
- ✅ Composition: **34 amino acids**, M.W. ≈ 3354 Da
- ✅ Variants: **Nisin A** (His-27) and **Nisin Z** (Asn-27)
- ✅ pI: **8.86** (cationic at pH < pI → use cation exchange)
- ✅ Mode of action: **Binds Lipid II** → forms **transmembrane pore** → PMF collapse → death
- ✅ Also inhibits **spore outgrowth** (active against *Clostridium botulinum*!)
- ✅ **NOT effective against Gram-negative** bacteria (outer membrane barrier)
- ✅ EDTA + nisin → effective against Gram-negative
- ✅ Fermentation time: **12–18 h** (fast!)
- ✅ Auto-induction via **NisK/NisR** two-component system (quorum sensing)
- ✅ Commercial product: **"Nisaplin" 2.5% w/w**
- ✅ E234; **GRAS** since 1988
- ✅ Activity: **1 IU = 1 µg pure nisin**; commercial nisin = 40 IU/µg

### 🔑 High-Yield Comparison Points (For 14-mark questions)
- ✅ Xanthan: **Aerobic / Gram-negative / Long fermentation / Alcohol precipitation**
- ✅ Nisin: **Microaerobic / Gram-positive / Short fermentation / Chromatography purification**
- ✅ Both are **extracellular products** → simplified DSP
- ✅ Both are **GRAS** and **E-numbered** for food use
- ✅ Both have **strict process control** requirements: O₂ for xanthan, pH for nisin

### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Traps
- ❗ Xanthan uses **ED pathway**, not EMP — write this correctly.
- ❗ Xanthan must be **pasteurized before precipitation** (preserve viscosity).
- ❗ Nisin mechanism is **Lipid II binding** — never write "DNA/RNA binding."
- ❗ Nisin pI is **8.86** — at low pH (acidic), it is **positively charged** → use **cation exchange**.
- ❗ *L. lactis* needs **β-glycerophosphate** buffer (not phosphate buffer — it inhibits growth).

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — PRODUCTION OF XANTHAN GUM AND NISIN**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — NEP 2020 Aligned OBE Framework*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biofertilizer (Rhizobium, Azotobacter) and Biopesticide (Bacillus thuringiensis)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biofertilizer (Rhizobium, Azotobacter) and Biopesticide (Bacillus thuringiensis)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biofertilizers — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBBBT304, Module 4):**
> *Biofertilizers are biologically active formulations consisting of living or latent cells of efficient strains of nitrogen-fixing, phosphate-solubilising, or cellulolytic microorganisms that, when applied to seed, soil, or composting pits, multiply and participate in nutrient cycling, thereby enhancing soil fertility and crop productivity.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a biofertilizer is **not** a fertilizer in the chemical sense (N, P, K granules). It is a **living microbial inoculant** that performs the *function* of a fertilizer by making nutrients bioavailable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (BIS, India):** A commercial biofertilizer carrier product must guarantee a minimum population of **10^7 to 10^8 CFU/g** (Colony Forming Units per gram) at the time of manufacture, and **10^6 CFU/g** at the expiry date, as per the **FCO (Fertilizer Control Order), 1985** standards.

## 1.2 Rhizobium — The Root Nodule Symbiont

**Definition:**
*Rhizobium* is a genus of Gram-negative, rod-shaped, motile, aerobic soil bacteria belonging to the family *Rhizobiaceae* (α-proteobacteria). It establishes a **symbiotic (mutualistic) association** with the root nodules of leguminous plants, fixing atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) in exchange for photosynthetically-derived carbohydrates.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Underground Food Court":**
Imagine a vegetable seller (the *Rhizobium* bacterium) who cannot cook his own food. A restaurant owner (the *legume plant*) has a huge kitchen (the leaves doing photosynthesis) and produces abundant sugars but cannot buy spices (fixed nitrogen). The seller sets up a small counter *inside* the restaurant's basement (root nodule), cooks the spice (fixes N₂) using a free energy source (sugars from the plant), and **both parties share profits** — the plant gets nitrogen, the bacterium gets carbon. This is the **symbiotic bargain** of *Rhizobium*-legume interaction.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "**R**hizobium = **R**estaurant symbiosis in roots of **R**ajma/Chana (legumes)."

## 1.3 Azotobacter — The Free-Living Nitrogen Fixer

**Definition:**
*Azotobacter* is a genus of free-living, obligately aerobic, Gram-negative, oval/coccoid soil bacteria belonging to family *Pseudomonadaceae* (γ-proteobacteria). Unlike *Rhizobium*, it does **not** require a plant host. It fixes atmospheric N₂ in the rhizosphere and surrounding bulk soil **independently**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Independent Freelancer":**
Where *Rhizobium* is a "company employee" tied to a host factory, *Azotobacter* is a **freelance consultant** who works from a co-working space (free soil), uses public Wi-Fi (soil organic carbon), and provides nitrogen services to any neighbouring "client" plant root. Famous species: *Azotobacter chroococcum* (most common in Indian soils), *A. vinelandii*, *A. beijerinckii*, *A. agilis*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** *Rhizobium* = **symbiotic, legume-specific**. *Azotobacter* = **free-living (asymbiotic / non-symbiotic), non-specific, aerobic.**

## 1.4 Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) — The Biopesticide

**Definition:**
*Bacillus thuringiensis* (commonly abbreviated **Bt**) is a Gram-positive, spore-forming, facultative anaerobic bacterium of the family *Bacillaceae*. During sporulation, it produces **parasporal crystalline inclusions** composed of **Cry (crystal) proteins** and **Cyt (cytolytic) proteins**, which are highly specific insecticidal toxins active against lepidopteran, dipteran, and coleopteran insect larvae.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Smart Bomb":**
Think of Bt as a *guided missile* manufacturer. When insect larvae ingest Bt spores along with the crystal protein (the "warhead"), the alkaline pH of the insect midgut (pH **10–11**) dissolves the crystal. Specific gut proteases activate the protoxin into an active toxin that punches holes in the gut epithelium. The insect stops feeding and dies of septicaemia. Crucially, the toxin is **selective** — mammals (with acidic stomach pH ~2) are unaffected, making Bt a **green pesticide**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Bt is the **single most commercially successful biopesticide worldwide**, accounting for >**90%** of the global bioinsecticide market. It is used as a spray (e.g., *DiPel*, *Biobit*) and as transgene in Bt-cotton, Bt-brinjal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rhizobium — Mode of Action and Symbiotic Process

The *Rhizobium*-legume symbiosis proceeds through a highly orchestrated molecular dialogue:

1. **Pre-infection signal exchange:** Legume roots exude **flavonoids** (e.g., luteolin, genistein) into the rhizosphere. *Rhizobium* senses these via the *NodD* regulator protein.
2. **Nod factor synthesis:** Activated *NodD* switches on the *nod* genes (*nodABC*), producing **Nod factors** (lipochitooligosaccharides, LCOs).
3. **Root hair curling:** Nod factors trigger root hair curling, forming an "infection thread."
4. **Cortical cell division:** Nod factors stimulate cortical cell division → **nodule organogenesis**.
5. **Bacteroid formation:** Bacteria are released into nodule cells, differentiate into **bacteroids**, and begin **nitrogenase** synthesis.
6. **N₂ fixation:** Atmospheric N₂ is reduced to NH₃ in bacteroids using the **nitrogenase enzyme complex** with high ATP demand (16 ATP per N₂ reduced).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Leghaemoglobin?** The enzyme nitrogenase is **oxygen-sensitive (O₂ inhibits irreversibly)**. Leghaemoglobin (a plant-derived oxygen carrier, analogous to haemoglobin) buffers O₂ to nanomolar levels inside the nodule, allowing respiration (high ATP) but protecting nitrogenase.

## 2.2 Azotobacter — Mode of Action and Free-Living N₂ Fixation

*Azotobacter* fixes N₂ as a free-living aerobe. To overcome the O₂ sensitivity of nitrogenase, it employs **two protective strategies**:

1. **High respiratory rate:** *Azotobacter* maintains an exceptionally high O₂ consumption rate (Q_O₂), keeping intracellular O₂ near zero.
2. **Cyanide-resistant alternative oxidase** and **conformational protection** of nitrogenase.

Additionally, *Azotobacter* produces:
- **Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA):** Plant growth hormone (auxin).
- **Gibberellins & Cytokinins:** Stimulate root and shoot growth.
- **Siderophores:** Iron-chelators that suppress soil pathogens.
- **Ammonia:** Direct N supply to non-legume crops (cereals, vegetables, cotton).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Alert:** *Azotobacter* fixes only **5–20 mg N/g of carbon source consumed**, much less than *Rhizobium* (100–200 mg N/g C), but its value lies in *non-specificity* and *phytohormone co-production*.

## 2.3 Bacillus thuringiensis — Mode of Action

The insecticidal activity of Bt follows a precise **four-step mechanism**:

1. **Ingestion:** Larvae feed on Bt-sprayed foliage/spores.
2. **Solubilisation:** In the **alkaline midgut (pH 10–11)**, the crystal dissolves, releasing protoxin (130 kDa).
3. **Activation:** Midgut proteases (trypsin/chymotrypsin) cleave the protoxin into the active **65 kDa toxin core**.
4. **Pore formation:** The active toxin binds to specific receptors (e.g., **Cadherin, APN, ALP**) on the midgut epithelial membrane, oligomerises, and forms **cation-selective pores**, causing osmotic lysis, cell death, and larval mortality.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Phenomenon | Equation / Relation | Constants & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nitrogenase stoichiometry | $N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \longrightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$ | 16 ATP / N₂; 1 mol N₂ = 28 g | Biofermenter O₂/ATP demand design |
| Symbiotic N₂ fixation rate (Rhizobium) | $R_N = \dfrac{m \cdot N_{cells}}{t}$ | mg N fixed / g C source / day | Inoculant potency assessment |
| BOD of fermentation broth | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - B}{P}$ | mg/L; D = DO, B = seed, P = dilution | Effluent treatment in biofertilizer plants |
| Cry protein dose-response | $LC_{50} = \dfrac{a \cdot C^n}{1 + b \cdot C^n}$ (Hill eqn.) | µg toxin / mL diet; n = Hill coeff. | Biopesticide formulation strength |
| CFU count | $CFU/mL = \dfrac{\text{colonies} \times \text{dilution factor}}{\text{volume plated (mL)}}$ | colonies/mL | Quality control of inoculants |
| Biopesticide spore viability | $V = \dfrac{N_{final}}{N_{initial}} \times 100\%$ | %; shelf-life assessment | FCO / BIS specification compliance |
| Ammonification (urea hydrolysis) | $(NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{urease}} 2NH_3 + CO_2$ | — | Soil nitrogen release model |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** The nitrogenase reaction is written with **8 electrons + 8 H⁺**, NOT 6. Forgetting the **H₂ evolution step** (1 mol H₂ per N₂ fixed) costs marks.

## 2.5 Engineering and Agricultural Utility

- **Rhizobium** → Used in pulse crop cultivation (chickpea, pigeon pea, soybean). Reduces urea fertilizer input by 50–70 kg N/ha.
- **Azotobacter** → Used for non-legumes (rice, maize, cotton, sugarcane); also useful in reclamation of alkaline soils.
- **Bt** → Used as foliar spray in vegetables, cotton; transgenes in Bt-cotton (Bollgard II), Bt-brinjal; larvicidal against *Aedes* mosquito vectors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometry of the Nitrogenase Reaction

We derive the half-reactions and combine them to obtain the overall nitrogenase stoichiometry, which is the **single most important equation for any biofertilizer question**.

**Step 1 — Substrate reduction (N₂ → 2 NH₃):**

Nitrogen undergoes a **6-electron reduction** (N: oxidation state 0 → −3).

$$N_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^- \longrightarrow 2NH_3$$

**Step 2 — Obligatory H₂ evolution (proton reduction side-reaction):**

Nitrogenase always evolves at least **1 mol H₂ per mol N₂**, requiring 2 electrons.

$$2H^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2$$

**Step 3 — Summing the two half-reactions:**

Adding (Step 1) and (Step 2) gives the complete electron count.

$$N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- \longrightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2$$

**Step 4 — Coupling with ATP hydrolysis:**

Each electron transfer through nitrogenase consumes **2 ATP**; hence 8 e⁻ × 2 = **16 ATP** per N₂ reduced.

$$N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \longrightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$$

**Step 5 — Mass balance check:**

- **N:** 2 on each side ✓
- **H:** 8 (from H⁺) + 2×1 (in NH₃, but bound) — confirm with explicit count: LHS H = 8, RHS H = 6 (in 2NH₃) + 2 (in H₂) = 8 ✓
- **Charge:** LHS = 8(+1) + 8(−1) = 0; RHS = 0 ✓
- **Energy:** 16 ATP hydrolyzed (irreversible thermodynamic drive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic Reality:** The ΔG°′ of this reaction is **−15.2 kcal/mol** (i.e., spontaneous), but only because **ATP hydrolysis (ΔG°′ = −7.3 kcal/mol × 16 = −117 kcal/mol)** drives it. Without ATP, the reaction is thermodynamically unfavourable — this is why *Rhizobium* needs 20–30% of the plant's photosynthate.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Organisms

| Parameter | *Rhizobium* | *Azotobacter* | *Bacillus thuringiensis* |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gram reaction / morphology** | Gram-negative rods | Gram-negative oval/coccoid | Gram-positive rod, endospore-forming |
| **Lifestyle** | Symbiotic (legume nodule) | Free-living (soil) | Free-living (epiphytic, soil) |
| **Primary function** | N₂ fixation | N₂ fixation + phytohormones (IAA, GA) | Insecticidal Cry/Cyt protein |
| **Host/Target** | Specific legumes (chickpea, soybean) | Non-specific (cereals, vegetables) | Lepidopteran/dipteran/coleopteran larvae |
| **N₂ fixed (typical)** | 50–300 kg N/ha/yr | 5–20 mg N/g C | Not applicable (does not fix N₂) |
| **Toxin / Key enzyme** | Nitrogenase | Nitrogenase + IAA pathway | Cry1Aa, Cry1Ab, Cry1Ac, Cry2Ab, Cry3Aa, Cry4Ba |
| **O₂ sensitivity** | Protected by **leghaemoglobin** | Protected by high respiration rate | Not relevant for toxin |
| **Aerobic / Anaerobic** | Microaerophilic inside nodule | Obligate aerobe | Facultative anaerobe |
| **Carrier material (typical)** | Peat, lignite, charcoal | Peat, vermiculite, talc | Wettable powder, oil dispersion, granules |
| **Shelf life (formulated)** | 6–12 months | 6–12 months | 1–2 years (spore-stable) |
| **FCO standard CFU/g** | ≥ **10^7** | ≥ **10^7** | ≥ **10^8** (spore count) |
| **KTU typical exam weight** | High (7–14 marks) | Medium (3–7 marks) | High (7–14 marks) |
| **Key Indian commercial example** | *Nitrofix*, *Rhizocert* | *Azotobact*, *BioN* | *DiPel* (Bt kurstaki), *Biobit* |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — CFU Calculation

> **Problem (KTU style):** A biofertilizer broth is serially diluted 10⁻⁶. From this dilution, 0.1 mL is plated on yeast-extract mannitol agar. After 5 days incubation, **150 colonies** are counted. Calculate CFU/mL of the original broth.

**Step 1 — Identify the dilution factor used for plating:**

The plate corresponds to the **10⁻⁶** dilution.

**Step 2 — Apply the CFU formula:**

$$CFU/mL = \dfrac{\text{colonies} \times \text{reciprocal of dilution factor}}{\text{volume plated (mL)}}$$

$$CFU/mL = \dfrac{150 \times 10^6}{0.1} = 150 \times 10^7 = 1.5 \times 10^{9}\,CFU/mL$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation for FCO compliance:**

This broth exceeds the FCO minimum of **10⁷ CFU/g** by **two orders of magnitude**, indicating a high-potency broth suitable for direct carrier mixing.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Cry Protein Dose-Response (LC₅₀)

> **Problem:** A bioassay shows that at a Cry1Ac concentration of **0.4 µg/mL** diet, 50% of *Helicoverpa armigera* neonate larvae die in 7 days (Hill coefficient n = 1). Estimate the dose at which 90% mortality (LC₉₀) is observed.

**Step 1 — Use the Hill equation:**

For n = 1, the dose-response simplifies to the Michaelis-Menten form:

$$M = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot C}{K_d + C}$$

where **M = mortality fraction**, **K_d = LC₅₀**.

**Step 2 — Find K_d:**

Given: at C = 0.4 µg/mL, M = 0.50.

$$0.50 = \dfrac{0.4}{K_d + 0.4} \;\Rightarrow\; K_d + 0.4 = 0.8 \;\Rightarrow\; K_d = 0.4\,\mu g/mL$$

**Step 3 — Solve for LC₉₀ (M = 0.90):**

$$0.90 = \dfrac{C_{90}}{0.4 + C_{90}}$$

$$0.90 \times (0.4 + C_{90}) = C_{90}$$

$$0.36 + 0.90 \cdot C_{90} = C_{90}$$

$$0.36 = 0.10 \cdot C_{90}$$

$$C_{90} = 3.6\,\mu g/mL$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

A **9-fold** increase in toxin concentration (0.4 → 3.6 µg/mL) is required to push mortality from 50% to 90%. This has direct cost implications for Bt formulation; commercial Bt products are typically formulated at **LC₉₀ to LC₉₅** levels for field efficacy.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — BOD of Biofertilizer Plant Effluent

> **Problem:** A 5 mL sample of fermenter wastewater is diluted to 300 mL. Initial DO = 8.0 mg/L. After 5 days at 20°C, DO = 4.0 mg/L. Seed correction = 0.5 mg/L. Calculate BOD₅.

**Step 1 — Compute the dilution factor:**

$$P = \dfrac{300\,mL}{5\,mL} = 60$$

**Step 2 — Apply the BOD equation (no seed correction in standard form):**

$$BOD_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)}{P}$$

$$BOD_5 = \dfrac{8.0 - 4.0}{60} = 0.0667\,mg/L$$

**Step 3 — With seed correction (more accurate):**

$$BOD_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - B}{P} = \dfrac{4.0 - 0.5}{60} = 0.0583\,mg/L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** High BOD effluents from biofertilizer plants indicate residual sugars — the effluent must be treated in an ETP before CPCB discharge (<**100 mg/L BOD₅** for inland surface water as per Indian standards).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rhizobium–Legume Symbiosis — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Legume Root Exudates - Flavonoids e.g. Luteolin] --> B[Rhizobium NodD Sensor Protein Activated]
    B --> C[nod Gene Expression - nodABC, nodFE]

    H --> I[Leghaemoglobin Buffers O2 to nM Level]
    I --> J[N2 Reduction to NH3 - Nitrogenase Activity]
    J --> K[Ammonia Transported as Glutamine to Shoot]
    K --> L[Plant Growth Enhanced - Photosynthate to Bacteroid]
    L --> H
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soil Organic Carbon - Carbohydrates] --> B[Azotobacter - Aerobic Respiration]
    B --> C[High O2 Consumption Rate - QO2]

    D --> E[Nitrogenase Active - N2 to NH3]
    E --> F[NH3 Released to Rhizosphere]
    B --> G[IAA Biosynthesis via Tryptophan Pathway]
    B --> H[Siderophore Production - Iron Chelation]
    G --> I[Root and Shoot Growth Stimulation]

    I --> K
    J --> K
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bt Spore + Crystal Inclusion - Cry Protein Protoxin] --> B[Insect Larva Ingests Spore on Foliage]
    B --> C[Midgut pH 10-11 - Alkaline Solubilisation of Crystal]
    C --> D[Protease Activation - 130 kDa Protoxin to 65 kDa Active Toxin]
    D --> E[Binding to Specific Receptors - Cadherin APN ALP]
    E --> F[Oligomerisation of Toxin]
    F --> G[Insertion into Brush Border Membrane]
    G --> H[Cation Selective Pore Formation]
    H --> I[Osmotic Lysis of Midgut Epithelial Cells]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Starter Culture - Bt kurstaki HD-1] --> B[Seed Fermenter - 10 L Working Volume]
```
